source.title: Authorization & Audit Evidence Caseflow: Collection, Preservation, and Escalation
source.author: Marisol Vega
source.body:
Summary:
This runbook defines a single, auditable caseflow for capturing authorization decisions, runtime audit events, and data-residency constraints across incidents and compliance requests. It is intended for Security, SRE, Platform, and DevEx to coordinate how evidence is collected, stored, and escalated such that legal, privacy, and external audit requests can be satisfied within SLA.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: provide a prescriptive, end-to-end recipe for evidence capture when an access event, failed authorization, or data residency question intersects with an incident or compliance review.
- Scope: covers service-level RBAC signals, audit-log harvest points, preservation envelopes for cross-region incidents, retention mapping for legal holds, and a lightweight exception/escalation flow for timebound deviations.
- Exclusions: endpoint-level packet captures and deep host forensics (handled by IR team under separate forensic chain-of-custody procedures).

Key concepts and definitions:
- Evidence Envelope: a timeboxed bundle of artifacts (logs, entitlement snapshots, configuration, model artifacts) assembled for a single investigation or audit request.
- Preservation Point: the earliest durable record (S3 object, write-ahead log chunk, or audit store segment) that includes the event/time window under investigation.
- Authz Snapshot: deterministic dump of policy state and active entitlements for a tenant or principal at a given UTC timestamp.
- Residency Tag: canonical label on events/objects indicating the geographic residency requirement (e.g., eu:fractional, na:sovereign).

Roles and ownership:
- Security (owner): owns policy for evidence minimality, redaction rules, and escalation to legal.
- SRE / Observability (owner): owns audit log retention, immutable store configuration, and query interfaces.
- Platform / Infra: runs the preservation tooling (snapshot generator, ingest job) and enforces residency tags at write-time.
- Product / Customer Success: coordinates with customers for evidence requests that include customer data or PII.

High-level caseflow (stepwise):
1) Trigger detection: a failed or suspicious authorization event fires an incident marker (e.g., 'AUTHZ_FAILURE_HIGH_RISK') in the event bus.
2) Automated sampling: preservation agent evaluates risk -> if threshold met, it marks Preservation Point and begins streaming matching partitions to an immutable bucket.
3) Snapshot capture: Authz Snapshot generator records policy config, active roles/SCIM state, and service account bindings for the snapshot window (+/- 5 minutes).
4) Evidence envelope assembly: orchestrator collects audit log segment IDs, snapshot artifact references, and residency tags into the evidence envelope manifest.
5) Short-term hold: envelope is placed into a short-term preserved store (30 days) for triage. Legal hold can promote to long-term retention.
6) Triage and enrichment: Security analyst annotates envelope (tags, notes), attaches relevant traces and replay artifacts, and requests additional data if needed.
7) Escalation and disclosure: if customer data or cross-border issues appear, Security escalates to Legal and Privacy per the disclosure matrix.
8) Closure or retention: incident closed -> envelope either expires per retention policy or is archived under a legal hold.

Evidence collection matrix (described):
(Use when assembling an envelope - map artifact -> producer -> retention window -> preservation trigger)
- Authorization events: produced by auth-service (authz-proxy). Retention default: 365 days. Preservation trigger: failure, policy-change, or elevated-risk flag.
- Audit logs (API calls): produced by api-gateway, routed to audit-logs topic. Retention default: 540 days in hot store, immutable archive 7 years. Preservation trigger: any incident with customer-impact or legal request.
- Entitlement/SCIM snapshots: produced by snapshot-service on demand. Retention default: 2 years or per request. Preservation trigger: any incident involving user provisioning or privilege escalation.
- KV cache traces & KV keys: produced by serving-runtime; only included if authorized and scoped to the incident (redact model payloads). Retention default: 90 days.
- Deployment manifests & release tags: produced by ci-cd pipeline. Retention default: 3 years. Preservation trigger: rollout-related incidents.

Data residency and cross-border handling:
- Residency tags must be injected at write-time by the service emitting the event. The orchestrator uses tags to select a preservation target that satisfies residency constraints.
- For incidents spanning regions, the orchestration will create a ‘minimalized’ envelope with metadata and proofs-of-access that can be exported cross-border while keeping resident PII in-region if required.
- When a requester (internal or external) asks for evidence that crosses residency boundaries, the disclosure flow requires Privacy approval and a Residency Export Approval ticket in the case management system. Approval must reference the legal basis and retained in the envelope manifest.

Secure SDLC integration (how to bake evidence readiness into normal engineering flow):
- PR checklist additions (must be present for any auth-affecting change):
  1. Unit tests for policy fall-through and deny-by-default.
  2. Integration test that validates audit events are emitted for success/failure paths.
  3. Schema bump tests to ensure audit schema compatibility.
  4. Migration plan with snapshot rollback steps for policy changes.
- Release gate: any release touching auth, entitlement paths, or residency code must include a release-note section listing the expected audit log schema changes and the snapshot retention implications.

Forensics preservation commands and examples:
- Preserve a specific audit log segment (example):
  - aws s3 cp s3://audit-hot/segments/segment-2029-01-14-15-00.gz s3://evidence-preserve/envelopes/<ENVELOPE_ID>/ && aws s3api put-object-tagging --bucket evidence-preserve --key envelopes/<ENVELOPE_ID>/segment-2029-01-14-15-00.gz --tagging 'TagSet=[{Key=preserved,Value=true}]'
- Generate authz snapshot (example curl):
  - curl -X POST "https://internal.snapshot-service/api/v1/snapshot?scope=tenant:acme&ts=2029-01-14T15:00:00Z" -H "Authorization: Bearer $PRESERVE_TOKEN" -o authz-snapshot-acme-20290114.json

Redaction and minimality guidance:
- Minimize personally-identifying fields by default: if an evidence request includes PII outside residency, apply automated field-level redaction policies unless Legal instructs otherwise.
- Keep an internal mapping of reversible vs irreversible redaction. Only Security+Legal may approve reversible redaction (i.e., reversible with KMS keys).

Retention rules and escalation thresholds (summary table described):
- Short-term preserved envelopes: 30 days (auto-delete unless escalated).
- Standard preserved artifacts: 90–540 days depending on artifact class.
- Legal hold / eDiscovery: override to indefinite until explicit release.
- Escalation threshold: any envelope containing customer PII, export requests for data crossing geography, or evidence required by government demand -> escalate to Legal within 4 business hours.

Exception and risk-handling flow (when automated preservation cannot be applied):
1) Document the reason for preservation failure into the incident timeline.
2) Create a manual capture ticket assigned to Platform and Security with 24-hour SLA to collect best-effort artifacts.
3) If collection cannot meet residency constraints, invoke Residency Exception review with Privacy & Legal (approval recorded in envelope manifest).
4) If exception is granted, record compensating controls (sampled audits, revocation timeline).

Packaging evidence for audits and regulators:
- Envelope manifest must include: unique envelope ID, list of artifact URIs, checksum values (SHA256), residency flags, approver signatures (Security/Legal), and a redaction manifest if any fields were redacted.
- Preferred delivery formats: GPG-signed tarball for internal review; SFTP over customer-dedicated link or secure POA channel for regulated customers.

Operational tooling and dashboards (owner leads):
- Preservation monitor: shows active envelopes, retention expirations, and open escalation tickets.
- Query interface: prebuilt queries for common auditor requests (authz queries by principal, timeline reconstructions, cross-tenant artifact lists).
- Alerting: on failed preservation attempts, automatic 1-hour paging to Platform on-call.

Privacy and legal checklist before disclosure:
- Confirm jurisdictional basis for disclosure.
- Confirm minimal set of artifacts necessary.
- Ensure signatures for export/transfer (Residency Export Approval).
- Attach Legal approval and retention justification to envelope manifest.

Post-incident review and continuous improvement:
- After-action steps include: evidence completeness score (0–100), gap analysis, and a remediation plan with owners and due dates.
- Quarterly tabletop exercises should validate: snapshot correctness, residency-tag fidelity, and that the Preservation monitor can restore envelopes end-to-end.

Appendix A — Example envelope manifest (JSON snippet, redacted for doc):
{
  "envelope_id": "env-2029-00042",
  "created_at": "2029-01-14T15:12:03Z",
  "artifacts": ["s3://evidence-preserve/envelopes/env-2029-00042/segment-15.gz"],
  "residency": "eu",
  "approvals": ["security:marisol.vega", "legal:anne.kapoor"]
}

Appendix B — Common queries and examples:
- Find authz failures by principal: SELECT * FROM audit_logs WHERE event_type = 'AUTHZ_DENY' AND principal_id = '<PRINCIPAL>' AND ts BETWEEN '<START>' AND '<END>';
- Reconstruct role bindings at T: curl https://internal.snapshot-service/api/v1/snapshot?scope=tenant:acme&ts='<T>'

Contacts and escalation matrix:
- Security on-call: pager (primary) -> sec-oncall@redwood.ai (TL: +1 555-0199)
- Platform on-call: platform-oncall@redwood.ai
- Legal (privacy escalation): privacy-legal@redwood.ai

Change log:
- 2027-08-15: Initial draft and tabletop playtested.
- 2028-03-02: Added automated Preservation monitor and short-term hold semantics.
- 2029-01-22: Updated cross-border export approval flow and added example preservation commands.

Related artifacts and references:
- Internal: audit-logging schema (audit-events-v3), snapshot-service API spec, evidence-preserve S3 lifecycle config.
- External: Applicable data-protection laws (GDPR guidance, Schrems II notes), regulator request templates.

End of document.

source.title: Rapid Authorization Containment and Evidence Orchestration Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview:

Purpose: This playbook prescribes a fast, auditable, cross-functional process for containing authorization-related incidents (privilege escalation, token misuse, delegated-authorization failures), collecting compliance-grade evidence, and orchestrating multi-team handoffs while respecting data residency and retention constraints. It is intended for SREs, SecOps, on-call engineers, product security, and legal.

Scope: RBAC and auth incidents impacting production inference endpoints, control-plane configuration (roles, attribute stores, delegation), audit log integrity, or any event that may require regulatory evidence packaging (SOC2 / ISO / regional data residency). Excludes non-authorization incidents (use standard incident process).

Quick summary of goals:
- Contain active authorization exposure within 15 minutes for critical incidents.
- Secure and snapshot forensic evidence within 60 minutes of detection.
- Produce a packaged evidence bundle suitable for auditors and legal within 48 hours.

Ownership and roles:
1) Incident Lead (IL) — primary decision maker for containment and communications. Typical: SecOps on-call.
2) Forensic Engineer (FE) — executes log snapshots, chain-of-custody packaging, and ensures immutability. Typical: SRE/Platform engineer.
3) Auth Owner (AO) — owner of the affected auth system (RBAC, attribute service, token issuer).
4) Legal & Compliance Liaison (LCL) — evaluates cross-border evidence transfer and legal hold requirements.
5) Oncall Product Engineer (PE) — fixes product-side misconfigurations or rollbacks.

Escalation matrix (initial):
- P0: All-hands. IL pages Security Pager (SLA 15 min). Notify CTO/CDO.
- P1: SecOps & FE within 30 min. Notify Product and SRE leads.
- P2: Normal on-call review, follow standard SLA.

Detection and classification:
- Signals: entitlement-denied anomalies, sudden expansion of role bindings, long-lived token issuance spikes, delegation grant API errors, unusual authz decision latencies.
- Primary detectors: authz-decision-rate-monitor (alert if delta > 200% baseline), entitlement-delta-feed, token-issuer-threshold alarms, audit ingestion gaps.
- Triage categories: accidental misconfig (configuration drift), service compromise (exposed keys/service account), delegated-authorization exploit, audit-log tampering.

Immediate containment steps (IL / AO / FE) — target 15 minutes:
1) Isolate: if possible, toggle auth-policy read-only or switch to safe-mode routing (policy: global-fallback-deny). Example: 
   - CLI: redwoodctl auth policy set --mode read-only --scope production
2) Revoke tokens: invalidate suspicious tokens or rotate the token issuer signing key (if rotation safe given client constraints). Use short-lived revoke lists for federated tokens.
   - Example command: redwoodctl auth tokens revoke --selector "issuer:external,age<24h"
3) Rollback recent role bindings: list role-binding changes in last 30 minutes and revert approved commits.
   - Query example: SELECT * FROM entitlement_audit WHERE timestamp > now() - 30m AND action IN ('bind','grant')
4) Apply limiting firewall rules or rate limits for the affected service(s) to prevent broad exploitation.

Evidence collection checklist (FE) — target snapshot in 60 minutes:
1) Preserve audit streams: ensure no further ingestion overwrites the current partition. Mark current offsets and copy to immutable storage (S3 bucket: s3://redwood-forensics/immutable/YYYYMMDD/incident-id).
   - CLI example: forensicsctl snapshot-audit --stream authz-stream --to s3://redwood-forensics/immutable/2028-06-01/INC-12345 --note "initial snapshot"
2) Export entitlement change logs (RBAC bindings) with full context (actor, actor_metadata, change_request_id, before/after).
3) Capture system state: configs, running policy versions, signed kernel/agent versions, and relevant tracer IDs.
4) Collect ephemeral artifacts: process dumps only when authorized by LCL. Document chain-of-custody (who requested, who executed).
5) Record preservation metadata: timestamp (UTC), preserver identity, hashing algorithm (sha256), storage location, retention SLA label.

Chain-of-custody and evidence packaging:
- All collected artifacts must be SHA-256 hashed and stored in an immutable object store. The FE must record a manifest that includes: artifact name, SHA-256, timestamp, collector, and storage URI.
- Use evidence-bundler tool to produce a signed bundle: evidence-bundler assemble --manifest <manifest.json> --sign-with /keys/forensics-signing-key.pem --output INC-12345-bundle.tar.gz
- Attach all relevant audit partitions, entitlement CSVs, config diffs, and a narrative timeline (see Timeline section).

Data residency and cross-border considerations:
- Before exporting audit data across regions, consult LCL. By default, evidence stays in the originating residency region for 72 hours. Any transfer beyond that requires a documented justification and an encryption-at-rest policy using customer KMS where applicable.
- Retention mapping (example table):
  Resource Type | Default Retention | Evidence Access SLA | Residency constraint
  --------------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------
  Auth audit logs | 365 days | 60 min snapshot | stored in originating region unless approved
  Entitlement diffs | 540 days | 24 hrs | mirrored for DR but restricted for PII
  Token issuance records | 90 days | 60 min | redact PII before cross-border transfer

Secure SDLC tie-ins (pre-incident hardening):
- All role-binding changes must flow through Git-backed policy repos (policy-as-code) with signed commits and CI checks including smoke tests and entitlement simulation.
- Release gates: any auth policy change targeting production must have canary and automated policy-simulated-test pass rate >= 99.5%.
- Mandatory code-signing for any binary that can modify auth tables.

Investigative timeline and documentation (IL / FE):
1) Create incident timeline doc entry (confluence path: /incidents/INC-12345) with the following sections: detection, initial containment, evidence snapshots, changes made, communications, and next steps.
2) Document all commands run, files exported, and identities of actors. Use the template below for the narrative section.

Template: Narrative timeline entry
- Detected at (UTC): 2028-06-01T10:32:00Z
- Detected by: authz-decision-rate-monitor rule #53
- Initial severity: P0 -> escalated to P0 at 10:40Z
- Containment actions: CLI commands issued (list), tokens revoked, snapshot URIs
- Evidence bundle URI: s3://redwood-forensics/immutable/2028-06-01/INC-12345/INC-12345-bundle.tar.gz

Regulatory packaging and evidence handoff (LCL / Legal):
- Within 48 hours prepare an evidence report including: incident narrative, signed evidence bundle, statement of residency (where each artifact is stored), redaction notes for PII, and certification of chain-of-custody.
- For GDPR or regional data controls, include a data-flow diagram and export justification.
- Legal may request additional retention hold; FE should create a retention-hold tag on the evidence object.

Risk and exceptions governance:
- If containment actions (e.g., rotating root signing key) will materially impact customer availability, IL must open an exception request using the Authorization-Exception form and route to the Authorization Board. Include risk justification, rollback plan, and timeframe.
- All exceptions must be time-limited (max 7 days for emergency escalations without Board approval).

Post-incident actions and lessons learned:
- Within 7 days: IL to coordinate a 90-minute post-incident review (PIR) with SRE, Auth Owner, Product, and Legal. Publish PIR findings and action items into the incident record.
- Within 30 days: complete remediation tickets (policy-as-code fixes, CI test additions, telemetry improvements).
- Update policy-as-code repos and create regression tests in eval-harness to detect similar failures.

Operational tooling and recipes (appendix):
- Useful CLI snippets:
  - List recent entitlement binds: redwoodctl db query --sql "SELECT * FROM entitlement_audit WHERE timestamp > now() - interval '1 hour' ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT 200"
  - Snapshot audit stream: forensicsctl snapshot-audit --stream authz-stream --partition current --to s3://...
- Evidence bundler usage: evidence-bundler assemble --manifest manifest.csv --sign-with /keys/forensics-signing-key.pem --output <bundle>.tar.gz

Metrics and SLOs to monitor post-deployment:
- Mean Time to Contain (MTTC) for auth incidents: target <= 15 minutes (P0).
- Mean Time to Evidence Snapshot: target <= 60 minutes.
- Evidence bundle completeness: percent of incidents with completed bundle within 48 hours: target >= 95%.

Related references and links:
- Policy-as-code repo: git@internal:redwood/policy-repo.git
- Audit schema spec: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-schema-versioning-and-evidence-tagging-2026
- RBAC change retention policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/entitlement-provenance-and-retention-framework-2026

Revision history:
- 2028-03-15: Drafted by Priya Natarajan
- 2028-04-02: Reviewed by Ethan Cole and Maria Alvarez, added cross-region residency notes
- 2028-06-01: Published (updated evidence bundler commands, clarified exception process)

End of playbook. For questions or simulation requests (chaos-testing of authorization controls) contact security-oncall@redwood.example.internal or Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.example.internal).

title: Rapid Authorization Containment and Evidence Orchestration Playbook

Overview:

Purpose: This playbook prescribes a fast, auditable, cross-functional process for containing authorization-related incidents (privilege escalation, token misuse, delegated-authorization failures), collecting compliance-grade evidence, and orchestrating multi-team handoffs while respecting data residency and retention constraints. It is intended for SREs, SecOps, on-call engineers, product security, and legal.

Scope: RBAC and auth incidents impacting production inference endpoints, control-plane configuration (roles, attribute stores, delegation), audit log integrity, or any event that may require regulatory evidence packaging (SOC2 / ISO / regional data residency). - Contain active authorization exposure within 15 minutes for critical incidents.

source.title: Authorization Decision Logging and Data Sovereignty Playbook
source.author: Aisha Kapoor
source.content:
Overview:

This playbook defines how Redwood records authorization decisions (allow/deny), ties those records to the audit logging pipeline, and ensures decision data is stored and retained consistent with data residency and compliance requirements. It is intended for security engineers, SREs, compliance owners, product/platform engineers, and auditors.

Goals:
- Ensure every authorization decision is recorded with sufficient context to reconstruct who asked for access, why the decision occurred, and what policy evaluated.
- Maintain integrity and residency guarantees for decision logs (where they are stored, how long they are kept, and how they are provided as evidence).
- Integrate decision logging into incident and evidence workflows so audit packages can be produced within defined SLAs.

Scope and audience:
- Applies to: API authorization checks (token-based, service-to-service), console/admin role assignments, IAM changes initiated by UI/CLI/IaC.
- Out of scope: application-level business-logic telemetry that is not used for access enforcement (those follow product telemetry standards).

Principles:
1) All decisions are first-class audit artifacts: store an immutable decision record for every enforcement point.
2) Policy provenance: include policy id/version and evaluation reason for every decision.
3) Residency-aware ingestion: route decision records to region-local storage when required by customer residency profiles.
4) Minimal PII in logs: do not record sensitive request payloads; if necessary, redact and reference artifact IDs.

Key definitions:
- Decision record: Immutable event representing an allow/deny with context for requester, resource, action, policy and evaluation result.
- Decision store: Append-only storage for decision records (immutable bucket + write-once index).
- Evidence package: Collected artifacts (decision records, role history, policy versions, session recordings, key rotation logs) for auditors.

Authorization logging schema (required fields):
- timestamp (ISO-8601)
- request_id (correlation id)
- principal_id (user/service id)
- principal_type (user/service/key)
- action (e.g., inference.create, model.deploy)
- resource (resource path/arn)
- decision (allow|deny|conditional)
- policy_id
- policy_version
- evaluation_reason (text enum: policy_match, fallback-deny, role-binding, legacy-exception)
- enforcement_point (service name + version)
- region_tag (data residency tag for routing)
- obfuscated_payload_ref (optional artifact id if payload was stored separately)
- signature (HMAC or KMS signature of record metadata)

Example decision record (text snippet):
```
{
  "timestamp": "2028-03-01T14:22:03Z",
  "request_id": "req-4b2a-9f1d",
  "principal_id": "svc:billing-ingest@redwood",
  "principal_type": "service",
  "action": "models:upload",
  "resource": "arn:redwood:model:eu-west-1:customer-1234:model-xyz",
  "decision": "deny",
  "policy_id": "policy-iam-023",
  "policy_version": "v3.5",
  "evaluation_reason": "role-binding",
  "enforcement_point": "authz-proxy-v2",
  "region_tag": "eu",
  "signature": "sig:abcd..."
}
```

Audit pipeline and SLA:
- Ingestion: decision records are emitted synchronously by enforcement points and enqueued to the audit ingestion service. Goal: ingestion latency < 15s (99th percentile) for global flows; < 5s for region-local critical flows.
- Enrichment: enrichment layer attaches account metadata, role snapshots, and policy content at time-of-decision. Aim: enrichment complete within 60s of ingestion.
- Immutable storage: write-once objects stored in region-designated immutable buckets. Retention tags set at write-time.
- Search & query: short-term hot index (30 days) + cold archive (object store). Queries for recent 30 days should return within 10s for typical filters.

Retention & data residency mapping (minimum standards):
- Customer-controlled residency (when customer selects EU-only): all decision records referencing customer resources must remain in EU storage and never be replicated outside EU. Retention defaults:
  - High-sensitivity customer data / regulated customers: 7 years (or per contract)
  - Standard infra authorization events: 1 year
  - Short-lived debug traces: 30 days (redacted or obfuscated)
- Internal security-critical events (privileged role changes, emergency JIT approvals): retain 7 years and replicate to secure central archive per legal requirements.

Residency mapping examples (reference):
- region_tag = "eu" -> store in eu.audit.redwood.internal (S3/MinIO bucket: eu-decision-immutable)
- region_tag = "us" -> store in us.audit.redwood.internal
- region_tag = "apac" -> store in apac.audit.redwood.internal (customer-specific opt-in subject to contract)

Evidence workflow (audit request -> package delivery):
1) Request intake: compliance requests evidence via ticketing system (ticket ID). Owner: Compliance Operations. SLA: acknowledge within 8 business hours.
2) Scoping: Compliance + Security agree scope (time range, principals, resources). Owner: Security Evidence Lead. Deadline: 3 business days.
3) Extraction: Use decision-store export tooling to extract decision records by filters (request_id range, resource, principal). Command example: 
   curl -X POST https://audit-api.internal/export -H 'Authorization: Bearer $EVIDENCE_TOKEN' -d '{"filter": {"resource": "arn:redwood:model:...", "from": "2028-02-01T00:00:00Z", "to": "2028-02-28T23:59:59Z"}}' -o evidence-package-<ticket>.tgz
4) Correlation: Attach role history, policy snapshots, and session recordings (if allowed). Owner: SRE + Platform IAM.
5) Tamper-proofing: Generate signed manifest of artifacts and upload signed package to compliance vault. Owner: Security.
6) Delivery: Provide secure link to compliance/auditor and list of included artifacts and signatures. SLA: evidence package delivery within 10 business days for standard requests; expedited options available for high-priority regulatory audits.

Secure SDLC integration (controls to enforce decision logging correctness):
- PR gating: Any change to enforcement_point services or policy evaluation code must include unit tests that validate decision-record emission and schema conformance.
- IaC policy: Role bindings and policy manifests must be stored in git and pass a security policy linter (policy-lint) before merge.
- Pre-deploy checks: Canary deployment to staging with simulated decision traffic and verification that records are emitted and routed to the staging decision store.
- Release checklist extract: reviewer (security) approval, signed release artifact, rollout plan, rollback criteria.

Incident response for authorization-related incidents (summary playbook):
1) Identification: detection via anomaly signals (sudden spike in denies, privilege elevation alerts, JIT approvals outside windows).
2) Triage: Security engineer opens incident, captures a decision snapshot (immutable copy of decision stream for 72h window).
3) Containment: Revoke impacted tokens, disable affected role bindings, or apply emergency policy rollbacks.
4) Forensics: Freeze decision records, take signed manifests of all relevant artifacts (decision records, policy versions, role change events, session logs).
5) External notification: If customer data residency is implicated, notify Legal/Privacy and follow jurisdictional notification timelines.
6) Root cause & remediation: Complete within 14 days initial report; final remediation plan and verification within 60 days.
7) Evidence package: Assemble and store in compliance vault; delivery to auditors per evidence workflow.

Temporary exceptions and risk acceptance (process):
- Request: Engineered exception requests for policy changes or role assignments must be documented in the exception tracker with business justification, impact analysis, and mitigation plan.
- Approvals: Approval matrix requires Security Manager + Product Owner for low-risk exceptions, Security Director + Legal for moderate risk, and CISO approval for high risk.
- Timebox: Exceptions are timebound (max 30 days) and require automated expiry. Emergency JIT requests limited to 8 hours with automatic audit and post-mortem.
- Evidence: Each exception must be accompanied by decision trace examples demonstrating the exception behavior and a rollback plan.

Operational runbooks and owners:
- Audit ingestion issues: Owner: Eng-SRE, primary runbook link: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-ingest-failure. On-call escalation: 1st responder (SRE), 2nd (Platform), 3rd (Security).
- Policy drift detection: Owner: Applied-ML-and-Evals (for model-related policies) + Security. Weekly scan job publishes drift reports and blocking PRs.
- Evidence request handling: Owner: Compliance Operations.

Metrics and monitoring (dashboards):
- Decision emission coverage (percent of enforcement points emitting schema-complete records) – target: 99%
- Decision ingestion latency (p50/p95/p99) – target: p99 < 15s globally
- Evidence package turnaround time – target median 4 business days
- Residency violations detected (count) – target: 0

Appendix A — Sample checklist for production rollout of new enforcement point:
- [ ] Schema validated with policy-lint
- [ ] Tests for allow/deny/conditional paths present
- [ ] Region routing tested with residency-tagged simulated traffic
- [ ] Signed artifact and release notes uploaded
- [ ] Security review completed and documented
- [ ] Retention tags set and validated

Appendix B — Common artifacts included in an evidence package:
- Decision record exports (ndjson)
- Policy snapshots (yaml/json) and policy change history
- Role assignment and entitlements timeline
- Signed manifests and object store paths
- Relevant CI/CD deployment artifacts and release approvals

Contacts and escalation list:
- Security Evidence Lead: security-evidence@redwood.com (primary)
- Compliance Operations: compliance-ops@redwood.com
- Eng-SRE on-call: eng-sre-oncall@redwood.com
- Legal (privacy/regulatory): legal-privacy@redwood.com

Related docs and references:
- Event schema contract and retention runbook (see related_pages links above)
- Service identity rotation guidance
- Incident evidence orchestration playbook

Revision history:
- 2028-02-15: Initial draft by Aisha Kapoor
- 2028-02-28: Security review and enrichment (Rafael Mendes)
- 2028-03-02: Final revisions and publish

title: Authorization Decision Logging and Data Sovereignty Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines how Redwood records authorization decisions (allow/deny), ties those records to the audit logging pipeline, and ensures decision data is stored and retained consistent with data residency and compliance requirements. It is intended for security engineers, SREs, compliance owners, product/platform engineers, and auditors.

Goals:
- Ensure every authorization decision is recorded with sufficient context to reconstruct who asked for access, why the decision occurred, and what policy evaluated.
- Maintain integrity and residency guarantees for decision logs (where they are stored, how long they are kept, and how they are provided as evidence).
- Integrate decision logging into incident and evidence workflows so audit packages can be produced within defined SLAs.

Scope and audience:
- Applies to: API authorization checks (token-based, service-to-service), console/admin role assignments, IAM changes initiated by UI/CLI/IaC.
- Out of scope: application-level business-logic telemetry that is not used for access enforcement (those follow product telemetry standards).

Principles:
1) All decisions are first-class audit artifacts: store an immutable decision record for every enforcement point.
2) Policy provenance: include policy id/version and evaluation reason for every decision.
3) Residency-aware ingestion: route decision records to region-local storage when required by customer residency profiles.
4) Minimal PII in logs: do not record sensitive request payloads; if necessary, redact and reference artifact IDs.

Key definitions:
- Decision record: Immutable event representing an allow/deny with context for requester, resource, action, policy and evaluation result.
- Decision store: Append-only storage for decision records (immutable bucket + write-once index).
- Evidence package: Collected artifacts (decision records, role history, policy versions, session recordings, key rotation logs) for auditors.

Authorization logging schema (required fields):
- timestamp (ISO-8601)
- request_id (correlation id)
- principal_id (user/service id)
- principal_type (user/service/key)
- action (e.g., inference.create, model.deploy)
- resource (resource path/arn)
- decision (allow|deny|conditional)
- policy_id
- policy_version
- evaluation_reason (text enum: policy_match, fallback-deny, role-binding, legacy-exception)
- enforcement_point (service name + version)
- region_tag (data residency tag for routing)
- obfuscated_payload_ref (optional artifact id if payload was stored separately)
- signature (HMAC or KMS signature of record metadata)

Example decision record (text snippet):
```
{
  "timestamp": "2028-03-01T14:22:03Z",
  "request_id": "req-4b2a-9f1d",
  "principal_id": "svc:billing-ingest@redwood",
  "principal_type": "service",
  "action": "models:upload",
  "resource": "arn:redwood:model:eu-west-1:customer-1234:model-xyz",
  "decision": "deny",
  "policy_id": "policy-iam-023",
  "policy_version": "v3.5",
  "evaluation_reason": "role-binding",
  "enforcement_point": "authz-proxy-v2",
  "region_tag": "eu",
  "signature": "sig:abcd..."
}
```

Audit pipeline and SLA:
- Ingestion: decision records are emitted synchronously by enforcement points and enqueued to the audit ingestion service. Goal: ingestion latency < 15s (99th percentile) for global flows; < 5s for region-local critical flows.
- Enrichment: enrichment layer attaches account metadata, role snapshots, and policy content at time-of-decision. Aim: enrichment complete within 60s of ingestion.
- Immutable storage: write-once objects stored in region-designated immutable buckets. Retention tags set at write-time.
- Search & query: short-term hot index (30 days) + cold archive (object store). Queries for recent 30 days should return within 10s for typical filters.

Retention & data residency mapping (minimum standards):
- Customer-controlled residency (when customer selects EU-only): all decision records referencing customer resources must remain in EU storage and never be replicated outside EU. Retention defaults:
  - High-sensitivity customer data / regulated customers: 7 years (or per contract)
  - Standard infra authorization events: 1 year
  - Short-lived debug traces: 30 days (redacted or obfuscated)
- Internal security-critical events (privileged role changes, emergency JIT approvals): retain 7 years and replicate to secure central archive per legal requirements.

Residency mapping examples (reference):
- region_tag = "eu" -> store in eu.audit.redwood.internal (S3/MinIO bucket: eu-decision-immutable)
- region_tag = "us" -> store in us.audit.redwood.internal
- region_tag = "apac" -> store in apac.audit.redwood.internal (customer-specific opt-in subject to contract)

Evidence workflow (audit request -> package delivery):
1) Request intake: compliance requests evidence via ticketing system (ticket ID). Owner: Compliance Operations. SLA: acknowledge within 8 business hours.
2) Scoping: Compliance + Security agree scope (time range, principals, resources). Owner: Security Evidence Lead. Deadline: 3 business days.
3) Extraction: Use decision-store export tooling to extract decision records by filters (request_id range, resource, principal). Command example: 
   curl -X POST https://audit-api.internal/export -H 'Authorization: Bearer $EVIDENCE_TOKEN' -d '{"filter": {"resource": "arn:redwood:model:...", "from": "2028-02-01T00:00:00Z", "to": "2028-02-28T23:59:59Z"}}' -o evidence-package-<ticket>.tgz
4) Correlation: Attach role history, policy snapshots, and session recordings (if allowed). Owner: SRE + Platform IAM.
5) Tamper-proofing: Generate signed manifest of artifacts and upload signed package to compliance vault. Owner: Security.
6) Delivery: Provide secure link to compliance/auditor and list of included artifacts and signatures. SLA: evidence package delivery within 10 business days for standard requests; expedited options available for high-priority regulatory audits.

Secure SDLC integration (controls to enforce decision logging correctness):
- PR gating: Any change to enforcement_point services or policy evaluation code must include unit tests that validate decision-record emission and schema conformance.
- IaC policy: Role bindings and policy manifests must be stored in git and pass a security policy linter (policy-lint) before merge.
- Pre-deploy checks: Canary deployment to staging with simulated decision traffic and verification that records are emitted and routed to the staging decision store.
- Release checklist extract: reviewer (security) approval, signed release artifact, rollout plan, rollback criteria.

Incident response for authorization-related incidents (summary playbook):
1) Identification: detection via anomaly signals (sudden spike in denies, privilege elevation alerts, JIT approvals outside windows).
2) Triage: Security engineer opens incident, captures a decision snapshot (immutable copy of decision stream for 72h window).
3) Containment: Revoke impacted tokens, disable affected role bindings, or apply emergency policy rollbacks.
4) Forensics: Freeze decision records, take signed manifests of all relevant artifacts (decision records, policy versions, role change events, session logs).
5) External notification: If customer data residency is implicated, notify Legal/Privacy and follow jurisdictional notification timelines.
6) Root cause & remediation: Complete within 14 days initial report; final remediation plan and verification within 60 days.
7) Evidence package: Assemble and store in compliance vault; delivery to auditors per evidence workflow.

Temporary exceptions and risk acceptance (process):
- Request: Engineered exception requests for policy changes or role assignments must be documented in the exception tracker with business justification, impact analysis, and mitigation plan.
- Approvals: Approval matrix requires Security Manager + Product Owner for low-risk exceptions, Security Director + Legal for moderate risk, and CISO approval for high risk.
- Timebox: Exceptions are timebound (max 30 days) and require automated expiry. Emergency JIT requests limited to 8 hours with automatic audit and post-mortem.
- Evidence: Each exception must be accompanied by decision trace examples demonstrating the exception behavior and a rollback plan.

Operational runbooks and owners:
- Audit ingestion issues: Owner: Eng-SRE, primary runbook link: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-ingest-failure. On-call escalation: 1st responder (SRE), 2nd (Platform), 3rd (Security).
- Policy drift detection: Owner: Applied-ML-and-Evals (for model-related policies) + Security. Weekly scan job publishes drift reports and blocking PRs.
- Evidence request handling: Owner: Compliance Operations.

Metrics and monitoring (dashboards):
- Decision emission coverage (percent of enforcement points emitting schema-complete records) – target: 99%
- Decision ingestion latency (p50/p95/p99) – target: p99 < 15s globally
- Evidence package turnaround time – target median 4 business days
- Residency violations detected (count) – target: 0

Appendix A — Sample checklist for production rollout of new enforcement point:
- [ ] Schema validated with policy-lint
- [ ] Tests for allow/deny/conditional paths present
- [ ] Region routing tested with residency-tagged simulated traffic
- [ ] Signed artifact and release notes uploaded
- [ ] Security review completed and documented
- [ ] Retention tags set and validated

Appendix B — Common artifacts included in an evidence package:
- Decision record exports (ndjson)
- Policy snapshots (yaml/json) and policy change history
- Role assignment and entitlements timeline
- Signed manifests and object store paths
- Relevant CI/CD deployment artifacts and release approvals

Contacts and escalation list:
- Security Evidence Lead: security-evidence@redwood.com (primary)
- Compliance Operations: compliance-ops@redwood.com
- Eng-SRE on-call: eng-sre-oncall@redwood.com
- Legal (privacy/regulatory): legal-privacy@redwood.com

Related docs and references:
- Event schema contract and retention runbook (see related_pages links above)
- Service identity rotation guidance
- Incident evidence orchestration playbook

- 2028-02-15: Initial draft by Aisha Kapoor
- 2028-02-28: Security review and enrichment (Rafael Mendes)
- 2028-03-02: Final revisions and publish

source.title: Feature-Flag Access Governance and Evidence Playbook for Emergency Overrides
source.author: Arjun Patel
source.content:
Summary
-------
This playbook defines governance, RBAC controls, audit logging requirements, data residency constraints, SDLC gating, incident response procedures, and compliance evidence packaging for feature-flag emergency overrides (a.k.a. "kill switches" or "emergency features"). It covers both the control plane (console/CLI) and the runtime path (service-level toggles) and is intended for security engineers, SREs, product/platform engineers, and compliance reviewers.

Purpose and scope
-----------------
- Purpose: Ensure emergency feature-flag overrides can be executed with minimal blast radius, clear authorization, full auditable evidence, and predefined post-incident review steps.
- Scope: Applies to all feature-flag systems used to control inference routing, model selection, rollout gating, telemetry masking, or any change that affects production model outputs and customer data handling across Redwood-hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.
- Exclusions: Temporary local developer feature flags that do not touch production systems.

Definitions
-----------
- Emergency Override: An action which forces a system to a safe state (e.g., disable a model variant, revert routing to a fallback model, or mute logging to stop PII leakage) outside normal release processes.
- Control Plane Flag vs Runtime Flag: Control plane flags are changed via the console/CLI and affect configurations; runtime flags are evaluated at request time by services.

Principles
----------
1. Least-privilege execution: Only the minimal set of roles can initiate overrides.
2. Two-person controls for high-impact actions: Dual approval for overrides that change data residency, purge logs, or enable broad consumer-facing rollbacks.
3. Immutable audit trail: All override actions are logged to the centralized audit pipeline with immutable retention.
4. Evidence-first practice: Immediate evidence collection is required; evidence packaging must meet audit requirements for regulatory reviews.
5. Post-incident gating: Any override must be followed by RCA, mitigation, and, if required, a policy update in the SDLC.

Roles and responsibilities
--------------------------
- Security on-call: triage, approve (where designated), validate evidence collection, lead post-incident access review.
- SRE/Runtime owner: perform the override when authorized, execute runbook steps, ensure runtime invariants.
- Product owner: authorize business-impact changes and coordinate customer communications.
- Compliance/Ops: collect and package audit evidence for regulators.
- Feature-flag platform owners: ensure flags support metadata, expiration, scoping, and audit hooks.

RBAC model and matrix
---------------------
High-level rules:
- Default deny for override endpoints.
- Role bindings must be timebound for temporary elevation.
- Use attribute-based conditions (e.g., region, environment, incident severity) for scoping.

RBAC matrix (examples)
| Action | Low-impact flags (UI visibility only) | High-impact flags (routing, residency, log purge) | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| View flags | ops-reader | ops-reader | Read-only allowed for broad ops team |
| Schedule a disable (non-prod) | ops-engineer | ops-engineer | Non-prod only |
| Initiate emergency override (prod) | - | security-oncall + sre-oncall (approve/execute) | Two-person control enforced |
| Revoke override | security-oncall | security-oncall | Revoke requires single owner to restore |
| Grant temporary elevation | security-admin | security-admin | Must create entitlement ticket with TTL |

Technical controls required
---------------------------
- Flags must carry metadata: owner, intended impact, safe-state description, automated rollback TTL, allowed-roles, and residency-impact tag.
- All override APIs must accept a unique incident_id parameter and optional justification string.
- Feature-flag platform must support policy-based approvals (automated or manual) and emit structured audit events for each state change.

Audit logging requirements
-------------------------
Minimum fields to log for each override event (JSON payload example in Appendix):
- timestamp (ISO8601)
- actor_id (SCIM ID or service account)
- actor_role_bindings (list)
- action (e.g., flag.disable, flag.set-safe-state, flag.purge-logs)
- flag_id, flag_key, flag_scope (environments/regions)
- incident_id and justification_text
- approval_trace (if dual approval: approver_id, approver_role, approval_time)
- before_state, after_state (diff blob or pointer)
- retention_policy_applied (which retention rule was referenced)
- audit_store_checksum and storage_path (immutable store)

Logging delivery and retention
- All audit events must be forwarded to the centralized immutable audit store (S3 with Object Lock or equivalent for Private deployments) via the audit edge pipeline within 30s of action.
- Retention baseline: 7 years for actions affecting customer data residency or log purges; 2 years for lower-impact overrides.
- Short-term hot-store (30 days) must support full-text search and correlation with telemetry.

Data residency and retention considerations
----------------------------------------
- Any override that alters data residency (e.g., enable/disable a geo-routing flag) requires pre-approval from Data Governance unless it is a declared emergency with clear mitigation steps.
- If an override causes logs or telemetry to be redirected across jurisdictional boundaries, the override must record the legal_basis field and trigger a compliance notification to Legal.
- For Private deployments, evidence must not leave customer VPC without an explicit signed authorization; use in-situ evidence packaging when required.

SDLC gating and pre-deployment controls
-------------------------------------
Before a flag is used in production with emergency override capability, the following SDLC gates must be passed:
1. Security design review: documented threat model entry with affected data flows.
2. RBAC integration test: e2e tests validating binding enforcement and TTL behavior.
3. Audit pipeline test: simulated override with end-to-end audit record validation.
4. Runbook and rollback verification: rehearsed runbook in staging with game-day exercises.

Incident response: emergency override procedure
---------------------------------------------
1. Triage and decision: Security on-call assesses severity and decides on override necessity.
2. Assign incident_id: Create an incident in the designated tracker and include that id in the override call.
3. Dual approval (if required): Use the policy engine to request/record approver. For high-impact changes, require security-oncall and sre-oncall signatures.
4. Execute override: SRE executes the override using CLI with incident_id and justification.
   - CLI example: redwood-ffctl override --flag model-routing-A --state disabled --incident INC-20270210-17 --justification "stop-PII-exfil"
5. Verification: Confirm after_state and service health metrics for 15 minutes.
6. Evidence collection: Immediately trigger evidence collector (see Evidence Packaging section).
7. Communication: Notify product and legal if residency or contract-bound behaviors are impacted.
8. Post-incident: Create RCA, schedule entitlement attestation, and review RBAC bindings for any temporary elevations.

Evidence collection and packaging
--------------------------------
Required artifacts to collect within 60 minutes of override:
- Immutable audit event (full JSON payload) from audit store.
- Flag history timeline (60 minutes before through 24 hours after).
- Approval trace records (approver identities and timestamps).
- Related telemetry snapshots: routing tables, active KV cache entries, model versions served.
- Network flows and egress logs (if potential exfiltration).
- If logs were purged or redacted, include pre-purge snapshot and retention policy referenced.
- A signed evidence manifest (SHA256 hashes of artifacts) and storage locations.
Evidence packaging template (minimum):
- incident_id
- executive_summary (1–3 sentences)
- artifact_index (list of artifact keys/paths)
- retention_class (regulatory retention to apply)
- chain_of_custody (who accessed evidence)
- reviewer_signoffs

Exception and risk handling
---------------------------
- Pre-approved emergency exceptions: Small set of known and documented override types may be pre-authorized for single-person execution (e.g., kill switch for a telemetry stream known to leak non-customer data). These must be enumerated and time-limited in an exceptions registry.
- High-risk overrides (data-residency change, log-purge) cannot be pre-approved: they always require dual approval and legal notification.
- Risk scoring: Each override must be assigned a risk score (1–100) based on impact vectors: customer-data, residency, availability, reputation. If risk score >= 70, require elevated legal review post-override.

Monitoring and verification
--------------------------
- After any override, apply a 60-minute verification window: health checks, end-to-end request sampling, and audit-event replay must be validated.
- Automated anomaly detection should flag mismatches between expected and observed before_state/after_state for manual review.

Metrics and KPIs
---------------
- Time-to-override (target < 10 minutes for declared emergencies).
- Evidence completeness rate (target 100% for high-impact overrides).
- Number of temporary elevations per quarter (trend downwards).
- Number of post-override entitlements not revoked within 24 hours (target 0).

Operational runbooks and checklists
----------------------------------
- Runbook: emergency-override-execute.md (link to team-runbook).
- Checklist (for executing engineer):
  1. Confirm incident id and severity.
  2. Confirm approver IDs and record approval.
  3. Execute CLI/API with incident_id and justification.
  4. Verify after_state and publish verification snapshot.
  5. Trigger evidence collection job.
  6. Notify stakeholders.

Appendices
---------
Appendix A: Example audit event (JSON snippet)
{
  "timestamp": "2027-02-10T17:42:12Z",
  "actor_id": "scim:users:arjun.patel",
  "actor_role_bindings": ["security-oncall", "prod-ops"],
  "action": "flag.disable",
  "flag_id": "ff-model-routing-A",
  "flag_scope": "prod-us-east-1",
  "incident_id": "INC-20270210-17",
  "justification_text": "Mitigating active PII exfiltration from model outputs",
  "approval_trace": [{"approver_id": "scim:users:maya.chen", "approver_role": "security-oncall", "approval_time": "2027-02-10T17:41:58Z"}],
  "before_state": "enabled",
  "after_state": "disabled",
  "audit_store_checksum": "sha256:abcd...",
  "storage_path": "s3://redwood-audit/2027/02/10/INC-20270210-17.json"
}

Appendix B: CLI examples
- Execute emergency override (with approval stub):
  redwood-ffctl override --flag ff-model-routing-A --state disabled --incident INC-20270210-17 --justification "stop-PII-exfil" --approver maya.chen

Appendix C: Pre-approved exception registry sample
| exception_id | description | allowed_roles | expiry | notes |
| EXC-001 | Telemetry mute for non-customer-internal-only stream | sre-oncall | 2027-06-30 | Single-person allowed; must be recorded |

Related artifacts and links
--------------------------
- Feature-flag platform design doc (link: /confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/feature-flag-architecture)
- Audit pipeline contract (link: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/event-schema-contract-and-retention-runbook-2025)
- Incident runbook template (link: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/incident-runbook-template)

Review cadence and owner
------------------------
- Owner: Security (Arjun Patel).
- Review cadence: Quarterly for policies and after any high-impact override.

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-11-15: Created 
- 2027-02-10: Published with clarifications on evidence timelines and retention classes

Contact and escalation
----------------------
- Security oncall: pager / slack #sec-oncall
- SRE oncall: pager / slack #sre-oncall
- Compliance: compliance@redwood.example.com

title: Feature-Flag Access Governance and Evidence Playbook for Emergency Overrides
preview:
Summary
-------
This playbook defines governance, RBAC controls, audit logging requirements, data residency constraints, SDLC gating, incident response procedures, and compliance evidence packaging for feature-flag emergency overrides (a.k.a. "kill switches" or "emergency features"). It covers both the control plane (console/CLI) and the runtime path (service-level toggles) and is intended for security engineers, SREs, product/platform engineers, and compliance reviewers.

Purpose and scope
-----------------
- Purpose: Ensure emergency feature-flag overrides can be executed with minimal blast radius, clear authorization, full auditable evidence, and predefined post-incident review steps.
- Scope: Applies to all feature-flag systems used to control inference routing, model selection, rollout gating, telemetry masking, or any change that affects production model outputs and customer data handling across Redwood-hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.
- Exclusions: Temporary local developer feature flags that do not touch production systems.

Definitions
-----------
- Emergency Override: An action which forces a system to a safe state (e.g., disable a model variant, revert routing to a fallback model, or mute logging to stop PII leakage) outside normal release processes.
- Control Plane Flag vs Runtime Flag: Control plane flags are changed via the console/CLI and affect configurations; runtime flags are evaluated at request time by services.

Principles
----------
1. Least-privilege execution: Only the minimal set of roles can initiate overrides.
2. Two-person controls for high-impact actions: Dual approval for overrides that change data residency, purge logs, or enable broad consumer-facing rollbacks.
3. Immutable audit trail: All override actions are logged to the centralized audit pipeline with immutable retention.
4. Evidence-first practice: Immediate evidence collection is required; evidence packaging must meet audit requirements for regulatory reviews.
5. Post-incident gating: Any override must be followed by RCA, mitigation, and, if required, a policy update in the SDLC.

Roles and responsibilities
--------------------------
- Security on-call: triage, approve (where designated), validate evidence collection, lead post-incident access review.
- SRE/Runtime owner: perform the override when authorized, execute runbook steps, ensure runtime invariants.
- Product owner: authorize business-impact changes and coordinate customer communications.
- Compliance/Ops: collect and package audit evidence for regulators.
- Feature-flag platform owners: ensure flags support metadata, expiration, scoping, and audit hooks.

RBAC model and matrix
---------------------
High-level rules:
- Default deny for override endpoints.
- Role bindings must be timebound for temporary elevation.
- Use attribute-based conditions (e.g., region, environment, incident severity) for scoping.

RBAC matrix (examples)
| Action | Low-impact flags (UI visibility only) | High-impact flags (routing, residency, log purge) | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| View flags | ops-reader | ops-reader | Read-only allowed for broad ops team |
| Schedule a disable (non-prod) | ops-engineer | ops-engineer | Non-prod only |
| Initiate emergency override (prod) | - | security-oncall + sre-oncall (approve/execute) | Two-person control enforced |
| Revoke override | security-oncall | security-oncall | Revoke requires single owner to restore |
| Grant temporary elevation | security-admin | security-admin | Must create entitlement ticket with TTL |

Technical controls required
---------------------------
- Flags must carry metadata: owner, intended impact, safe-state description, automated rollback TTL, allowed-roles, and residency-impact tag.
- All override APIs must accept a unique incident_id parameter and optional justification string.
- Feature-flag platform must support policy-based approvals (automated or manual) and emit structured audit events for each state change.

Audit logging requirements
-------------------------
Minimum fields to log for each override event (JSON payload example in Appendix):
- timestamp (ISO8601)
- actor_id (SCIM ID or service account)
- actor_role_bindings (list)
- action (e.g., flag.disable, flag.set-safe-state, flag.purge-logs)
- flag_id, flag_key, flag_scope (environments/regions)
- incident_id and justification_text
- approval_trace (if dual approval: approver_id, approver_role, approval_time)
- before_state, after_state (diff blob or pointer)
- retention_policy_applied (which retention rule was referenced)
- audit_store_checksum and storage_path (immutable store)

Logging delivery and retention
- All audit events must be forwarded to the centralized immutable audit store (S3 with Object Lock or equivalent for Private deployments) via the audit edge pipeline within 30s of action.
- Retention baseline: 7 years for actions affecting customer data residency or log purges; 2 years for lower-impact overrides.
- Short-term hot-store (30 days) must support full-text search and correlation with telemetry.

Data residency and retention considerations
----------------------------------------
- Any override that alters data residency (e.g., enable/disable a geo-routing flag) requires pre-approval from Data Governance unless it is a declared emergency with clear mitigation steps.
- If an override causes logs or telemetry to be redirected across jurisdictional boundaries, the override must record the legal_basis field and trigger a compliance notification to Legal.
- For Private deployments, evidence must not leave customer VPC without an explicit signed authorization; use in-situ evidence packaging when required.

SDLC gating and pre-deployment controls
-------------------------------------
Before a flag is used in production with emergency override capability, the following SDLC gates must be passed:
1. Security design review: documented threat model entry with affected data flows.
2. RBAC integration test: e2e tests validating binding enforcement and TTL behavior.
3. Audit pipeline test: simulated override with end-to-end audit record validation.
4. Runbook and rollback verification: rehearsed runbook in staging with game-day exercises.

Incident response: emergency override procedure
---------------------------------------------
1. Triage and decision: Security on-call assesses severity and decides on override necessity.
2. Assign incident_id: Create an incident in the designated tracker and include that id in the override call.
3. Dual approval (if required): Use the policy engine to request/record approver. For high-impact changes, require security-oncall and sre-oncall signatures.
4. Execute override: SRE executes the override using CLI with incident_id and justification.
   - CLI example: redwood-ffctl override --flag model-routing-A --state disabled --incident INC-20270210-17 --justification "stop-PII-exfil"
5. Verification: Confirm after_state and service health metrics for 15 minutes.
6. Evidence collection: Immediately trigger evidence collector (see Evidence Packaging section).
7. Communication: Notify product and legal if residency or contract-bound behaviors are impacted.
8. Post-incident: Create RCA, schedule entitlement attestation, and review RBAC bindings for any temporary elevations.

Evidence collection and packaging
--------------------------------
Required artifacts to collect within 60 minutes of override:
- Immutable audit event (full JSON payload) from audit store.
- Flag history timeline (60 minutes before through 24 hours after).
- Approval trace records (approver identities and timestamps).
- Related telemetry snapshots: routing tables, active KV cache entries, model versions served.
- Network flows and egress logs (if potential exfiltration).
- If logs were purged or redacted, include pre-purge snapshot and retention policy referenced.
- A signed evidence manifest (SHA256 hashes of artifacts) and storage locations.
Evidence packaging template (minimum):
- incident_id
- executive_summary (1–3 sentences)
- artifact_index (list of artifact keys/paths)
- retention_class (regulatory retention to apply)
- chain_of_custody (who accessed evidence)
- reviewer_signoffs

Exception and risk handling
---------------------------
- Pre-approved emergency exceptions: Small set of known and documented override types may be pre-authorized for single-person execution (e.g., kill switch for a telemetry stream known to leak non-customer data). These must be enumerated and time-limited in an exceptions registry.
- High-risk overrides (data-residency change, log-purge) cannot be pre-approved: they always require dual approval and legal notification.
- Risk scoring: Each override must be assigned a risk score (1–100) based on impact vectors: customer-data, residency, availability, reputation. If risk score >= 70, require elevated legal review post-override.

Monitoring and verification
--------------------------
- After any override, apply a 60-minute verification window: health checks, end-to-end request sampling, and audit-event replay must be validated.
- Automated anomaly detection should flag mismatches between expected and observed before_state/after_state for manual review.

Metrics and KPIs
---------------
- Time-to-override (target < 10 minutes for declared emergencies).
- Evidence completeness rate (target 100% for high-impact overrides).
- Number of temporary elevations per quarter (trend downwards).
- Number of post-override entitlements not revoked within 24 hours (target 0).

Operational runbooks and checklists
----------------------------------
- Runbook: emergency-override-execute.md (link to team-runbook).
- Checklist (for executing engineer):
  1. Confirm incident id and severity.
  2. Confirm approver IDs and record approval.
  3. Execute CLI/API with incident_id and justification.
  4. Verify after_state and publish verification snapshot.
  5. Trigger evidence collection job.
  6. Notify stakeholders.

Appendices
---------
Appendix A: Example audit event (JSON snippet)
{
  "timestamp": "2027-02-10T17:42:12Z",
  "actor_id": "scim:users:arjun.patel",
  "actor_role_bindings": ["security-oncall", "prod-ops"],
  "action": "flag.disable",
  "flag_id": "ff-model-routing-A",
  "flag_scope": "prod-us-east-1",
  "incident_id": "INC-20270210-17",
  "justification_text": "Mitigating active PII exfiltration from model outputs",
  "approval_trace": [{"approver_id": "scim:users:maya.chen", "approver_role": "security-oncall", "approval_time": "2027-02-10T17:41:58Z"}],
  "before_state": "enabled",
  "after_state": "disabled",
  "audit_store_checksum": "sha256:abcd...",
  "storage_path": "s3://redwood-audit/2027/02/10/INC-20270210-17.json"
}

Appendix B: CLI examples
- Execute emergency override (with approval stub):
  redwood-ffctl override --flag ff-model-routing-A --state disabled --incident INC-20270210-17 --justification "stop-PII-exfil" --approver maya.chen

Appendix C: Pre-approved exception registry sample
| exception_id | description | allowed_roles | expiry | notes |
| EXC-001 | Telemetry mute for non-customer-internal-only stream | sre-oncall | 2027-06-30 | Single-person allowed; must be recorded |

Related artifacts and links
--------------------------
- Feature-flag platform design doc (link: /confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/feature-flag-architecture)
- Audit pipeline contract (link: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/event-schema-contract-and-retention-runbook-2025)
- Incident runbook template (link: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/incident-runbook-template)

Review cadence and owner
------------------------
- Owner: Security (Arjun Patel).
- Review cadence: Quarterly for policies and after any high-impact override.

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-11-15: Created

Contact and escalation
----------------------
- Security oncall: pager / slack #sec-oncall
- SRE oncall: pager / slack #sre-oncall

source.title: Authorization & Evidence Runbook for Delegated Access
source.author: Aisha Raman
source.content:
Overview
-------
This runbook describes the authorization model, delegation patterns, required audit evidence, and operational procedures for onboarding, operating, and investigating delegated access at Redwood Inference. It is intended for Security, IAM, SRE, Product Engineering, and Compliance teams. It explains how RBAC and attribute-bound delegation are implemented, what telemetry must be produced and retained, how artifacts are packaged for audits, and how risk exceptions are handled.

Purpose and scope
------------------
- Purpose: Provide a single, actionable reference for safely granting delegated access and proving compliance with access controls.
- Scope: Applies to all production and management-plane systems (hosted API control plane, Dedicated provisioning portal, Private deployment orchestration, infra consoles) and supporting telemetry (audit pipelines, SIEM).
- Excluded: Personal dev machines (see People Ops laptop policy) — those are covered by endpoint management docs.

Key definitions
---------------
- Delegated role: A short-lived or long-lived role granted to a team or third party that confers specific permissions to act on resources.
- Evidence bundle: A time-stamped package of logs, policy snapshots, attestation records and communications used for audit or incident response.
- ABAC attributes: Environment, region, data_sensitivity, project_owner, request_justification.

Principles
----------
1. Least privilege by default: start with minimal permissions and elevate only with documented justification and approval.
2. Short-lived delegation: prefer time-bound roles (max 30 days for standard delegates) using ephemeral credentials.
3. Audit-first operations: assume every access will be audited; require contextual telemetry and justification metadata on request.
4. Separation of duties: granting, reviewing, and attesting are handled by distinct roles.

Roles & responsibilities
------------------------
- IAM Team (owner): policy templates, role lifecycle automation, standard delegation workflows, and periodic attestation jobs.
- Security Ops: anomaly detection rules, audit evidence collection, owner for evidence bundles during investigations.
- Product Engineering: provide least-privilege role definitions per service and maintain mapping of APIs to fine-grained permissions.
- Compliance: declares required retention periods and approves exceptions for legal holds.
- Requesting Team Owner: provides request justification and operational expiration date.

Authorization model (recommended)
---------------------------------
1. RBAC core + ABAC constraints: define core roles (viewer, writer, operator, admin) and require at least two ABAC attributes for delegation: project_owner and data_sensitivity.
2. Policy naming convention: rwd:service:<service-name>:role:<role-name>:env:<env>
3. Example role binding: rwd:infra:operator:staging with attributes {project_owner: "billing-backend", data_sensitivity: "low"}

Delegation matrix (example)
--------------------------
- Service operator (common): can restart pods, inspect metrics, but cannot read PII data. Expiry: 7 days. Approval: team-owner + infra-oncall.
- Emergency escalation (rare): full service admin for incident triage. Expiry: 4 hours. Approval: Security Ops + Eng Lead.
- Third-party vendor: scoped to a single set of URIs and timeboxed. Expiry: contract-defined <= 30 days. Approval: Security + Procurement.

Required telemetry & audit logging
---------------------------------
All delegated access must surface the following telemetry (minimum):
- principal_id, role_name, delegation_request_id, timestamp, request_context (API path, query params limit to 1k chars), source_ip, user_agent, region, ABAC attributes, justification field, session_duration, actions_performed (list).
- Structured log schema: JSON lines with keys specified above. Log example (single-line):
  {"timestamp":"2027-01-10T14:02:11Z","principal_id":"alice@redwood.com","role_name":"rwd:infra:operator:prod","delegation_request_id":"del-3a7b","actions_performed":"restart:svc/payment","source_ip":"198.51.100.12","region":"us-west-2"}

SLOs & retention
-----------------
- Audit ingestion latency: 90th percentile <= 60s from access event to SIEM index.
- Alerting: any use of a role with data_sensitivity:high triggers high-priority alert.
- Default retention: 365 days for access logs.
- Extended retention: 7 years for legal holds and regulatory evidence; Compliance must create an evidence-hold.
- Access log snapshot: weekly immutable snapshots stored in region-specific object buckets (see Data residency).

Data residency and retention considerations
-----------------------------------------
- Logs must be stored according to the request's data_sensitivity attribute and customer region. For EU-region activity, mirror logs to eu-central-1 with encryption-at-rest using EU KMS key.
- For Private deployments, the evidence bundle must remain within customer VPC unless a signed export authorization is provided.

Evidence bundle composition
--------------------------
Each evidence bundle (for audits or incidents) should include the following artifacts and a manifest:
- manifest.txt (md5 checksum, creation time, collector id)
- policy_snapshot.json (the role and policy attached at time T)
- access_events.log (compressed, GZIP) containing the filtered structured logs for the time window
- session_recording.tgz (when session replay is enabled) or command_history.json
- attestation_emails.pdf or approval_ticket.csv with approver names and timestamps
- chain_of_custody.txt describing collection steps and operator identity
- preservation_certificate.txt (hashed signature by Security Ops)

Example evidence-bundle manifest (flat text example inside archive):
  manifest: {
    "bundle_id": "evid-20270110-3a7b",
    "collected_by": "security-ops-collector-4",
    "time_range": "2027-01-10T13:45:00Z/2027-01-10T14:20:00Z",
    "checksums": {"access_events.log.gz":"md5:6b1f..."}
  }

Procedure: Requesting delegated access (step-by-step)
-----------------------------------------------------
1. Requester opens Delegation Request in internal portal (path: /delegation/request). Provide: team_owner, service, requested_role, justification, expiration_time, ABAC attributes.
2. IAM automated checks run: validates role template, checks conflicts (no overlapping admin roles), and inserts delegation_request_id.
3. Approvals: team_owner + (if data_sensitivity:high then Security + Compliance). Approvers must sign in via SSO and provide short rationale.
4. If approved, IAM issues ephemeral credentials via internal STS with TTL = min(requested_expiry, template_max_ttl). An audit event is emitted.
5. Requester receives credentials and must include delegation_request_id in all requests via header X-RWD-Del-Id.

CLI example: request a delegated operator role (internal CLI rwdctl)
  rwdctl delegation request --service infra --role operator --expiry 168h --project billing-backend --justification "oncall restart"

Investigating anomalous delegated access
--------------------------------------
Trigger conditions (automated):
- Use of admin-level role outside business hours from a foreign IP
- Role used to access data with data_sensitivity:high without matching ABAC attributes
- Delegation_request_id reused across principals

Investigation playbook (prioritized)
1. Triage: Security Ops creates an Incident Incident-X and sets severity using incident matrix.
2. Preserve evidence: snapshot SIEM indices for the time window and isolate the tenant or role.
3. Collect evidence bundle (see composition). Use collector script: collect-evidence --from 2027-01-10T13:45:00Z --to 2027-01-10T14:20:00Z --role rwd:infra:operator:prod --out /tmp/evid-{{bundle_id}}.gz
4. Validate chain-of-custody: sign with Security Ops HSM key and store signature in internal storage (vault://evidence/signatures/...).
5. Coordinate with Product Eng and SRE for remediation (revoke role, rotate keys).
6. If customer data is affected, notify Compliance and Legal and activate regulatory timelines (72-hour breach notification for PII where applicable).

Collector CLI example (internal; pseudo-Bash)
  # run as security-ops-collector role
  collect-evidence \
    --siem-query "role_name:\\"rwd:infra:operator:prod\\" AND delegation_request_id:\\"del-3a7b\\"" \
    --start 2027-01-10T13:45:00Z --end 2027-01-10T14:20:00Z \
    --output /tmp/evid-e2f1.tgz

Secure SDLC touchpoints for delegated access features
----------------------------------------------------
- Design review: every new delegation capability must have an ADR that describes threat-model, ABAC attributes, logging schema and retention. See ADR template: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-template.
- Pre-merge checks: must include unit + integration tests that validate delegation TTL and header propagation (X-RWD-Del-Id).
- Release checklist: feature flag gating, canary population, and a rollback plan that revokes issued credentials.

Risk exceptions & acceptance process
-----------------------------------
When a team requests an exception (longer TTL, cross-region export of evidence, or broad role scope):
1. Submit exception request with risk assessment via the Exceptions portal. Include compensating controls (e.g., two-person attestation, dedicated logging sink).
2. Security Risk Board reviews weekly; approval requires Security Lead + Compliance. If customer-impacting, Legal signs off.
3. All approved exceptions are logged in the exception register with a hard expiry and required mitigations.

Evidence submission for external auditors
----------------------------------------
- Packaging: use the evidence bundle format and produce a signed registry entry.
- Delivery: SFTP to pre-authorized auditor endpoint OR share via secure artifact portal; never send raw logs to external email.
- Audit checklist: include policy_snapshot.json, access_events.log for requested windows, approval artifacts, and chain_of_custody.txt.

Operational dashboards & alerting (where to look)
------------------------------------------------
- Delegation overview dashboard (Grafana): panels for active delegated roles, TTL distribution, top principals, and region heatmap.
- SIEM rule set: rwd-delegation-anomaly-rules in Splunk/Elastic.
- Cost/usage monitor: identify if delegated roles cause burst in token usage relative to baseline.

Playbook maintenance & training
------------------------------
- Runbook owner: IAM Team. Review cadence: quarterly.
- Annual tabletop: Security runs a delegation tabletop exercise (Game Day) with SRE and Product teams.
- Training: Delegation requesters must complete the Delegation and Evidence course in the internal LMS; maintain records for compliance.

Appendices
---------
A. Sample policy template (pseudo-YAML)
  - name: rwd:infra:operator:prod
    actions: [restart_service, view_metrics, read_service_config]
    resources: [service:payment/*]
    attributes_required: [project_owner, environment]

B. Quick query examples
  - Elastic DSL: {"query": {"bool": {"must": [{"match": {"delegation_request_id": "del-3a7b"}}]}}}
  - SQL: SELECT timestamp, principal_id, action FROM access_events WHERE delegation_request_id = 'del-3a7b' ORDER BY timestamp;

C. Contact matrix
  - IAM oncall: #iam-oncall
  - Security Ops: security-ops@redwood.com, PagerDuty: secops
  - Compliance: compliance@redwood.com

D. Change log
  - 2026-11-05: created by Aisha Raman (initial draft)
  - 2026-12-10: added evidence manifest template and retention policy (review by Diego Morales)
  - 2027-01-12: clarified ABAC attributes and emergency escalation approval flow (reviewed by Priya Nair)

End of document
