Risk exceptions & acceptance process
-----------------------------------
When a team requests an exception (longer TTL, cross-region export of evidence, or broad role scope):
1. Submit exception request with risk assessment via the Exceptions portal. Include compensating controls (e.g., two-person attestation, dedicated logging sink).
2. Security Risk Board reviews weekly; approval requires Security Lead + Compliance. If customer-impacting, Legal signs off.
3. All approved exceptions are logged in the exception register with a hard expiry and required mitigations.

Evidence submission for external auditors
----------------------------------------
- Packaging: use the evidence bundle format and produce a signed registry entry.
- Delivery: SFTP to pre-authorized auditor endpoint OR share via secure artifact portal; never send raw logs to external email.
- Audit checklist: include policy_snapshot.json, access_events.log for requested windows, approval artifacts, and chain_of_custody.txt.

Operational dashboards & alerting (where to look)
------------------------------------------------
- Delegation overview dashboard (Grafana): panels for active delegated roles, TTL distribution, top principals, and region heatmap.
- SIEM rule set: rwd-delegation-anomaly-rules in Splunk/Elastic.
- Cost/usage monitor: identify if delegated roles cause burst in token usage relative to baseline.

Playbook maintenance & training
------------------------------
- Runbook owner: IAM Team. Review cadence: quarterly.
- Annual tabletop: Security runs a delegation tabletop exercise (Game Day) with SRE and Product teams.
- Training: Delegation requesters must complete the Delegation and Evidence course in the internal LMS; maintain records for compliance.

Appendices
---------
A. Sample policy template (pseudo-YAML)
  - name: rwd:infra:operator:prod
    actions: [restart_service, view_metrics, read_service_config]
    resources: [service:payment/*]
    attributes_required: [project_owner, environment]

B. Quick query examples
  - Elastic DSL: {"query": {"bool": {"must": [{"match": {"delegation_request_id": "del-3a7b"}}]}}}
  - SQL: SELECT timestamp, principal_id, action FROM access_events WHERE delegation_request_id = 'del-3a7b' ORDER BY timestamp;

C. Contact matrix
  - IAM oncall: #iam-oncall
  - Security Ops: security-ops@redwood.com, PagerDuty: secops
  - Compliance: compliance@redwood.com

D. Change log
  - 2026-11-05: created by Aisha Raman (initial draft)
  - 2026-12-10: added evidence manifest template and retention policy (review by Diego Morales)
  - 2027-01-12: clarified ABAC attributes and emergency escalation approval flow (reviewed by Priya Nair)

End of document

source.title: Post-Incident Authorization Reconstruction and Evidence Playbook
source.author: Aisha Patel
source.content:
Overview
--------
This playbook defines a standard, auditable process for reconstructing authorization decisions and packaging compliance evidence after a security incident that potentially involves access control or privilege misuse. It focuses on end-to-end traceability: collecting authoritative logs, reconstructing who could authorize what, preserving chain-of-custody, and producing a compliance-ready evidence bundle for legal, privacy, and audit stakeholders.

Scope
-----
- Incidents that involve one or more of: unauthorized access, privilege escalation, suspicious entitlement changes, service-account misuse, or failed/abnormal authorization flows.
- Applies across hosted, dedicated and private deployments; where data residency constraints exist, use the Data Residency section below.
- Excludes unrelated application-layer errors (see application incident playbooks).

Primary objectives
------------------
- Recreate a time-ordered view of authorization events that explains how access was granted or used.
- Produce an evidence bundle with signed checksums and documented custody suitable for regulators and internal audit.
- Identify root cause in access model, policy, or tooling and prescribe remediation steps with owners and deadlines.

Roles and responsibilities
--------------------------
- Security Incident Lead (SIL): overall owner of reconstruction, coordination with Legal/Compliance, final sign-off on evidence bundle.
- Forensics Engineer: executes log exports, integrity hashing, timeline reconstruction, and writes the forensic report.
- SRE / Logging Owner: provides raw log access, queries, and assistance with retention/backfill.
- Identity Team (IAM): provides entitlement snapshots, policy versions, and user provisioning records.
- Legal & Privacy: advises on disclosure obligations and holds copy of final bundle.
- Customer Success / Account Owner: notified for customer-impacting incidents per contract.

Prerequisites and authoritative sources
--------------------------------------
Collect the following authoritative sources (minimum required):
1) Audit log stream (immutable store): authorization.request, authorization.decision, token.issue, role.change events. Include full raw payload.
2) Identity change history: provisioning/deprovisioning events, group membership changes, SCIM logs.
3) Service account and key metadata: creation timestamps, last-used time, and rotation history.
4) Access policies and policy-version snapshot at incident time (RBAC/ABAC JSON/YAML).
5) Application-level traces that map request context to authz decisions (trace IDs, request IDs).
6) Network and host telemetry if lateral movement is suspected.
7) Configuration management records (IaC commits, deployment tags).

Required metadata for each artifact
----------------------------------
- ExportedBy: username/email that performed the export
- ExportTimestamp: ISO-8601 UTC
- SourceURI: canonical path or S3 URL to raw artifact
- Hash (sha256): hex digest
- RetentionPolicy: e.g. "90d-archive-encrypted"

Step-by-step reconstruction procedure
------------------------------------
1) Triage and scope confirmation (15–60 minutes)
   - SIL confirms affected tenant(s), systems, and preliminary timeframe.
   - Assign Forensics Engineer and request read-only access to audit store.
2) Preservation (first 2 hours)
   - Create immutable export snapshots of audit log partitions intersecting the timeframe. Use storage with object-lock (if available).
   - Capture entitlement snapshot: current policy + policy-version at T0 (use IAM API).
   - If data residency constraints exist, spin up analysis workspace in the approved region; do not move raw logs across borders.
3) Hashing and chain-of-custody (immediately after preservation)
   - For each exported file, compute sha256 and PGP-sign the digest. Store hashes in the custody ledger (append-only).
   - Record ExportedBy and ExportTimestamp into the incident ticket.
4) Timeline reconstruction (4–8 hours)
   - Normalize events by timestamp (UTC) and correlate by request_id / trace_id / subject_id.
   - Build a time-ordered table with columns: timestamp, event_type, subject_id, resource, decision, policy_version, correlation_id.
   - Identify authorization decision chains (e.g., token issuance -> delegation -> API call).
5) Policy-state mapping (4 hours)
   - Map observed decisions to the policy snapshot: show which rule matched and the effective evaluation path.
   - Where gaps exist (missing policy version), collect commit history from IaC and record a best-effort reconstruction.
6) Entitlement lineage and exposure analysis (8–16 hours)
   - Determine how entitlements propagated (group membership, inherited roles, service account delegation).
   - Compute blast radius: list principals with similar entitlements and last activity.
7) Evidence packaging & sign-off (same day if possible, otherwise 48–72h)
   - Create an evidence bundle (directory) containing raw exports, normalized CSV timelines, policy snapshots, hashes, verification keys, and a short Forensic Executive Summary (1–2 pages).
   - SIL obtains Legal sign-off and archives one copy in the regulated evidence store.

Minimum SLOs and timelines
--------------------------
- Preservation initiated within 2 hours of incident detection.
- Immutable export hash produced within 3 hours.
- Initial timeline & executive summary within 24 hours.
- Fully packaged evidence bundle within 72 hours (unless legal requests extension).

Sample CLI snippets
-------------------
- Export audit logs (example):
  # list partitions for 2026-07-19
  redwood-audit-cli list-partitions --date 2026-07-19 --service authz

  # export partition to secure bucket
  redwood-audit-cli export --partition p-20260719-3 --format ndjson --dest s3://evidence-bucket/INC-12345/

- Compute hash and sign (Linux):
  sha256sum exported-logs.ndjson | tee exported-logs.sha256
  gpg --default-key incident-signing-key --output exported-logs.sha256.sig --sign exported-logs.sha256

Normalization example (CSV header)
---------------------------------
timestamp, event_type, subject_id, resource, action, decision, policy_version, trace_id, raw_ref
2026-07-19T12:34:56Z, authorization.decision, user:alice@example.com, model/serve/123, invoke, allow, v2026.07.01, trace-abc123, s3://...

Data residency and cross-border constraints
-----------------------------------------
- If any affected tenant requires data to remain in-region, perform preservation and initial analysis in an approved regional workspace.
- Do not copy raw logs outside the allowed region; instead, run transforms in-region and export only derived, minimized artifacts (e.g., timelines with redacted PII) to the investigator's region when legally authorized.
- Document explicit legal approvals in the incident ticket prior to any cross-border transfer.

Chain-of-custody ledger template
-------------------------------
Use a single CSV file named custody-ledger.csv with entries:
artifact_id, source_uri, exported_by, export_ts, sha256, signature_uri, stored_at, access_restriction, notes

Example row:
art-0001, s3://evidence-bucket/INC-12345/audit-part-1.ndjson, a.patel@redwood.ai, 2026-07-19T13:05:02Z, 3f786850e387550fdab836ed7e6a5f7f, s3://evidence-bucket/INC-12345/art-0001.sha256.sig, gs://secure-archive/INC-12345, restricted, "Exported with object-lock enabled"

Remediation and entitlement rollback playbook (fast path)
--------------------------------------------------------
1) Short-term containment (0–30 minutes)
   - If ongoing misuse is detected, revoke session tokens and active API keys for implicated principals.
   - Apply temporary deny rule to the affected resource(s).
2) Targeted deprovisioning (30–120 minutes)
   - Deactivate compromised service accounts and rotate keys.
   - Remove risky role bindings, record pre/post snapshots.
3) Reassert least-privilege (24–72 hours)
   - Re-evaluate role definitions and reduce privileges where possible.
   - Issue org-wide advisory for similar entitlement patterns and schedule remediation tasks in IAM backlog.

Reporting and evidence delivery
------------------------------
- Evidence bundle recipients: Legal, Compliance, Internal Audit, C-level security liaison.
- Where customer data is involved, Customer Success coordinates notification per contract and Legal guidance.
- Deliverables: custody-ledger.csv, hashes & signatures, timeline.csv, raw exports (access-controlled), forensic-summary.pdf.

Post-incident review and preventive controls
------------------------------------------
- Within 7 days: conduct a joint postmortem meeting (Security, IAM, SRE, Product).
- Produce a remediation plan with owners and dates. Typical mitigations: tighter policy scoping, increased audit retention for sensitive resources, automated anomaly detection rules, mandatory JIT for high-risk roles.

Appendix A — Authorization event field mapping
--------------------------------------------
Use the following canonical mapping when normalizing logs:
- event_type: one of {authorization.request, authorization.decision, token.issue, role.binding.change, key.rotate}
- subject_id: canonical principal string (user:email | svc:service-name | app:key-id)
- resource: canonical resource path (model/<id> | project/<id>/dataset)
- policy_version: git sha or semantic tag
- decision: allow | deny | conditional

Appendix B — Quick query examples
--------------------------------
- Find all allow decisions for subject in a period (example ES query):
  GET /audit-*/_search
  { "query": { "bool": { "must": [{ "match": { "subject_id": "user:alice@example.com" }},{ "term": { "decision": "allow" }},{ "range": { "@timestamp": { "gte": "2026-07-19T00:00:00Z", "lte": "2026-07-20T00:00:00Z" }}}]}}}

Checklist (incident ticket tasks)
--------------------------------
- [ ] Preservation exports created and hashes computed
- [ ] Entitlement snapshot captured
- [ ] Timeline CSV produced
- [ ] Evidence bundle assembled and signatures verified
- [ ] Legal sign-off recorded
- [ ] Customer notification evaluated and executed (if needed)
- [ ] Postmortem scheduled and remediation plan created

Contacts and escalation
----------------------
- Security Oncall: security-oncall@redwood.ai
- Forensics rotation: forensics@redwood.ai
- IAM team: iam-owners@redwood.ai
- Legal (urgent): legal-incident@redwood.ai

References
----------
- Audit schema & retention runbook: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/event-schema-contract-and-retention-runbook-2025
- RBAC deprovisioning guidance: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/authorization-logging-and-deprovisioning-guidance-2026
- Data residency playbook: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/geo-smart-retention-and-audit-evidence-playbook-2027

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-01-15: Created by Aisha Patel (initial draft)
- 2026-03-02: Added data residency constraints (review: Marcus Lin)
- 2026-07-20: Expanded CLI examples and SLOs; changed confidentiality to restricted

art-0001, s3://evidence-bucket/INC-12345/audit-part-1.ndjson, a.patel@redwood.ai, 2026-07-19T13:05:02Z, 3f786850e387550fdab836ed7e6a5f7f, s3://evidence-bucket/INC-12345/art-0001.sha256.sig, gs://secure-archive/INC-12345, restricted, "Exported with object-lock enabled"

Remediation and entitlement rollback playbook (fast path)
--------------------------------------------------------
1) Short-term containment (0–30 minutes)
   - If ongoing misuse is detected, revoke session tokens and active API keys for implicated principals.
   - Apply temporary deny rule to the affected resource(s).
2) Targeted deprovisioning (30–120 minutes)
   - Deactivate compromised service accounts and rotate keys.
   - Remove risky role bindings, record pre/post snapshots.
3) Reassert least-privilege (24–72 hours)
   - Re-evaluate role definitions and reduce privileges where possible.
   - Issue org-wide advisory for similar entitlement patterns and schedule remediation tasks in IAM backlog.

Reporting and evidence delivery
------------------------------
- Evidence bundle recipients: Legal, Compliance, Internal Audit, C-level security liaison.
- Where customer data is involved, Customer Success coordinates notification per contract and Legal guidance.
- Deliverables: custody-ledger.csv, hashes & signatures, timeline.csv, raw exports (access-controlled), forensic-summary.pdf.

Post-incident review and preventive controls
------------------------------------------
- Within 7 days: conduct a joint postmortem meeting (Security, IAM, SRE, Product).
- Produce a remediation plan with owners and dates. Typical mitigations: tighter policy scoping, increased audit retention for sensitive resources, automated anomaly detection rules, mandatory JIT for high-risk roles.

Appendix A — Authorization event field mapping
--------------------------------------------
Use the following canonical mapping when normalizing logs:
- event_type: one of {authorization.request, authorization.decision, token.issue, role.binding.change, key.rotate}
- subject_id: canonical principal string (user:email | svc:service-name | app:key-id)
- resource: canonical resource path (model/<id> | project/<id>/dataset)
- policy_version: git sha or semantic tag
- decision: allow | deny | conditional

Appendix B — Quick query examples
--------------------------------
- Find all allow decisions for subject in a period (example ES query):
  GET /audit-*/_search
  { "query": { "bool": { "must": [{ "match": { "subject_id": "user:alice@example.com" }},{ "term": { "decision": "allow" }},{ "range": { "@timestamp": { "gte": "2026-07-19T00:00:00Z", "lte": "2026-07-20T00:00:00Z" }}}]}}}

Checklist (incident ticket tasks)
--------------------------------
- [ ] Preservation exports created and hashes computed
- [ ] Entitlement snapshot captured
- [ ] Timeline CSV produced
- [ ] Evidence bundle assembled and signatures verified
- [ ] Legal sign-off recorded
- [ ] Customer notification evaluated and executed (if needed)
- [ ] Postmortem scheduled and remediation plan created

Contacts and escalation
----------------------
- Security Oncall: security-oncall@redwood.ai
- Forensics rotation: forensics@redwood.ai
- IAM team: iam-owners@redwood.ai
- Legal (urgent): legal-incident@redwood.ai

References
----------
- Audit schema & retention runbook: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/event-schema-contract-and-retention-runbook-2025
- RBAC deprovisioning guidance: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/authorization-logging-and-deprovisioning-guidance-2026
- Data residency playbook: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/geo-smart-retention-and-audit-evidence-playbook-2027

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-01-15: Created by Aisha Patel (initial draft)
- 2026-03-02: Added data residency constraints (review: Marcus Lin)
- 2026-07-20: Expanded CLI examples and SLOs; changed confidentiality to restricted

source.title: Release access, residency, and compliance evidence manifest
source.author: Ava Mitchell
source.body:
Summary:
This manifest defines the standardized procedures, controls, and artifact packaging for production release access that intersects RBAC, audit logging, data residency, secure SDLC gates, incident response, and compliance evidence collection. It is intended for engineering release owners, security reviewers, platform engineers, and compliance auditors.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all production release pipelines (pipelines/redwood-release) and emergency hotfixes that modify: model-serving clusters, inference/runtime, authentication/authorization, and data-handling components.
- Does NOT replace team-level retention policies for business logs; it governs security/audit artifacts required for regulatory and internal controls.

Key definitions (for this manifest):
- Release Access: any privilege elevation or hold that allows code or config to reach production.
- Evidence Bundle: a signed archive containing artifacts required for audit review and legal hold.
- Residency Decision: the explicit selection of data locality and retention parameters for a release (region tag + retention profile).

Principles:
1) Least privilege by default: production release roles are narrow and timebound.
2) Procedural evidence: every production access event that enables a release must be accompanied by an Evidence Bundle.
3) Residency awareness: every release must record the data residency label and retention profile for affected artifacts.
4) Forensic readiness: preservation and integrity checks (hashing + signing) are mandatory for evidence required by audits and incidents.

Ownership and SLAs:
- Release owner (team): responsible for creating the Evidence Bundle and initiating approval. SLA: initial submission within 6 hours of release candidate being marked for production.
- Security reviewer (security): validates RBAC changes & residency; SLA: 24 business hours for non-emergency releases, 4 hours for expedited review.
- Compliance custodian (finance-legal): verifies bundle completeness for regulated customers; SLA: 3 business days.

RBAC controls and workflows:
1) Authorization model in use: entitlements are issued via entitlement-broker backed by the attribute store (authz-service).
2) Release-specific roles: release-approver, release-deployer, release-observer. All roles are timebound via just-in-time (JIT) activation; default TTL = 12 hours.
3) JIT activation: implemented through privileged-session-proxy with step-up MFA and reason capture.
4) Role review cadence: quarterly (90 days). Automated reminders created by entitlement-broker.

RBAC change procedure (numbered steps):
1. Developer opens release ticket and attaches proposed RBAC delta (YAML fragment exported from authz-service).
2. Release owner runs pre-flight checks: `redwoodctl release preflight --ticket RWD-1234` which verifies entitlement references and residency tags.
3. Security reviewer performs policy simulation in staging: `authz-sim --policy-diff ./delta.yaml --simulate 1000` and records results.
4. Upon approval, JIT activation is scheduled and deployment window reserved.

Audit logging and evidence capture:
- Audit services: audit-ingest (edge), audit-indexer (storage), audit-query (forensics).
- Minimum audit retention: 3 years for standard audit logs, 7 years for legal holds and regulated-customer artifacts.
- Log schema contract: all release-related events must include fields: event_id, actor_id, role, ticket_id, residency_tag, sha256(evidence_bundle), timestamp_iso.
- Tamper-proofing: Evidence Bundles must be signed with KMS key alias `kms-redwood/evidence`. Signatures persisted in the audit-indexer.

Evidence Bundle contents (required artifacts):
- release-manifest.json (manifest of changed services, config diffs, residency label)
- access-audit.csv (lines of access events for the deployment window)
- rbac-delta.yaml (policy fragment applied)
- pipeline-execution.log (full stdout/stderr from pipelines/redwood-release)
- residency-decision.txt (signed decision referencing customer/regulatory constraints)
- hash-sums.txt (sha256 of each file)
- signature.asc (KMS-signed manifest signature)

Evidence artifact matrix (text table):
Artifact | Source Service | Required Retention | Format
-------- | -------------- | ------------------ | ------
release-manifest.json | pipelines/redwood-release | 3y (7y if legal hold) | JSON
access-audit.csv | audit-ingest | 3y (7y) | CSV
rbac-delta.yaml | authz-service | 3y | YAML
pipeline-execution.log | CI-runner | 1y (archive on request) | text
residency-decision.txt | security + release-owner | 7y for regulated | text
hash-sums.txt | evidence-orchestrator | 7y | text
signature.asc | kms-redwood | 7y | armored PGP-like

Packaging and CLI (example):
- Create bundle: `tar -czf RWD-1234-evidence.tar.gz release-manifest.json access-audit.csv rbac-delta.yaml pipeline-execution.log residency-decision.txt hash-sums.txt`
- Sign bundle: `redwood-kms sign --alias kms-redwood/evidence --in RWD-1234-evidence.tar.gz --out signature.asc`
- Upload: `evidencectl upload --bundle RWD-1234-evidence.tar.gz --sig signature.asc --ticket RWD-1234 --residency eu-west-1`
- The evidence-orchestrator will atomically store the bundle and attach the signature and generate a sha256 that must be included in the release ticket.

Residency and retention decision process (quick checklist):
1) Identify all customer-data touchpoints changed by the release.
2) For each touchpoint, map to a residency zone (e.g., eu-west-1, us-east-1, ap-southeast-1).
3) Select retention profile: ephemeral (30d), standard-audit (3y), regulated-long (7y+).
4) If any regulated-long is selected, notify finance-legal and escalate approval.
5) Record residency-decision.txt and attach to Evidence Bundle.

Incident response integration (release-related incidents):
- If an incident occurs during or within 48 hours after deployment:
  1) Triage team pulls the Evidence Bundle SHA and all corresponding audit-indexer entries.
  2) Freeze further release-related RBAC changes until root cause assigned.
  3) For suspected exfiltration or residency violation, invoke legal hold (finance-legal) and move artifacts to immutable storage.

Exception handling and risk acceptance:
- Minor exceptions (no customer-data residency exposure): documented in ticket with owner signature and auto-archived after 90 days.
- High-risk exceptions (cross-border data, extended retention) must be entered into the Risk Register with: risk-id, owner, compensating controls, and expiration. All high-risk exceptions require executive sign-off (security + finance-legal) and generate a compliance evidence task assigned to the Compliance Custodian.

Audit checklist for reviewers (go/no-go):
- Evidence Bundle present and signature verified against kms-redwood.
- RBAC delta reviewed and simulated with no policy allow-list regression.
- Residency-decision recorded and matches infrastructure tags.
- Audit logs for deployment window are complete and contain required schema fields.
- Hashes match and signature.asc verifies correctly.

Frequently used commands and templates (copyable):
- Preflight: `redwoodctl release preflight --ticket {{TICKET}}`
- Evidence upload: `evidencectl upload --bundle {{BUNDLE}} --sig {{SIG}} --ticket {{TICKET}} --residency {{REGION}}`
- Sign: `redwood-kms sign --alias kms-redwood/evidence --in {{BUNDLE}} --out signature.asc`

Templates:
- Minimal release-manifest.json (example):
  {
    "ticket_id": "RWD-1234",
    "owner": "team-inference",
    "changed_services": ["inference-runtime", "authz-service"],
    "residency_tag": "eu-west-1",
    "retention_profile": "regulated-long"
  }

Operational notes and monitoring:
- The evidence-orchestrator pushes a completion event to the release SQS topic when bundle storage and signature validation succeeds. Pipelines will not auto-promote release until that event is received.
- Metrics to track: evidence_upload_latency (p50/p95), evidence_bundle_size_bytes (p95), signature_verification_failures (count). Alert thresholds: evidence_upload_latency.p95 > 10m for 3 consecutive releases -> page oncall-security.

Change control and roadmap items (short-term):
- Automate extraction of pipeline-execution.log segments relevant to the release diff. Target Q3 2026.
- Integrate entitlement-broker with audit-query for on-demand policy-explain for reviewers (Q4 2026).

Appendix A — Common failure modes and remediation:
- Missing access-audit.csv: verify audit-ingest is enabled for the pipeline runner; if missing, reconstruct from pipeline agent logs and mark with evidence-reconstruction tag.
- Signature mismatch: re-run signing via `redwood-kms` and check `kms-redwood` key rotation policy; if rotation occurred, escalate to security-kms.

Appendix B — Contacts and escalation:
- Security oncall: security-oncall@redwood.com (pager via Opsgenie)
- Compliance custodian: compliance@redwood.com
- Release platform (pipelines): infra-cicd@redwood.com

This manifest is authoritative for release-related access and evidence handling. Minor variations for partner-managed deployments must be documented and approved by security and compliance custodians.

- Security oncall: security-oncall@redwood.com (pager via Opsgenie)
- Compliance custodian: compliance@redwood.com
- Release platform (pipelines): infra-cicd@redwood.com

This manifest is authoritative for release-related access and evidence handling. Minor variations for partner-managed deployments must be documented and approved by security and compliance custodians.

source.title: Assurance Convergence Protocol: Controls Alignment for Auth, Logging, Residency & SDLC
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Summary
This document defines the Assurance Convergence Protocol (ACP): a single operational rubric that aligns RBAC and authentication controls, audit logging guarantees, data residency and retention schedules, secure SDLC gates, and incident-response evidence workflows. The ACP exists to reduce control gaps, accelerate audit preparation, and make exception and risk decisions traceable and reversible.

Scope
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments (Redwood API, Dedicated capacity, and Private/VPC builds).
- Covers: role/permission lifecycle, authn/authz telemetry, audit-log schema & ingestion, retention/residency mapping for customer and telemetry data, SDLC release gates that require security evidence, and incident evidence capture & handoff.
- Excludes product feature design decisions; those are covered in product PRDs but must reference the ACP when they introduce new control requirements.

Guiding principles
1) Single source of truth for control ownership and artifacts. Every control references: owner team, canonical artifact location, retention policy, and review cadence.
2) Minimum viable evidence for audits. Evidence artifacts are pragmatic: attestations, logs, manifests, and linkable references (no bulky ad-hoc exports).
3) Traceable exceptions. Any deviation uses the exception workflow with explicit mitigation and automatic expiration.
4) Recovery-first: incident evidence collection must preserve forensic fidelity while minimizing service impact.

Controls matrix (operational view)
Control | Owner | Canonical artifact(s) | Retention | Review cadence | Escalation on failure
--- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---
RBAC (role binding + approval history) | identity-team | s3://redwood-secure-artifacts/rbac/attestations/{YYYY-MM}.json, identity-db change log | 7 years for audit roles; 1 year for ephemeral roles | Quarterly automated attestation; manual if unusual changes | Pager to identity-team; escalation to Security on failed attestation
AuthN (SSO + service tokens) | identity-team | oidc-logs (Kafka topic), service-secret-rotation-manifest | 2 years for auth events; secrets rotated per policy | Monthly rotation verification | Immediate rotate + token invalidation SOP
Audit logging (ingestion + schema) | infra-sre / observability | audit-index (clickhouse/olap), immutable-s3: s3://redwood-audit/immutable/{date}.ndjson | 7 years (prod); 1 year (dev/staging) | Continuous ingestion SLA checks; schema contracts validated on deploy | Fallback to immutable-s3 + backlog reingestion; SEV2 if >5% missing
Data residency mapping (customer data tag & location) | legal-compliance / infra-sre | residency-manifest.csv, residency-mapping-api | 7 years or per contractual obligations | Annual and on-new-region deploy | Block new deployment until mapping verified
Secure SDLC gate evidence (pre-release) | eng-platform / sec-ops | pre-release-evidence bundle (tests, SCA, attestation) | 3 years (release artifacts) | Per-release gating | Release hold and security review board (SRB) for failed gates
Incident evidence capture | sec-ops | incident-evidence-manifest.json + forensic bundles in s3://redwood-forensics/{incident-id}/ | 10 years for high-severity incidents; 2 years for medium | After-action review (14 days) | Immediate forensic preservation + legal notification as required

Operational thresholds and SLA examples
- Audit log ingestion SLA: 99.9% of events indexed within 5 minutes of generation.
- Auth event retention for realtime detection: events must be queryable for 90 days with sub-10s query latency.
- RBAC attestation pass rate: 100% of owners respond to quarterly attestations; exceptions automatically auto-expire after 30 days.

How artifacts are named and stored (convention)
- Naming convention: <control>-<artifact-type>-<YYYYMM>-<short-id>.ext
  - Example: rbac-attestation-202603-identitydb.json
- Canonical storage: secure S3 (KMS-encrypted) buckets with object-lock enabled for immutable audit buckets. Buckets: redwood-secure-artifacts, redwood-audit, redwood-forensics.

Evidence manifest example (artifact-level index)
{
  "incident_id": "IR-2026-0427-01",
  "artifacts": [
    {"path": "s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/conntrack.ndjson", "type": "network-capture", "hash": "sha256:..."},
    {"path": "s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/rbac-attestation-202603.json", "type": "attestation"}
  ],
  "collected_by": "sec-ops@redwood.com",
  "collected_at": "2026-03-05T14:22:31Z"
}

Automated checks and CI integration
- Code-level gates (SDLC): every merge to main must pass security-oriented checks documented below. The pre-release-evidence bundle is produced by the CI pipeline (build artifact + SCA report + unit/integration test summary) and uploaded to the canonical artifact location.
- Example pre-release policy (yaml snippet):
policy:
  ssa_threshold: 0.0
  sca_blocking: true
  required_artifacts: ["sast-report.json","dependency-sbom.json","qa-eval-summary.json"]

Exception & risk approval workflow (operational steps)
1) Submit exception ticket in Jira component: SECURITY-EXCEPTIONS with fields: control-id, risk-rating (low/med/high), mitigation, expiration date.
2) Auto-attach required artifacts: short risk memo, compensating controls evidence, mitigation acceptance checklist.
3) Security reviews within 5 business days. For high risk: SRB convenes within 48 hours.
4) If approved, exception is timeboxed and a webhook populates enforcement rules (e.g., rate-limiting, increased monitoring) for its duration.
5) Expired exceptions auto-revoke; owners must re-request or system reverts to default control state.

Incident evidence collection: step-by-step (for sec-ops)
1) Triage: identify incident ID and severity. Assign an evidence custodian (person) in PagerDuty incident.
2) Preserve: enable object-lock and snapshot affected systems. If possible, pause log rotation and create immutable copy:
   - CLI: aws s3 cp s3://service-logs/prod/2026-03-05/ s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/ --recursive --sse aws:kms
3) Collect: gather RBAC change logs, auth events (time window +/- 30 minutes), deployment events, and model-serving runtime traces.
4) Hash & catalog: compute sha256 for each artifact and add to incident-evidence-manifest.json.
5) Share: handoff to Legal/Compliance via the secure evidence portal; include a minimal chain-of-custody statement.

Audit readiness playbook (quarterly)
1) Run artifact discovery job across canonical buckets; produce a gaps report.
2) Resolve high-priority gaps within 7 business days.
3) Perform sample replays: validate that audit logs can be reconstructed and that telemetry maps to control events.
4) Produce an audit readiness attestation signed by owners; store at s3://redwood-secure-artifacts/audit-attestations/{YYYY-MM}.json

Monitoring and alerting (examples)
- Metric: audit_ingest_lag_p50/p95/p99. Alert if p95 > 300s for more than 5 minutes (SEV2).
- Metric: rbac_attestation_response_rate. Alert if < 80% 7 days after attestation launch (SEV3).
- Metric: exception_count_by_risk. Pager duty if any high-risk exception active longer than 14 days.

Operational responsibilities (owners at a glance)
- identity-team: role lifecycle, SSO, service account policy, attestation orchestration.
- infra-sre: audit log pipeline, immutable storage, region mapping.
- sec-ops: incident evidence collection, chain-of-custody, SRB coordination.
- legal-compliance: residency mapping decisions, regulatory evidence requirements.
- eng-platform: CI gate enforcement and pre-release artifact generation.

Appendix A — Evidence templates and quick CLI snippets
- RBAC attestation retrieval (Athena):
  SELECT * FROM rbac_attestations WHERE month = '2026-03';
- Audit bucket immutable copy (example):
  aws s3api put-object-lock-configuration --bucket redwood-audit --object-lock-configuration '{"ObjectLockEnabled": "Enabled","Rule":{"DefaultRetention":{"Days":3650}}}'
- Minimal chain-of-custody text: Collector: <name> | Collected at: <timestamp> | Method: snapshot/s3-copy | Notes: <short description>

Appendix B — Frequently asked questions
Q: When should a product change trigger residency re-review?
A: Any feature that stores or indexes customer-provided data in a new region or service tier requires a residency-mapping change request and legal signoff prior to rollout.

Q: Who signs the pre-release evidence bundle?
A: The engineering release lead plus the eng-platform and sec-ops approvers must sign; legal only signs when the release affects residency or contractual controls.

Change log
- 2026-02-15: Document created (Ava Chen).
- 2026-03-10: Added CLI snippets and tightened exception webhook behavior (reviewers Miguel Alvarez, Priya Nair).

Related artifacts and links
- Canonical bucket policies: s3://redwood-secure-artifacts/policies.md
- SRB charter and membership: /confluence/security-and-compliance/risk-and-exceptions/srb-charter

source.title: Deployment Gatekeeper: Forensics & Evidence Guide
source.author: Mariana Ortiz
source.body:
Summary

This guide defines the Deployment Gatekeeper process used by Redwood Inference to ensure every production deployment includes the necessary RBAC, audit logging, data residency, and secure-SDLC evidence required for post-deployment forensics and regulatory review. The Gatekeeper is both a human-and-automation workflow: a checklist enforced in CI, a short manual signoff for high-risk changes, and a standardized evidence bundle that accompanies releases.

Scope

- Applies to all model-serving releases, control-plane changes, and customer-impacting infra updates targeting production, dedicated, or private deployments.
- Does NOT replace incident response runbooks; it provides evidence-collection and preventative verification steps pre- and post-deploy.

Definitions

- Evidence bundle: A packaged set of artifacts (audit logs, entitlement snapshots, release manifest, residency assertions) retained in the Evidence Index for the release.
- Gatekeeper: CI job + duty officer review that must pass before production rollout.

Roles & Responsibilities

- Release Owner (engineering): owns the release manifest, deployment steps, and ensures artifacts are attached to the build.
- Security Gatekeeper (security team): verifies RBAC and audit configuration, performs elevated checks for cross-region or sensitive-data releases.
- SRE/Platform (ops): validates observability and log-forwarding pipelines are healthy; triggers fallback if pipelines are degraded.
- Compliance Custodian (compliance team): spot-checks evidence bundles for regulated customers and retains final artifacts where required.

Pre-deployment Gate Criteria (CI enforced)

1) RBAC verification
   - Ensure all new or changed roles are listed in the entitlement manifest.
   - Run the entitlement-scan job: `tools/entitlement-scan --release $RELEASE --baseline $MASTER` (returns non-zero on unexpected bindings).
   - Check that no privileges are granted with wildcards for prod-tenants.

2) Audit logging configuration
   - `AUDIT_ENABLED=true` in release manifest for affected services.
   - Validate that log forwarding is configured (Audit -> Kafka -> Immutable-store).
   - Verify schema version: `audit-schema: v2.1` (see audit schema catalog).

3) Data residency assertion
   - For any dataset or model artifacts flagged `residency=eu` or `us:restricted`, include residency certificate files and KMS region.

4) Secure SDLC gates
   - PRs affecting production code must have: 2 reviewers, passing CI, and a signed-off security checklist in PR description.

5) Evidence bundle properties
   - At least the following artifacts are required in the Evidence bundle: `release-manifest.json`, `entitlement-snapshot.json`, `audit-config.yaml`, `residency-statement.txt`, `crc32` checksums for binaries.

Automated Enforcement (CI job example)

- CI job name: `gatekeeper:produce-evidence`
- Steps:
  1. run entitlement-scan -> writes `entitlement-snapshot.json`
  2. validate audit-config -> writes `audit-config.yaml`
  3. residency-checker -> writes `residency-statement.txt`
  4. package artifacts -> `tar -czf evidence-$RELEASE.tar.gz evidence/*`
  5. upload to Evidence Index with metadata tag `release:$RELEASE`

Evidence Packaging Template (table)

| Artifact name | Purpose | Owner | Retention | Format |
|---------------|---------|-------|-----------|--------|
| release-manifest.json | canonical list of images, hashes, rollout policy | Release Owner | 7 years (default) | JSON |
| entitlement-snapshot.json | RBAC bindings at deploy time | Security | 7 years* | JSON |
| audit-config.yaml | audit endpoints & schema | SRE | 7 years | YAML |
| audit-log-index-link.txt | pointer to immutable store partition | SRE | 7 years | text |
| residency-statement.txt | KMS regions and residency claims | Compliance | 7 years | text |
| build-signature.asc | cryptographic signature of release | Release Owner | 7 years | ascii-armored |

*Retention may be longer for regulated customers per contract. See Data Residency & Retention Mapping below.

Evidence Collection Procedure (ordered)

1) Pre-pack: ensure CI runs successfully and `evidence/` directory contains all required artifacts.
2) Immutable upload: upload `evidence-$RELEASE.tar.gz` to Evidence Index (S3 immutable bucket). Example: `aws s3 cp evidence-$RELEASE.tar.gz s3://evidence-index/$RELEASE/ --acl bucket-owner-full-control --tagging "release=$RELEASE"`
3) Indexing: CI calls Evidence Index API to create a record with fields: release, owner, region, residency tags, sensitivity.
4) Hash and sign: `gpg --armor --detach-sign evidence-$RELEASE.tar.gz` and store both tar and signature.

Commands & queries (forensics toolkit)

- Extract entitlement snapshot for release: `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://evidence.internal/api/v1/releases/$RELEASE/artifacts/entitlement-snapshot.json`
- Query audit store for events during rollout window (example using internal query service):
  - Query: `SELECT * FROM audit_events WHERE release = '$RELEASE' AND timestamp BETWEEN '$T0' AND '$T1';`
- Example jq filter to list principals with privilege escalations: `jq '.bindings[] | select(.permission=="admin") | {principal: .principal, role: .role}' entitlement-snapshot.json`

Data Residency & Retention Mapping (summary table)

| Residency classification | Evidence location | Retention default | Special handling |
|-------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| public | Evidence Index (global) | 3 years | No special controls |
| controlled | Evidence Index (regional partition) | 7 years | Region-located key, restricted access |
| restricted | Evidence Index (customer vault) | contract-defined (>=7y) | HSM-backed keys, manual legal hold |

Secure SDLC Integration

- The Gatekeeper CI job is integrated into release pipelines via a required `gatekeeper:approve` stage. Releases that mutate entitlements or audit pipeline configuration require a human security gate review.
- Security checklist template to be embedded in PR description: short answers to items (yes/no) with linked artifacts. Example: `RBAC changes: Yes -> entitlement-snapshot.json attached`

Incident Response: Forensic Steps after a behavior anomaly is detected

1) Triage & preserve
   - If an anomaly relates to a release window, immediately snapshot the Evidence Index record (mark with `legal-hold=true`) and export the related audit partitions.
   - Trigger `incident-evidence-freeze` playbook: `./runbooks/incident-evidence-freeze.sh --release $RELEASE --incident $INCIDENT_ID`

2) Short window queries
   - Use the Query Service to extract: entitlement-snapshot, audit events, and metrics. Archive results to a secure detention bucket.

3) RBAC containment
   - If the entitlement snapshot shows overprivilege, follow these steps:
     1. Remove offending binding in staging and run canary rollback.
     2. For production, apply a timebound revocation and create a mitigation ticket.
     3. Record the change in the Evidence bundle and append a remediation note.

4) Legal hold & external evidence sharing
   - Compliance Custodian authorizes evidence release to external auditors. All external shares must be recorded in `evidence-access.log` and redacted according to privacy rules.

Operational Alerts & SLOs

- Gatekeeper pipeline health SLOs:
  - Evidence packaging completes within 10 minutes for normal releases (90th percentile).
  - Immutable upload success rate >= 99.5% over 30 days.
  - Audit-forwarding latency under 3s for audit event ingestion (99th percentile).

Fallback & Rollback Criteria

- If evidence upload fails: block deployment and notify SRE + Security Gatekeeper. An attack vector exists if audit forwarding is broken during a rollout.
- If audit-schema validation fails: require emergency rollback and immediate remediation.

Templates & Attachments

- Minimal PR checklist (copy into a PR):
  - [ ] 2x reviewers
  - [ ] gatekeeper:produce-evidence successful
  - [ ] `entitlement-snapshot.json` attached
  - [ ] `audit-config.yaml` validated
  - [ ] residency statement included (if applicable)

- Evidence bundle filename pattern: `evidence-<service>-<release>-<YYYYMMDD>.tar.gz`

Automation Snippet (example YAML for gatekeeper job)

pipeline:
  name: gatekeeper
  jobs:
    - name: produce-evidence
      image: infra/tools:stable
      steps:
        - run: entitlement-scan --release $RELEASE --out evidence/entitlement-snapshot.json
        - run: audit-config-validate ./audit-config.yaml
        - run: residency-checker --release $RELEASE --out evidence/residency-statement.txt
        - run: tar -czf evidence-$RELEASE.tar.gz evidence/*
        - run: aws s3 cp evidence-$RELEASE.tar.gz s3://evidence-index/$RELEASE/ --tagging "release=$RELEASE"

FAQ

Q: Who can unfreeze an Evidence Index record?
A: Only the Security Gatekeeper or Compliance Custodian can lift `legal-hold` flags after agreement with Legal. All unfreeze events are audited.

Q: What if a third-party plugin prevents audit-forwarding?
A: Treat as a high-severity SRE incident; the deployment gate should block automatically until forwarding is restored or routed to a fallback collector.

Related pages & references

- See also: entitlement provisioning, secure SDLC release gates, and forensic log retention policies (links in the metadata).

Appendix: Quick evidence checklist (one-liner version for on-call)

- RBAC snapshot present; Audit forwarding healthy; Residency declared; Evidence archive uploaded and signed; Gatekeeper CI green.

End of document

title: Deployment Gatekeeper: Forensics & Evidence Guide
preview:
Summary

This guide defines the Deployment Gatekeeper process used by Redwood Inference to ensure every production deployment includes the necessary RBAC, audit logging, data residency, and secure-SDLC evidence required for post-deployment forensics and regulatory review. The Gatekeeper is both a human-and-automation workflow: a checklist enforced in CI, a short manual signoff for high-risk changes, and a standardized evidence bundle that accompanies releases.

Scope

- Applies to all model-serving releases, control-plane changes, and customer-impacting infra updates targeting production, dedicated, or private deployments.
- Does NOT replace incident response runbooks; it provides evidence-collection and preventative verification steps pre- and post-deploy.

Definitions

- Evidence bundle: A packaged set of artifacts (audit logs, entitlement snapshots, release manifest, residency assertions) retained in the Evidence Index for the release.
- Gatekeeper: CI job + duty officer review that must pass before production rollout.

Roles & Responsibilities

- Release Owner (engineering): owns the release manifest, deployment steps, and ensures artifacts are attached to the build.
- Security Gatekeeper (security team): verifies RBAC and audit configuration, performs elevated checks for cross-region or sensitive-data releases.
- SRE/Platform (ops): validates observability and log-forwarding pipelines are healthy; triggers fallback if pipelines are degraded.
- Compliance Custodian (compliance team): spot-checks evidence bundles for regulated customers and retains final artifacts where required.

Pre-deployment Gate Criteria (CI enforced)

1) RBAC verification
   - Ensure all new or changed roles are listed in the entitlement manifest.
   - Run the entitlement-scan job: `tools/entitlement-scan --release $RELEASE --baseline $MASTER` (returns non-zero on unexpected bindings).
   - Check that no privileges are granted with wildcards for prod-tenants.

2) Audit logging configuration
   - `AUDIT_ENABLED=true` in release manifest for affected services.
   - Validate that log forwarding is configured (Audit -> Kafka -> Immutable-store).
   - Verify schema version: `audit-schema: v2.1` (see audit schema catalog).

3) Data residency assertion
   - For any dataset or model artifacts flagged `residency=eu` or `us:restricted`, include residency certificate files and KMS region.

4) Secure SDLC gates
   - PRs affecting production code must have: 2 reviewers, passing CI, and a signed-off security checklist in PR description.

5) Evidence bundle properties
   - At least the following artifacts are required in the Evidence bundle: `release-manifest.json`, `entitlement-snapshot.json`, `audit-config.yaml`, `residency-statement.txt`, `crc32` checksums for binaries.

source.title: Model Exfiltration: Evidence Capture, RBAC Containment, and Compliance Packaging
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Overview:

This document defines the standard operating procedure for responding to suspected or confirmed model exfiltration incidents involving Redwood-hosted inference systems. It combines RBAC containment controls, audit log evidence capture queries, data-residency and retention considerations, secure-SDLC remediation guidance, and the compliance packaging checklist required for regulatory/enterprise customers.

Scope and intent:
- Applies to all incidents where model internals, embeddings, prompt corpora, or customer-provided data may have been exfiltrated from any Redwood environment (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Focus is on rapid containment, defensible forensic evidence capture, and creating a reproducible compliance evidence package for audits.

Goals:
1. Stop ongoing exfiltration within 15 minutes of detection where possible.
2. Preserve forensic evidence with documented chain-of-custody.
3. Limit blast radius via RBAC and credential containment.
4. Produce a compliance artifact that supports SOC2/ISO/GDPR inquiries.

Threat model (concise):
- Insider misuse of privileged machine/service accounts.
- API key leakage used to drive high-rate queries and streaming token capture.
- Side-channel or model extraction via repeated adaptive queries.
- Compromise of a private deployment (customer-managed) that can be used to move artifacts to unauthorised locations.

Ownership and responders:
- Incident Commander (IC): Security Oncall (primary).
- Forensics Lead: Security Engineering.
- Service Owner(s): Runtime / Serving (if hosted) or Customer Success for private deploys.
- Legal & Privacy: notified immediately for potential data subject impact.
- Communications: Customer Success and PR as required.

Priority classification:
- P0: Confirmed exfiltration of customer PII or model weights/secret prompt corpora.
- P1: Strong indicators of extraction attempts (high similarity outputs, repeated boundary probing).
- P2: Unusual query patterns without clear exfil evidence.

Immediate triage checklist (first 15 minutes):
1. IC logs timeline and assigns Forensics Lead.
2. Snapshot current access tokens and session table. Note: do NOT revoke yet if live forensic capture is required—coordinate with Forensics Lead.
3. Temporarily throttle or rate-limit suspected API keys/routes.
4. Place affected model endpoints into maintenance mode (deny external requests) if P0/P1.
5. Preserve volatile state: export KV caches, current LRU for KV cache, last-10K request traces for the model shard.

Containment actions (RBAC-focused):
- Immediate actions (if P0):
  1. Revoke all session tokens associated with implicated service identities and API keys via central auth service.
  2. Disable short-lived service principals and mark for rotation (mark "rotating=true" in identity registry).
  3. Apply a temporary deny policy on the model-serving role for all non-essential principals:
     - Example deny policy (pseudo-YAML):
       deny: true
       principal_tags: ["external", "service-account", "developer"]
       resources: ["/models/*/serve", "/models/*/stream"]
       timeframe: "T+0 to T+72h"
- Isolation for private deployments: instruct customer to disable external egress in VPC and snapshot the deployment. Provide step-by-step commands (see "Private deploy containment" below).

Audit log evidence capture (guidance and example queries):
- Preserve immutable audit streams first: forward the affected time window to evidence-bucket with write-once retention (see audit retention policy).
- Minimum evidence fields to capture per request:
  - timestamp, request_id, model_id, route, principal_id, ip_address, user_agent, input_length, output_length, tokens_generated, response_sample_hash, parent_request_id

- Example Splunk/Kusto queries (adapt to platform):
  1) High-rate callers in window:
   index=redwood_audit sourcetype=requests earliest="-1h" | stats count by principal_id | where count > 10000
  2) Streams with large outputs:
   index=redwood_audit route="/stream*" | where tokens_generated > 10000 | table timestamp, principal_id, model_id, tokens_generated
  3) Similarity clustering (post-hoc):
   index=redwood_audit | eval sample_hash=sha256(substr(response,0,4096)) | stats count by sample_hash | where count > 3

- Immutable evidence export example (CLI):
  aws s3 cp s3://audit-stream/2026-02-12/window-0001 nds-case-1234/ --storage-class DEEP_ARCHIVE --metadata chain-of-custody=sealed

Forensic capture checklist (ASCII table):
| Artifact type | Recommended action | Retention label | Notes |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|-------|
| API request traces | Export full JSON payloads for suspected window | evidence:primary | Include parent_request_id links |
| Streaming chunks | Capture earliest and latest 1MB per stream | evidence:primary | Chunk ordering matters |
| KV cache snapshot | Dump hot KV namespaces | evidence:secondary | May contain derived embeddings |
| Service credentials | Export credential issuance logs | evidence:primary | Include rotation events |
| Network egress logs | Capture flow logs + destination IPs | evidence:primary | Correlate with S3/ftp targets |

Data residency & retention considerations:
- If evidence contains EU personal data, keep evidence within EU-bound storage only unless Legal approves cross-border transfer. Mark exports with residency tag: residency=eu|na|apac.
- Evidence retention for compliance packaging:
  - Primary evidence: retain 7 years encrypted (for high-sensitivity incidents) or at least 1 year for lower severity.
  - Secondary artifacts: retain 1 year unless requested otherwise.
  - All retention decisions must be logged in the incident ticket and approved by Legal.

Private deploy containment (customer instructions snippet):
1) SSH into management node: ssh ops@<customer-vpn-ip>
2) sudo iptables -I OUTPUT -m conntrack --ctstate NEW -j DROP  # blocks new external connections
3) sudo systemctl stop redwood-agent.service
4) Snapshot /var/lib/redwood/models and upload to customer-provided S3 with immutable flags

Secure SDLC & remediation steps after containment:
1. Triage root cause: credential leakage, model attack pattern, or config issue.
2. Create a scoped fix PR with: code changes, unit tests for input sanitization/rate-limits, and regression prompt-set evals (see prompt-sets-and-benchmarks).
3. Require mandatory security review and replay of the incident pattern in non-prod for validation.
4. Backport fixes to any maintained runtime branches.
5. Extend CI to include an automated exfiltration smoke-test (detect high-output response patterns).

Compliance evidence packaging (deliverable checklist):
- Cover letter: incident timeline, summary, impact assessment, decision log.
- Chain-of-custody manifest: who exported which artifact, when, target storage.
- Artifact index: CRC/sha256 checksums and storage URIs for every exported file.
- RBAC audit: list of principals revoked/rotated and justification for each.
- Legal sign-off: redacted data subject notifications and regulator contacts if applicable.
- Technical appendix: set of Splunk/Kusto queries used, sample outputs, and threat-model mapping.

Risk and exceptions governance (temporary elevated access):
- Any emergency elevation for triage must be approved by: IC + Forensics Lead + One Legal approver. Approval must be recorded in the ticket with a start/expiry time (max 72 hours).
- All temporary grants must be short-lived credentials (max TTL 1 hour) and tied to a unique justification tag.
- Post-incident, conduct privileged access attestation and rotate any keys used by elevated users.

Post-incident review (30/60/90-day cadence):
1. 72-hour hot debrief: capture immediate lessons and patch rollout plan.
2. 30-day technical retrospective: attackers path, detection gaps, automation opportunities.
3. 90-day risk verification: confirm all artifacts retained per retention schedule, and that recommended controls are in place and tested.

Detection metrics and KPIs to track post-incident:
- Mean time to contain (goal: < 15m for P0).
- Number of compromised principals rotated.
- Percentage of evidence exports that meet "immovable" (immutable, checksummed)标准.
- Number of code/CI changes deployed to reduce extraction risk.

Appendices:
A. Example deny policy JSON (authz service):
{
  "policy_id": "deny-model-serve-temporary-2026-02-12",
  "effect": "deny",
  "resources": ["arn:redwood:model:*/serve"],
  "principals": ["*"],
  "conditions": {"time_window": "2026-02-12T15:00Z/2026-02-15T15:00Z"}
}

B. Quick forensics command snippets:
- Export last 1000 requests for model m-123:
  curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://audit-api.internal/v1/requests?model_id=m-123&limit=1000" > m-123-window.json
- Hash a file for chain-of-custody:
  sha256sum m-123-window.json > m-123-window.json.sha256

C. Contacts and escalation matrix:
- Security oncall: security-oncall@redwood.ai (Pager)
- Forensics team: se-forensics@redwood.ai
- Legal counsel (privacy): legal-privacy@redwood.ai
- Customer success escalation lead: cs-escalation@redwood.ai

Document change log:
- 2026-02-12: Created by Ava Chen.
- 2026-03-02: Expanded containment controls and added private deploy instructions (reviewers: Diego Morales, Priya Nair).

title: Model Exfiltration: Evidence Capture, RBAC Containment, and Compliance Packaging
preview:
Overview:

This document defines the standard operating procedure for responding to suspected or confirmed model exfiltration incidents involving Redwood-hosted inference systems. It combines RBAC containment controls, audit log evidence capture queries, data-residency and retention considerations, secure-SDLC remediation guidance, and the compliance packaging checklist required for regulatory/enterprise customers.

Scope and intent:
- Applies to all incidents where model internals, embeddings, prompt corpora, or customer-provided data may have been exfiltrated from any Redwood environment (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Focus is on rapid containment, defensible forensic evidence capture, and creating a reproducible compliance evidence package for audits.

Goals:
1. Stop ongoing exfiltration within 15 minutes of detection where possible.
2. Preserve forensic evidence with documented chain-of-custody.
3. Limit blast radius via RBAC and credential containment.
4. Produce a compliance artifact that supports SOC2/ISO/GDPR inquiries.

Threat model (concise):
- Insider misuse of privileged machine/service accounts.
- API key leakage used to drive high-rate queries and streaming token capture.
- Side-channel or model extraction via repeated adaptive queries.
- Compromise of a private deployment (customer-managed) that can be used to move artifacts to unauthorised locations.

Ownership and responders:
- Incident Commander (IC): Security Oncall (primary).
- Forensics Lead: Security Engineering.
- Service Owner(s): Runtime / Serving (if hosted) or Customer Success for private deploys.
- Legal & Privacy: notified immediately for potential data subject impact.
- Communications: Customer Success and PR as required.

Priority classification:
- P0: Confirmed exfiltration of customer PII or model weights/secret prompt corpora.
- P1: Strong indicators of extraction attempts (high similarity outputs, repeated boundary probing).
- P2: Unusual query patterns without clear exfil evidence.

Immediate triage checklist (first 15 minutes):
1. IC logs timeline and assigns Forensics Lead.
2. Snapshot current access tokens and session table. Note: do NOT revoke yet if live forensic capture is required—coordinate with Forensics Lead.
3. Temporarily throttle or rate-limit suspected API keys/routes.
4. Place affected model endpoints into maintenance mode (deny external requests) if P0/P1.
5. Preserve volatile state: export KV caches, current LRU for KV cache, last-10K request traces for the model shard.

Containment actions (RBAC-focused):
- Immediate actions (if P0):
  1. Revoke all session tokens associated with implicated service identities and API keys via central auth service.
  2. Disable short-lived service principals and mark for rotation (mark \"rotating=true\" in identity registry).
  3. Apply a temporary deny policy on the model-serving role for all non-essential principals:
     - Example deny policy (pseudo-YAML):
       deny: true
       principal_tags: [\"external\", \"service-account\", \"developer\"]
       resources: [\"/models/*/serve\", \"/models/*/stream\"]
       timeframe: \"T+0 to T+72h\"
- Isolation for private deployments: instruct customer to disable external egress in VPC and snapshot the deployment. Provide step-by-step commands (see \"Private deploy containment\" below).

Audit log evidence capture (guidance and example queries):
- Preserve immutable audit streams first: forward the affected time window to evidence-bucket with write-once retention (see audit retention policy).
- Minimum evidence fields to capture per request:
  - timestamp, request_id, model_id, route, principal_id, ip_address, user_agent, input_length, output_length, tokens_generated, response_sample_hash, parent_request_id

- Example Splunk/Kusto queries (adapt to platform):
  1) High-rate callers in window:
   index=redwood_audit sourcetype=requests earliest=\"-1h\" | stats count by principal_id | where count > 10000
  2) Streams with large outputs:
   index=redwood_audit route=\"/stream*\" | where tokens_generated > 10000 | table timestamp, principal_id, model_id, tokens_generated
  3) Similarity clustering (post-hoc):
   index=redwood_audit | eval sample_hash=sha256(substr(response,0,4096)) | stats count by sample_hash | where count > 3

- Immutable evidence export example (CLI):
  aws s3 cp s3://audit-stream/2026-02-12/window-0001 nds-case-1234/ --storage-class DEEP_ARCHIVE --metadata chain-of-custody=sealed

Forensic capture checklist (ASCII table):
| Artifact type | Recommended action | Retention label | Notes |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|-------|
| API request traces | Export full JSON payloads for suspected window | evidence:primary | Include parent_request_id links |
| Streaming chunks | Capture earliest and latest 1MB per stream | evidence:primary | Chunk ordering matters |
| KV cache snapshot | Dump hot KV namespaces | evidence:secondary | May contain derived embeddings |
| Service credentials | Export credential issuance logs | evidence:primary | Include rotation events |
| Network egress logs | Capture flow logs + destination IPs | evidence:primary | Correlate with S3/ftp targets |

Data residency & retention considerations:
- If evidence contains EU personal data, keep evidence within EU-bound storage only unless Legal approves cross-border transfer. Mark exports with residency tag: residency=eu|na|apac.
- Evidence retention for compliance packaging:
  - Primary evidence: retain 7 years encrypted (for high-sensitivity incidents) or at least 1 year for lower severity.
  - Secondary artifacts: retain 1 year unless requested otherwise.
  - All retention decisions must be logged in the incident ticket and approved by Legal.

Private deploy containment (customer instructions snippet):
1) SSH into management node: ssh ops@<customer-vpn-ip>
2) sudo iptables -I OUTPUT -m conntrack --ctstate NEW -j DROP  # blocks new external connections
3) sudo systemctl stop redwood-agent.service
4) Snapshot /var/lib/redwood/models and upload to customer-provided S3 with immutable flags

Secure SDLC & remediation steps after containment:
1. Triage root cause: credential leakage, model attack pattern, or config issue.
2. Create a scoped fix PR with: code changes, unit tests for input sanitization/rate-limits, and regression prompt-set evals (see prompt-sets-and-benchmarks).
3. Require mandatory security review and replay of the incident pattern in non-prod for validation.
4. Backport fixes to any maintained runtime branches.
5. Extend CI to include an automated exfiltration smoke-test (detect high-output response patterns).

Compliance evidence packaging (deliverable checklist):
- Cover letter: incident timeline, summary, impact assessment, decision log.
- Chain-of-custody manifest: who exported which artifact, when, target storage.
- Artifact index: CRC/sha256 checksums and storage URIs for every exported file.
- RBAC audit: list of principals revoked/rotated and justification for each.
- Legal sign-off: redacted data subject notifications and regulator contacts if applicable.
- Technical appendix: set of Splunk/Kusto queries used, sample outputs, and threat-model mapping.

Risk and exceptions governance (temporary elevated access):
- Any emergency elevation for triage must be approved by: IC + Forensics Lead + One Legal approver. Approval must be recorded in the ticket with a start/expiry time (max 72 hours).
- All temporary grants must be short-lived credentials (max TTL 1 hour) and tied to a unique justification tag.
- Post-incident, conduct privileged access attestation and rotate any keys used by elevated users.

Post-incident review (30/60/90-day cadence):
1. 72-hour hot debrief: capture immediate lessons and patch rollout plan.
2. 30-day technical retrospective: attackers path, detection gaps, automation opportunities.
3. 90-day risk verification: confirm all artifacts retained per retention schedule, and that recommended controls are in place and tested.

Detection metrics and KPIs to track post-incident:
- Mean time to contain (goal: < 15m for P0).
- Number of compromised principals rotated.
- Percentage of evidence exports that meet \"immovable\" (immutable, checksummed)标准.
- Number of code/CI changes deployed to reduce extraction risk.

Appendices:
A. Example deny policy JSON (authz service):
{
  \"policy_id\": \"deny-model-serve-temporary-2026-02-12\",
  \"effect\": \"deny\",
  \"resources\": [\"arn:redwood:model:*/serve\"],
  \"principals\": [\"*\"],
  \"conditions\": {\"time_window\": \"2026-02-12T15:00Z/2026-02-15T15:00Z\"}
}

B. Quick forensics command snippets:
- Export last 1000 requests for model m-123:
  curl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\" \"https://audit-api.internal/v1/requests?model_id=m-123&limit=1000\" > m-123-window.json
- Hash a file for chain-of-custody:
  sha256sum m-123-window.json > m-123-window.json.sha256

C. Contacts and escalation matrix:
- Security oncall: security-oncall@redwood.ai (Pager)
- Forensics team: se-forensics@redwood.ai
- Legal counsel (privacy): legal-privacy@redwood.ai
- Customer success escalation lead: cs-escalation@redwood.ai

Document change log:
- 2026-02-12: Created by Ava Chen.
- 2026-03-02: Expanded containment controls and added private deploy instructions (reviewers: Diego Morales, Priya Nair).
