Approved position:
- Do not claim “HIPAA compliant” as a blanket statement.
- Any BAA request must be routed to Legal and Security.
Approved language:
“Redwood can discuss support for regulated workloads and applicable deployment options. If a BAA is required, Redwood will review and respond through Legal.”

### 3) Pen test frequency and sharing reports
Approved position:
- Do not disclose detailed schedules publicly.
- Do not upload pen test reports into portals without NDA and explicit approval.
Approved language:
“Redwood performs regular security testing, including third-party assessments. Reports may be available under NDA through our evidence request process; distribution is limited.”

### 4) Vulnerability management (scan cadence, patch SLAs)
Approved position:
- Avoid hard time-bound remediation commitments unless contractually approved.
Approved language:
“Redwood operates a vulnerability management program that includes continuous monitoring and prioritized remediation based on severity and exposure. We can provide program-level details under NDA.”

### 5) Data residency guarantees
Approved position:
- Never guarantee residency beyond documented region availability and the customer’s configuration.
- Ensure Hosted vs Private differences are explicit.
Approved language:
“Data residency depends on the deployment mode and region configuration. Redwood supports regional deployments and can describe data flows and storage locations for the selected region. If strict residency is required, Dedicated or Private deployment options may be recommended.”

### 6) Retention and deletion requirements
Approved position:
- Use the customer-facing retention overview as the baseline.
- Do not promise custom deletion timelines without confirmation.
Approved language:
“Retention and deletion controls vary by data type and deployment mode. Redwood provides documented retention and deletion behavior and can discuss configuration options for logs and observability data depending on plan and deployment.”

### 7) “Do you train models on our data?”
Approved position:
- Use the Security FAQ’s approved definition and avoid ambiguous phrasing.
Approved language:
“Customer data submitted for inference is not used to train Redwood-provided models by default. We can provide additional details on data handling and any optional programs under separate agreement, if applicable.”

### 8) Audit logging requirements (fields, export, retention)
Approved position:
- Do not claim event types or export destinations not documented.
Approved language:
“Redwood provides audit logging for key administrative and access events. Export and retention options depend on deployment mode and configuration. We can share the current event taxonomy and export options referenced in our audit logging overview.”

## Prohibited language (do not use)
The following statements are not allowed in customer questionnaires unless Legal/GRC explicitly approves for a specific deal:
- “We guarantee…” (unless it is a contractual SLA in an executed agreement)
- “We are ISO 27001 certified” (unless GRC confirms active certification and scope)
- “We are HIPAA compliant” / “HIPAA certified”
- “We will support X by [date]” (roadmap commitment)
- “We never store any customer data” (overbroad; storage/processing depends on data type and configuration)
- “Customer data is deleted immediately” (unless documented behavior and configuration support it)

## Records and auditability
GRC maintains an exceptions log capturing:
- Account and date
- Questionnaire question text
- Final approved response text
- Severity level
- Approvers
- Any evidence shared and channel used
- Expiration/review date (if applicable)

Exception approvals must be retained for at least 24 months for internal auditability and to support consistent future responses.

## Review cadence and ownership
- Owner: GRC (Olga Petrov)
- Review cadence: Quarterly, and additionally after any material product changes affecting security posture (auth/RBAC, audit logging, data retention/residency, encryption/KMS, Private deployment packaging).
- Proposed changes are reviewed by Security Engineering (Marcus Lin), Product Security PM (Naomi Feldman), and Legal (Michael Grant) for any language with contractual risk.

## Questions / escalation contacts
- Primary: GRC (Olga Petrov) for process, evidence, and compliance language
- Technical: Security Engineering (Marcus Lin) for control feasibility and boundaries
- Capability alignment: Product Security PM (Naomi Feldman)
- Contractual terms: Legal (Michael Grant)
- Executive escalation: Dr. Aisha Rahman (CISO) for S3/high-risk exceptions

## Changelog
- 2026-02-10: Clarified severity matrix; added explicit prohibition on roadmap language; expanded approved alternatives for residency/retention and audit logging; aligned approval steps with RFP response ownership SOP.
- 2025-11-04: Added expedite path and evidence-sharing guardrails.
- 2025-09-18: Initial publication.
text:
## Purpose
This policy defines how Redwood Inference responds when a customer security questionnaire (including RFPs, vendor risk tools, and third-party platforms) asks for a control, certification, contract term, or product capability that Redwood does not support, cannot verify, or cannot commit to.

The goals are to:
- Provide a consistent, accurate response process across Sales, Solutions Engineering (SE), Security Engineering, GRC, and Legal.
- Prevent over-commitment (e.g., implying certifications, features, or SLAs that do not exist).
- Offer approved alternative language that is truthful, competitive-safe, and aligned to current product capabilities across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.
- Ensure all deviations from standard language are reviewed, approved, and traceable.

## Scope
This policy applies to:
- Customer security questionnaires and RFP security sections (any format: spreadsheets, portals, PDFs).
- Security addendums and procurement “security schedule” documents when they include control claims.
- Written responses sent via email, portal comments, or uploaded attachments.

Out of scope:
- Product roadmap commitments (handled via Product/PM processes; do not include in questionnaire responses).
- Customer-specific contractual negotiations (handled by Legal/Finance; this policy covers only the security/control content and exception handling path).

## Definitions
**Standard answer**: Pre-approved language in the Security FAQ / RFP library that can be used without additional review.

**Exception**: Any response that:
- Contradicts a standard answer, or
- Introduces new claims, new scope, or new commitments, or
- Answers “Yes” when Redwood’s current capability is “No/Partial/Not applicable,” or
- Provides evidence not listed in the evidence request playbook.

**Non-supported control**: A requested control/capability that Redwood does not provide today (e.g., customer requests “customer-managed keys for Hosted” when not available) or that is not applicable (e.g., “on-prem air-gapped install” when not offered).

**Approved alternative language**: A standardized way to answer “No/Partial” while describing current controls, compensating controls, or deployment-mode options.

## Policy statement (non-negotiables)
1) We must not misrepresent certifications or compliance status.
- Do not claim “ISO 27001 certified” unless Redwood has a current certificate and scope explicitly covers the relevant services.
- Do not claim “HIPAA compliant” or “PCI compliant” as a blanket statement; use approved, qualified language.

2) We must not promise features, SLAs, audit outcomes, timelines, or roadmap items in questionnaire responses.
- If a question requires a future-state commitment, respond with “Not currently supported” plus approved alternative language and (if appropriate) invite a follow-up security review call.

3) Evidence sharing is controlled.
- Only provide artifacts via the SOC 2 / evidence request process.
- Do not upload restricted artifacts into third-party portals without confirming NDA and approved distribution.

4) Any deviation from standard language requires approval per this policy.

## Roles and responsibilities
**Sales / Account Executive (AE)**
- Owns customer timeline, portal access, and delivery of final questionnaire.
- Ensures the latest standard answers are used.
- Initiates an Exception Request when needed (see workflow).

**Solutions Engineering (SE)**
- Drafts technical responses using standard language.
- Flags ambiguous or capability-sensitive questions (logging, retention, encryption, SSO/RBAC, Private deployment boundaries).

**GRC (Owner: Olga Petrov; backup: Daniel Carter)**
- Owns this policy and approves compliance/control assertions.
- Validates that requested evidence is available and shareable per process.
- Maintains the exception log and the approved alternative language library.

**Security Engineering (Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Hanae Suzuki)**
- Validates technical accuracy and shared responsibility boundaries.
- Confirms whether a control is supported, partially supported, or not supported in specific deployment modes.

**Product Security PM (Naomi Feldman)**
- Confirms alignment to current product capability; blocks any roadmap commitments.
- Coordinates with Product/Engineering when questionnaire language risks mischaracterizing the platform.

**Legal (Michael Grant) / Commercial Terms (Sofia Mendes)**
- Approves any language that creates contractual obligations (e.g., breach notification times, audit rights, indemnities, flow-down terms).
- Reviews deviations that may be interpreted as contractual commitments even when placed in a questionnaire.

**Executive sponsor / escalation (Dr. Aisha Rahman, CISO)**
- Final approver for high-risk exceptions and material claims.

## When to file an Exception Request
File an Exception Request when any of the following apply:
- The only truthful answer is “No” but the account team believes “No” may block procurement and needs compensating language.
- The question is ambiguous and could be interpreted as a contractual promise (examples: “guarantee,” “will,” “shall,” “commit,” “ensure”).
- The question asks about:
  - Certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI, HIPAA/BAA)
  - Pen testing frequency and report sharing
  - Vulnerability scanning cadence and results
  - Data residency guarantees
  - Data retention defaults and deletion timelines
  - Audit logging specifics and retention/export
  - Encryption/KMS key ownership boundaries
  - Subprocessor management terms
  - BCP/DR RTO/RPO commitments
  - Breach notification timeframes
- The response requires attaching evidence not listed in the evidence request playbook.

If none of the above apply and a standard answer exists, use the standard answer and proceed.

## Exception severity and approval matrix
Use the highest applicable severity.

### Severity 0 (S0) — Standard
Criteria:
- Matches approved standard answer verbatim.
- No new evidence.
Approval:
- No additional approval beyond SE/AE normal process.
SLA:
- Same business day.

### Severity 1 (S1) — Low risk clarification
Criteria:
- Minor clarification that does not change meaning or scope.
- No contractual language.
Approval:
- GRC OR Security Engineering.
SLA:
- 1 business day.

### Severity 2 (S2) — Material security posture claim
Criteria:
- Any new control assertion, qualified claim, or deployment-mode-specific nuance that could be relied upon.
- Any “Partial” answer that needs careful boundary setting.
Approval:
- GRC + Security Engineering + Product Security PM.
SLA:
- 2 business days.

### Severity 3 (S3) — High risk / contractual / regulated
Criteria:
- Contract-like commitments (e.g., breach notification window, audit rights, RTO/RPO), regulated workload claims (HIPAA/BAA), certification language, or customer requests for restricted evidence.
Approval:
- GRC + Security Engineering + Product Security PM + Legal; CISO approval required if:
  - The exception materially changes a previously stated position, OR
  - The customer insists on non-standard wording that increases risk.
SLA:
- 3 business days (expedite path available).

## Workflow: How to request and approve an exception
### Step 1 — Identify and tag the exception
AE or SE identifies the question and:
- Copies the exact question text (including any definitions) into the Exception Request.
- Notes the deployment mode in scope (Hosted / Dedicated / Private) and region constraints.
- Proposes a draft response (or marks “Need proposed language”).
- Adds the customer due date and portal submission constraints.

### Step 2 — Create an Exception Request ticket
Create a ticket in the “Security Questionnaire Exceptions” intake channel (preferred) or email GRC if intake tooling is unavailable.
Required fields:
- Account name and deal stage
- Due date/time zone
- Deployment mode(s)
- The question text and the customer’s required answer format
- Proposed response (if any)
- Requested evidence (if any)
- Business impact if unanswered / if answered “No”

### Step 3 — Triage (GRC)
GRC assigns severity (S0–S3) and routes reviewers:
- Security Engineering for technical validation
- Product Security PM for capability alignment
- Legal for contractual impact

### Step 4 — Review and redlines
Reviewers provide:
- Approved final language (copy/paste ready)
- Any required qualifiers (deployment-mode specifics)
- Any evidence gating requirements (NDA, distribution limits)

### Step 5 — Approval and logging
GRC records:
- Final approved language
- Approvers
- Any caveats and time-bounds (e.g., “valid as of 2026-02-10”)

### Step 6 — Customer submission
AE/SE submits the approved response exactly as provided.
If the portal forces incompatible formats (e.g., dropdown “Yes/No”), do not force “Yes” to fit the portal. Use “No” and add the approved explanation in comments, or select “Partial” if available.

### Step 7 — Post-mortem and library update
If an exception repeats across accounts, GRC updates the RFP response library / FAQ standard answers and notes the change in the next review cycle.

## Expedite path (urgent deal deadlines)
If the customer due date is < 24 hours:
- AE marks the request “URGENT” and provides the portal link and exact submission deadline.
- GRC may convene a 15-minute async approval (Slack + doc) with Security Engineering + Product Security PM.
- Legal review is still required for S3 items; if Legal is unavailable, the response must use the conservative standard language.

## Approved alternative language (copy/paste templates)
Use these templates when answering “No/Partial” for non-supported controls. Do not modify meaning without approval.

### Template A — Not currently supported (capability)
“Not currently supported. Redwood Inference does not provide [requested capability] in [deployment mode]. We can offer [closest supported control/capability] and can discuss architecture options (e.g., Dedicated or Private deployment) to meet the underlying requirement.”

### Template B — Partial support (deployment-mode qualifier)
“Partially supported, depending on deployment mode. For Hosted: [Hosted statement]. For Dedicated: [Dedicated statement]. For Private: [Private statement]. We can review requirements and recommend the deployment model that best meets your control objectives.”

### Template C — Evidence available under NDA
“We can provide [artifact name] under NDA via Redwood’s evidence request process. Please confirm an executed NDA (or request one), and we will share the artifact through the approved channel.”

### Template D — Control is customer responsibility (shared responsibility)
“This control is primarily the customer’s responsibility in [deployment mode]. Redwood provides the following supporting controls: [list]. We can share our shared responsibility model details upon request.”

### Template E — Contractual terms routed to Legal
“Please refer to the applicable agreement and security addendum for contractual commitments. We can provide Redwood’s standard position via Legal review.”

## High-frequency exception topics and approved positions
### 1) ISO 27001 / “ISO compliant”
Approved position:
- Do not claim certification unless explicitly confirmed by GRC for the current period and scope.
- Use qualified alignment language if applicable.
Approved language:
“Redwood maintains a security program aligned to common industry frameworks. If you require ISO 27001 certification, we can discuss our current posture and provide available assurance artifacts through our evidence process.”

### 2) HIPAA / BAA
Approved position:
- Do not claim “HIPAA compliant” as a blanket statement.
- Any BAA request must be routed to Legal and Security.
Approved language:
“Redwood can discuss support for regulated workloads and applicable deployment options. If a BAA is required, Redwood will review and respond through Legal.”

### 3) Pen test frequency and sharing reports
Approved position:
- Do not disclose detailed schedules publicly.
- Do not upload pen test reports into portals without NDA and explicit approval.
Approved language:
“Redwood performs regular security testing, including third-party assessments. Reports may be available under NDA through our evidence request process; distribution is limited.”

### 4) Vulnerability management (scan cadence, patch SLAs)
Approved position:
- Avoid hard time-bound remediation commitments unless contractually approved.
Approved language:
“Redwood operates a vulnerability management program that includes continuous monitoring and prioritized remediation based on severity and exposure. We can provide program-level details under NDA.”

### 5) Data residency guarantees
Approved position:
- Never guarantee residency beyond documented region availability and the customer’s configuration.
- Ensure Hosted vs Private differences are explicit.
Approved language:
“Data residency depends on the deployment mode and region configuration. Redwood supports regional deployments and can describe data flows and storage locations for the selected region. If strict residency is required, Dedicated or Private deployment options may be recommended.”

### 6) Retention and deletion requirements
Approved position:
- Use the customer-facing retention overview as the baseline.
- Do not promise custom deletion timelines without confirmation.
Approved language:
“Retention and deletion controls vary by data type and deployment mode. Redwood provides documented retention and deletion behavior and can discuss configuration options for logs and observability data depending on plan and deployment.”

### 7) “Do you train models on our data?”
Approved position:
- Use the Security FAQ’s approved definition and avoid ambiguous phrasing.
Approved language:
“Customer data submitted for inference is not used to train Redwood-provided models by default. We can provide additional details on data handling and any optional programs under separate agreement, if applicable.”

### 8) Audit logging requirements (fields, export, retention)
Approved position:
- Do not claim event types or export destinations not documented.
Approved language:
“Redwood provides audit logging for key administrative and access events. Export and retention options depend on deployment mode and configuration. We can share the current event taxonomy and export options referenced in our audit logging overview.”

## Prohibited language (do not use)
The following statements are not allowed in customer questionnaires unless Legal/GRC explicitly approves for a specific deal:
- “We guarantee…” (unless it is a contractual SLA in an executed agreement)
- “We are ISO 27001 certified” (unless GRC confirms active certification and scope)
- “We are HIPAA compliant” / “HIPAA certified”
- “We will support X by [date]” (roadmap commitment)
- “We never store any customer data” (overbroad; storage/processing depends on data type and configuration)
- “Customer data is deleted immediately” (unless documented behavior and configuration support it)

## Records and auditability
GRC maintains an exceptions log capturing:
- Account and date
- Questionnaire question text
- Final approved response text
- Severity level
- Approvers
- Any evidence shared and channel used
- Expiration/review date (if applicable)

Exception approvals must be retained for at least 24 months for internal auditability and to support consistent future responses.

## Review cadence and ownership
- Owner: GRC (Olga Petrov)
- Review cadence: Quarterly, and additionally after any material product changes affecting security posture (auth/RBAC, audit logging, data retention/residency, encryption/KMS, Private deployment packaging).
- Proposed changes are reviewed by Security Engineering (Marcus Lin), Product Security PM (Naomi Feldman), and Legal (Michael Grant) for any language with contractual risk.

## Questions / escalation contacts
- Primary: GRC (Olga Petrov) for process, evidence, and compliance language
- Technical: Security Engineering (Marcus Lin) for control feasibility and boundaries
- Capability alignment: Product Security PM (Naomi Feldman)
- Contractual terms: Legal (Michael Grant)
- Executive escalation: Dr. Aisha Rahman (CISO) for S3/high-risk exceptions

## Changelog
- 2026-02-10: Clarified severity matrix; added explicit prohibition on roadmap language; expanded approved alternatives for residency/retention and audit logging; aligned approval steps with RFP response ownership SOP.
- 2025-11-04: Added expedite path and evidence-sharing guardrails.
- 2025-09-18: Initial publication.

source.title: SOC 2 Customer Evidence Request Playbook (Sales/SE + GRC)
source.author: Olga Petrov
source.body:
## Purpose
This playbook defines the standard process for responding to customer and prospect requests for SOC 2 reports and related security/compliance evidence. It ensures we:
- Provide accurate, approved materials (no ad-hoc claims).
- Protect restricted audit artifacts and customer-sensitive information.
- Meet predictable response timelines for enterprise procurement.

This page is the authoritative internal reference for evidence handling. Customer-facing summaries should link to approved collateral (e.g., Security FAQ / one-pagers) rather than attaching restricted audit evidence.

## Scope
**In scope**
- SOC 2 Type II report distribution under NDA.
- Common supporting evidence (policies, control summaries, penetration test attestation letters where available, security overview documents).
- Handling follow-up questions and exceptions requests.

**Out of scope**
- Promising roadmap items or control implementations not currently supported.
- Providing raw auditor workpapers, detailed test scripts, internal tickets, or vulnerability scan outputs.

## Roles & responsibilities
- **Requestor (Sales / Solutions Engineering / Customer Success):** Owns customer communication, ensures intake is complete, confirms timeline, and sends approved evidence package.
- **GRC Lead (Olga Petrov):** Owns process, approves evidence set, assigns analyst, maintains artifact list and this playbook.
- **Compliance Evidence Reviewer (Daniel Carter):** Executes triage, validates NDA, prepares package, logs completion.
- **Security Engineering reviewer (Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei):** Supports technical clarifications and validates security statements when needed.
- **Legal reviewer (Michael Grant):** Reviews/approves NDAs and any deviations from standard terms.
- **Executive escalation (Dr. Aisha Rahman):** Escalation path for high-risk requests, urgent deadlines, or non-standard disclosures.

## Where to start (intake)
All evidence requests must be logged and routed through the **Evidence Request Intake Form**.

**Evidence Request Intake Form (internal):**
- Link: https://go.redwood.internal/security/evidence-request-intake
- Required fields:
  1) Account name + region (e.g., US/EU) and deal stage
  2) Customer security contact + email
  3) Requested artifacts (SOC 2 report, bridge letter, policies, etc.)
  4) Due date and reason for urgency
  5) NDA status (signed / in progress / customer requires their NDA)
  6) Deployment mode under evaluation (Hosted / Dedicated / Private)
  7) Any special regulatory constraints (HIPAA, PCI, FedRAMP, etc.)

**Tracking:** After submission, the request is automatically recorded in the **Evidence Request Triage Queue** (restricted sheet) with an assigned status and owner.

## NDA requirements (non-negotiable for SOC 2 report)
### When an NDA is required
- **SOC 2 Type II report:** Always requires an NDA (customer NDA or Redwood standard NDA) executed prior to sharing.
- **Bridge letter (if available):** Requires NDA.
- **Security policies and control summaries:** Typically internal/restricted; may be shared case-by-case under NDA based on content.

### Standard NDA flow
1) Requestor confirms whether there is an existing executed NDA in place with the customer.
2) If none exists:
   - Use **Redwood standard mutual NDA** when possible.
   - If customer insists on their paper, route to Legal.
3) Evidence is only shared after Legal confirms execution.

### Notes for Sales/SE
- Do not email the SOC 2 report to a personal email address.
- Do not upload the SOC 2 report to third-party portals unless approved by GRC + Legal (see “Questionnaire portals” below).

## Response timelines (SLA)
We target the following turnaround times once intake is complete and NDA status is clear:

| Request type | Target SLA | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Standard SOC 2 report under executed NDA | 2 business days | Includes packaging + controlled delivery |
| First-time NDA execution (Redwood NDA) | 3–5 business days | Depends on customer signature turnaround |
| Customer paper NDA review | 5–10 business days | May require redlines; plan ahead |
| Follow-up evidence Q&A | 2 business days | For straightforward clarifications |
| Non-standard evidence request / exceptions | 5+ business days | Requires escalation and written approval |

**Clock starts** when intake is complete (including a due date, named recipient, and NDA path).

## Artifact catalog (what we can share)
The exact package depends on the customer and deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). Default packages are below.

### Default evidence package (most enterprise requests)
- SOC 2 Type II report (current period), under NDA
- SOC 2 “customer evidence cover note” (approved email template)
- Enterprise Security FAQ (current published version)
- Data retention & deletion overview (customer-facing)
- Audit logging overview (customer-facing)

### Additional items (case-by-case)
- Penetration test attestation letter / executive summary (if available for distribution)
- Subprocessor list (customer-facing canonical link or current list under controlled sharing)
- Security policy excerpts (approved excerpts only; no internal-only sections)
- Business continuity / DR overview (summary form)

### Not shareable (do not provide)
- Auditor workpapers, evidence screenshots with internal identifiers, ticket exports
- Raw vulnerability scan output, detailed findings, or exploit PoCs
- Internal incident details beyond approved public/customer-specific disclosures
- Architecture diagrams labeled internal-only unless explicitly approved for that customer

If the customer requests any “Not shareable” items, route to the **Customer Security Questionnaire Exceptions Policy** and escalate to GRC.

## Evidence storage & access controls
### Source of truth
All official compliance evidence artifacts are stored in:
- **Restricted Drive folder:** “Security & Compliance → Compliance Evidence → SOC 2 (Restricted)”
- Access is limited to GRC, Security leadership, and designated reviewers.

### How requestors receive artifacts
- Requestors do **not** browse the restricted folder directly unless explicitly approved.
- GRC provides either:
  1) A time-bound secure link to a customer-specific package folder, or
  2) An approved attachment set sent via controlled channel (see below).

### Access requests
If a Sales/SE/CS team member needs access to restricted evidence storage for operational reasons, they must file an internal access request per policy and receive GRC approval.

## Controlled delivery (how to send evidence)
Approved delivery channels (ranked):
1) **Secure link to customer-specific package folder** with least-privilege sharing
2) **Encrypted email attachment** (only when secure link is not viable)
3) **Customer procurement/security portal upload** (requires explicit approval)

Minimum delivery requirements:
- Confirm recipient identity (named security/procurement contact).
- Confirm NDA execution.
- Share only the minimum necessary artifacts.
- Include the “No further distribution” reminder (template below).

### Template: cover note to accompany SOC 2 report
Subject: Redwood Inference SOC 2 Report (Confidential – Under NDA)

Body:
- Attached / linked is Redwood Inference’s SOC 2 Type II report. This material is confidential and provided under the terms of our executed NDA.
- Please limit distribution to personnel involved in your security/compliance review.
- If you have follow-up questions, please consolidate them (where possible) and we will respond within our standard evidence SLA.
- For product capability questions (SSO/RBAC/audit logging/data retention), please reference the linked Security FAQ and reach out if you need deployment-mode-specific clarification.

## Triage workflow (step-by-step)
### 1) Intake completeness check (Requestor)
Before pinging GRC, ensure the intake form includes:
- Customer name, due date, named recipient
- Requested items (explicit list)
- NDA status/path
- Deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)

### 2) Triage (GRC)
GRC (Olga/Daniel) will:
- Confirm whether an NDA is required and executed
- Confirm the correct SOC 2 report version/period
- Identify any special constraints (e.g., EU residency concerns, on-prem needs, HIPAA asks)
- Assign an owner in the Evidence Request Triage Queue

### 3) Package assembly (GRC analyst)
- Build a customer-specific evidence package folder or attachment set
- Validate that any included supporting artifacts are approved for distribution
- Apply redactions if needed (rare; must be approved by GRC lead)

### 4) Review & approval gates
- **Standard package:** GRC analyst + GRC lead approval
- **Non-standard requests:** Add Security Eng reviewer and Legal as needed
- **Any new claim language:** Must align to the published Security FAQ and the approved claims matrix; if not covered, escalate to Product Security PM (Naomi Feldman)

### 5) Delivery (Requestor)
- Send using controlled delivery
- Log delivery date and method in the triage queue
- Capture any follow-up questions and route back through the ticket/thread so we keep a complete audit trail

### 6) Close-out
GRC marks the request complete and tags:
- Artifacts shared
- NDA status
- Any deviations/exceptions
- Notes that should inform future FAQ/RFP library updates

## Handling questionnaires and portal uploads
If a customer requests that we upload the SOC 2 report to an external portal:
- Requestor must confirm:
  - Portal vendor name
  - Required user accounts and access scope
  - Who on the customer side will have access
- GRC + Legal must approve the upload method before proceeding.

Preferred alternative: provide a secure link under NDA rather than uploading to multiple portals.

## Common pitfalls (what to avoid)
- Sharing an outdated SOC 2 report or an internally-drafted control narrative.
- Using “ISO certified” language unless explicitly accurate and approved (see Security FAQ do/don’t-say guidance).
- Answering beyond the question (e.g., giving future-state capability promises).
- Forwarding evidence internally in broad channels; restricted materials should stay in restricted storage with minimal distribution.

## Escalations
Escalate immediately to GRC lead + CISO when:
- Customer requests raw security testing outputs, internal incident details, or auditor workpapers.
- Customer demands evidence before NDA execution.
- The deadline is < 2 business days and the request is tied to deal closure.
- The request involves regulated frameworks requiring special handling (e.g., HIPAA/BAA, PCI, government requirements).

Primary escalation contacts:
- GRC: Olga Petrov
- Evidence operations: Daniel Carter
- Security leadership: Dr. Aisha Rahman
- Security engineering: Marcus Lin
- Legal: Michael Grant

## Auditability & versioning
- Every evidence request must be tracked end-to-end in the Evidence Request Triage Queue.
- The SOC 2 report version and audit period must be recorded for each delivery.
- Any deviations from this playbook (artifact exceptions, portal upload approvals) must be documented in the request record.

## FAQ
**Q: Can we share a SOC 2 “certificate” instead of the report?**
A: SOC 2 is an attestation report, not a certification. Use the approved SOC 2 one-pager / cover note language and provide the report under NDA.

**Q: Can we send evidence to multiple customer recipients?**
A: Prefer a single named security/procurement owner. If additional recipients are needed, confirm they are covered under the NDA and limit sharing to those involved in the review.

**Q: The customer asks for ISO 27001 certification. What do we say?**
A: Use the approved ISO alignment language from the enablement kit and do not claim certification unless explicitly confirmed by GRC-approved collateral.

**Q: The customer asks for a pen test report.**
A: We generally do not distribute full pen test reports. If an attestation letter or executive summary is approved for sharing, GRC can provide it under NDA. Otherwise, route as an exception request.

## Changelog
- 2026-02-10: Updated SLAs, clarified portal upload approvals, and aligned “not shareable” list with current restricted evidence controls.
- 2025-11-18: Added standardized cover note template and tightened NDA requirements.
- 2025-09-15: Initial publication.

title: SOC 2 Customer Evidence Request Playbook (Sales/SE + GRC)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook defines the standard process for responding to customer and prospect requests for SOC 2 reports and related security/compliance evidence. It ensures we:
- Provide accurate, approved materials (no ad-hoc claims).
- Protect restricted audit artifacts and customer-sensitive information.
- Meet predictable response timelines for enterprise procurement.

This page is the authoritative internal reference for evidence handling. Customer-facing summaries should link to approved collateral (e.g., Security FAQ / one-pagers) rather than attaching restricted audit evidence.

## Scope
**In scope**
- SOC 2 Type II report distribution under NDA.
- Common supporting evidence (policies, control summaries, penetration test attestation letters where available, security overview documents).
- Handling follow-up questions and exceptions requests.

**Out of scope**
- Promising roadmap items or control implementations not currently supported.
- Providing raw auditor workpapers, detailed test scripts, internal tickets, or vulnerability scan outputs.

## Roles & responsibilities
- **Requestor (Sales / Solutions Engineering / Customer Success):** Owns customer communication, ensures intake is complete, confirms timeline, and sends approved evidence package.
- **GRC Lead (Olga Petrov):** Owns process, approves evidence set, assigns analyst, maintains artifact list and this playbook.
- **Compliance Evidence Reviewer (Daniel Carter):** Executes triage, validates NDA, prepares package, logs completion.
- **Security Engineering reviewer (Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei):** Supports technical clarifications and validates security statements when needed.
- **Legal reviewer (Michael Grant):** Reviews/approves NDAs and any deviations from standard terms.
- **Executive escalation (Dr. Aisha Rahman):** Escalation path for high-risk requests, urgent deadlines, or non-standard disclosures.

## Where to start (intake)
All evidence requests must be logged and routed through the **Evidence Request Intake Form**.

**Evidence Request Intake Form (internal):**
- Link: https://go.redwood.internal/security/evidence-request-intake
- Required fields:
  1) Account name + region (e.g., US/EU) and deal stage
  2) Customer security contact + email
  3) Requested artifacts (SOC 2 report, bridge letter, policies, etc.)
  4) Due date and reason for urgency
  5) NDA status (signed / in progress / customer requires their NDA)
  6) Deployment mode under evaluation (Hosted / Dedicated / Private)
  7) Any special regulatory constraints (HIPAA, PCI, FedRAMP, etc.)

**Tracking:** After submission, the request is automatically recorded in the **Evidence Request Triage Queue** (restricted sheet) with an assigned status and owner.

## NDA requirements (non-negotiable for SOC 2 report)
### When an NDA is required
- **SOC 2 Type II report:** Always requires an NDA (customer NDA or Redwood standard NDA) executed prior to sharing.
- **Bridge letter (if available):** Requires NDA.
- **Security policies and control summaries:** Typically internal/restricted; may be shared case-by-case under NDA based on content.

### Standard NDA flow
1) Requestor confirms whether there is an existing executed NDA in place with the customer.
2) If none exists:
   - Use **Redwood standard mutual NDA** when possible.
   - If customer insists on their paper, route to Legal.
3) Evidence is only shared after Legal confirms execution.

### Notes for Sales/SE
- Do not email the SOC 2 report to a personal email address.
- Do not upload the SOC 2 report to third-party portals unless approved by GRC + Legal (see “Questionnaire portals” below).

## Response timelines (SLA)
We target the following turnaround times once intake is complete and NDA status is clear:

| Request type | Target SLA | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Standard SOC 2 report under executed NDA | 2 business days | Includes packaging + controlled delivery |
| First-time NDA execution (Redwood NDA) | 3–5 business days | Depends on customer signature turnaround |
| Customer paper NDA review | 5–10 business days | May require redlines; plan ahead |
| Follow-up evidence Q&A | 2 business days | For straightforward clarifications |
| Non-standard evidence request / exceptions | 5+ business days | Requires escalation and written approval |

**Clock starts** when intake is complete (including a due date, named recipient, and NDA path).

## Artifact catalog (what we can share)
The exact package depends on the customer and deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). Default packages are below.

### Default evidence package (most enterprise requests)
- SOC 2 Type II report (current period), under NDA
- SOC 2 “customer evidence cover note” (approved email template)
- Enterprise Security FAQ (current published version)
- Data retention & deletion overview (customer-facing)
- Audit logging overview (customer-facing)

### Additional items (case-by-case)
- Penetration test attestation letter / executive summary (if available for distribution)
- Subprocessor list (customer-facing canonical link or current list under controlled sharing)
- Security policy excerpts (approved excerpts only; no internal-only sections)
- Business continuity / DR overview (summary form)

### Not shareable (do not provide)
- Auditor workpapers, evidence screenshots with internal identifiers, ticket exports
- Raw vulnerability scan output, detailed findings, or exploit PoCs
- Internal incident details beyond approved public/customer-specific disclosures
- Architecture diagrams labeled internal-only unless explicitly approved for that customer

If the customer requests any “Not shareable” items, route to the **Customer Security Questionnaire Exceptions Policy** and escalate to GRC.

## Evidence storage & access controls
### Source of truth
All official compliance evidence artifacts are stored in:
- **Restricted Drive folder:** “Security & Compliance → Compliance Evidence → SOC 2 (Restricted)”
- Access is limited to GRC, Security leadership, and designated reviewers.

### How requestors receive artifacts
- Requestors do **not** browse the restricted folder directly unless explicitly approved.
- GRC provides either:
  1) A time-bound secure link to a customer-specific package folder, or
  2) An approved attachment set sent via controlled channel (see below).

### Access requests
If a Sales/SE/CS team member needs access to restricted evidence storage for operational reasons, they must file an internal access request per policy and receive GRC approval.

## Controlled delivery (how to send evidence)
Approved delivery channels (ranked):
1) **Secure link to customer-specific package folder** with least-privilege sharing
2) **Encrypted email attachment** (only when secure link is not viable)
3) **Customer procurement/security portal upload** (requires explicit approval)

Minimum delivery requirements:
- Confirm recipient identity (named security/procurement contact).
- Confirm NDA execution.
- Share only the minimum necessary artifacts.
- Include the “No further distribution” reminder (template below).

### Template: cover note to accompany SOC 2 report
Subject: Redwood Inference SOC 2 Report (Confidential – Under NDA)

Body:
- Attached / linked is Redwood Inference’s SOC 2 Type II report. This material is confidential and provided under the terms of our executed NDA.
- Please limit distribution to personnel involved in your security/compliance review.
- If you have follow-up questions, please consolidate them (where possible) and we will respond within our standard evidence SLA.
- For product capability questions (SSO/RBAC/audit logging/data retention), please reference the linked Security FAQ and reach out if you need deployment-mode-specific clarification.

## Triage workflow (step-by-step)
### 1) Intake completeness check (Requestor)
Before pinging GRC, ensure the intake form includes:
- Customer name, due date, named recipient
- Requested items (explicit list)
- NDA status/path
- Deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)

### 2) Triage (GRC)
GRC (Olga/Daniel) will:
- Confirm whether an NDA is required and executed
- Confirm the correct SOC 2 report version/period
- Identify any special constraints (e.g., EU residency concerns, on-prem needs, HIPAA asks)
- Assign an owner in the Evidence Request Triage Queue

### 3) Package assembly (GRC analyst)
- Build a customer-specific evidence package folder or attachment set
- Validate that any included supporting artifacts are approved for distribution
- Apply redactions if needed (rare; must be approved by GRC lead)

### 4) Review & approval gates
- **Standard package:** GRC analyst + GRC lead approval
- **Non-standard requests:** Add Security Eng reviewer and Legal as needed
- **Any new claim language:** Must align to the published Security FAQ and the approved claims matrix; if not covered, escalate to Product Security PM (Naomi Feldman)

### 5) Delivery (Requestor)
- Send using controlled delivery
- Log delivery date and method in the triage queue
- Capture any follow-up questions and route back through the ticket/thread so we keep a complete audit trail

### 6) Close-out
GRC marks the request complete and tags:
- Artifacts shared
- NDA status
- Any deviations/exceptions
- Notes that should inform future FAQ/RFP library updates

## Handling questionnaires and portal uploads
If a customer requests that we upload the SOC 2 report to an external portal:
- Requestor must confirm:
  - Portal vendor name
  - Required user accounts and access scope
  - Who on the customer side will have access
- GRC + Legal must approve the upload method before proceeding.

Preferred alternative: provide a secure link under NDA rather than uploading to multiple portals.

## Common pitfalls (what to avoid)
- Sharing an outdated SOC 2 report or an internally-drafted control narrative.
- Using “ISO certified” language unless explicitly accurate and approved (see Security FAQ do/don’t-say guidance).
- Answering beyond the question (e.g., giving future-state capability promises).
- Forwarding evidence internally in broad channels; restricted materials should stay in restricted storage with minimal distribution.

## Escalations
Escalate immediately to GRC lead + CISO when:
- Customer requests raw security testing outputs, internal incident details, or auditor workpapers.
- Customer demands evidence before NDA execution.
- The deadline is < 2 business days and the request is tied to deal closure.
- The request involves regulated frameworks requiring special handling (e.g., HIPAA/BAA, PCI, government requirements).

Primary escalation contacts:
- GRC: Olga Petrov
- Evidence operations: Daniel Carter
- Security leadership: Dr. Aisha Rahman
- Security engineering: Marcus Lin
- Legal: Michael Grant

## Auditability & versioning
- Every evidence request must be tracked end-to-end in the Evidence Request Triage Queue.
- The SOC 2 report version and audit period must be recorded for each delivery.
- Any deviations from this playbook (artifact exceptions, portal upload approvals) must be documented in the request record.

## FAQ
**Q: Can we share a SOC 2 “certificate” instead of the report?**
A: SOC 2 is an attestation report, not a certification. Use the approved SOC 2 one-pager / cover note language and provide the report under NDA.

**Q: Can we send evidence to multiple customer recipients?**
A: Prefer a single named security/procurement owner. If additional recipients are needed, confirm they are covered under the NDA and limit sharing to those involved in the review.

**Q: The customer asks for ISO 27001 certification. What do we say?**
A: Use the approved ISO alignment language from the enablement kit and do not claim certification unless explicitly confirmed by GRC-approved collateral.

**Q: The customer asks for a pen test report.**
A: We generally do not distribute full pen test reports. If an attestation letter or executive summary is approved for sharing, GRC can provide it under NDA. Otherwise, route as an exception request.

## Changelog
- 2026-02-10: Updated SLAs, clarified portal upload approvals, and aligned “not shareable” list with current restricted evidence controls.
- 2025-11-18: Added standardized cover note template and tightened NDA requirements.
- 2025-09-15: Initial publication.

source.title: RBAC Evidence Accelerator and Failure-Mode Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

This playbook describes a structured, automation-first approach for: (1) ensuring RBAC and authorization changes are safe to deploy, (2) capturing immutable compliance evidence across failures and cross-border failovers, and (3) integrating audit logging, data-residency constraints, and SDLC gating into incident response and risk exception processes. It is intended for engineering SREs, security engineers, release managers, and compliance owners.

Scope
- Applies to: service accounts, role templates, entitlement matrices, role-binding changes, attribute-based access policy updates, and key release/build artifacts for model-serving and control-plane components.
- Environments: staging, canary, production, and cross-region failover (multi-region deployments).
- Exclusions: ad-hoc developer test accounts (see Access Sandbox policy).

Why this exists
- We observed gaps in evidence capture during several cross-region incidents: missing correlation between audit events and deployment artifacts; inconsistent retention applied by region; and lack of a single automatable artifact bundle for auditors. This playbook codifies the steps and tool primitives to close those gaps and minimize time-to-evidence during incidents and audits.

Ownership and SLAs
- Playbook owner: Security Engineering (Priya Natarajan)
- Oncall reviewer: SRE (Engineering)
- Evidence availability SLO: 95% of requests return a complete evidence bundle within 48 hours after incident declaration.
- Audit logging durability target: event replication to immutable index within 120 seconds for production-critical services.

Key Concepts and Definitions
- Evidence Bundle: Signed tarball containing: deployment manifest, CI pipeline run log, RBAC change diff, audit-event slice (time-bounded), KMS-access logs (key one-time actions), residency map.
- Correlation ID: 128-bit hex token attached to every RBAC change request and propagated in the pipeline, change API, and audit events.
- Residency Tag: Per-request geo tag (e.g., eu, na, ap) attached at ingress and enforced by policy.

Pre-deployment Controls (SDLC Gates)
1) Change Request and Ticketing
   - All RBAC/entitlement changes require a pre-authorized TFR (Technical Feature Request) with: justification, list of affected service-ids, residency impact matrix, and rollback plan.
2) CI/CD Policy Checks (automated)
   - Gate jobs: rbac-static-analysis, residency-impact, audit-propagation-checks. Failure of any job blocks promotion.
   - Example CLI: 
     - ./ci/gate-check --pr 1931 --jobs rbac-static-analysis,residency-impact
3) Signed Artifact Requirement
   - All release artifacts touching authz config must be GPG-signed and produce an artifact signature entry stored in the evidence catalog.

Operational Procedures: Standard Change (non-incident)
1) Author change in feature branch with change-spec (YAML):
   - change-spec fields: id, author, target-roles[], reason, residency-scopes[], correlation_id (auto-generated)
2) Run CI gates; attach correlation_id to pipeline metadata.
3) Canary rollout (10%) for 12 hours; collect audit slice: events where correlation_id = X.
4) If no anomalies (see detection thresholds below), proceed to full rollout and automatically generate Evidence Bundle.
5) Bundle publishing: evidence-bundler --correlation-id X --out s3://evidence-buckets/locked/{id}.tar.gz --kms-key arn:aws:kms:region:acct:key/aa11bb22

Detection thresholds and anomaly signals
- Unexpected 5xx in auth service after change: >0.5% increase over 1 hour window compared to baseline.
- Entitlement spike: more than 3x the historical attach rate for a role in 30 minutes.
- Cross-border residency violation: any event where request.residency_tag != resource.required_residency.

Incident response: RBAC or Residency Failure Mode
Purpose: This section covers both security incidents caused by authorization misconfiguration and operational failover events (region outage requiring residency override).

Declaration criteria
- Incident declared when: (A) a critical service experiences authorization failures impacting >20% of user requests for >5 minutes, or (B) an active cross-border residency breach is detected.

Immediate actions (0–15 minutes)
1) Pager escalation: security-oncall + oncall-sre + product-owner.
2) Run the fast-capture script: ./tools/incident/capture-evidence --correlation-id {id} --window now-15m --include-logs --include-deploy --out /tmp/{id}-fast-bundle.tar.gz
3) If service is degraded, enable fallback policy (policy id: fallback-std-2024) to route to read-only or lower-entitlement model variants. Document action in the incident ticket.

Containment (15–60 minutes)
1) Snapshot active role bindings: authctl list-bindings --format json --out /tmp/{id}-bindings.json
2) Lock further role changes by setting change-control mode to maintenance: ./auth-api set-maintenance true --reason "incident {id}"
3) If incident is a residency breach, trigger geo-fence: ./residency block-region --region <code> --apply-affected-resources=true
4) Start evidence orchestration task which will: collect audit slice, pipeline runs, KMS logs, and network egress metrics for the window [-30m, +30m].

Forensic collection and packaging (60–180 minutes)
- Evidence collector specification (table):
| Artifact | Retriever | Retention location | Responsible |
| -------- | --------- | ------------------ | ----------- |
| Audit events (auth-service) | audit-query --q "correlation_id:{id}" --start {t0} --end {t1} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/audit.ndjson | Oncall SRE |
| CI pipeline logs | ci-api fetch --build {build_id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/ci.log | Release Eng |
| RBAC diff | git diff --name-only {pre} {post} && git show {post}:auth/roles.yaml | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/rbac.yaml | Security Eng |
| KMS access audit | kms-audit --filter correlation_id={id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/kms.log | Security Eng |
| Network egress metrics | net-metrics --flow filter cid:{correlation_id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/egress.csv | NetOps |

- All artifacts must be SHA256-summed and the manifest signed by security-oncall. Example manifest line: "sha256:3b2f...  audit.ndjson"

Evidence bundling and legal hold
1) After collection, invoke: evidence-signer --bundle s3://evidence/immutable/{id} --signer /keys/evidence-signer-key --out s3://evidence/locked/{id}.tar.gz
2) Apply legal-hold tag if incident class >= P2: aws s3api put-object-tagging --bucket evidence --key locked/{id}.tar.gz --tagging "LegalHold=true&Retention=10y"
3) Notify Legal and Compliance channels with bundle link and summary.

Risk and Exception Handling (pre-authorized deviations)
- Risk acceptance template (must be attached to TFR): impact, compensating controls, monitoring plan, expiration date, approver (security+compliance).
- Temporary residency exception process: approval required from Regional Data Custodian and CISO; exception artifacts must be included in Evidence Bundle with a signed attestation.
- Automatic rollback triggers: if anomaly thresholds are exceeded during canary, system must automatically invoke rollback within 5 minutes and produce rollback evidence.

Audit Logging Schema (auth-service minimal required fields)
- JSON event example:
{ "timestamp": "2026-02-01T15:12:10Z", "service": "auth-service", "correlation_id": "a3f9c5e2-...", "actor": { "type": "service_account", "id": "sa-deployer-prod" }, "action": "role.bind", "target": { "type": "role", "id": "role/model-inference-writer" }, "residency": "eu", "outcome": "success|failure", "request_id": "req_...", "metadata": { "pipeline_build": 7213 } }

Retention and Residency Mapping (summary table)
| Artifact Class | Default Retention | Cross-border constraints |
| -------------- | ----------------- | ----------------------- |
| Audit events (auth) | 7 years (immutable store) | EU events stored in EU primary + replicated index in NA (read-only), replication only after redaction | 
| CI logs | 3 years | Build artifacts containing customer data do not leave region without exception |
| Evidence Bundles | 10 years (legal hold support) | Bundles that contain residency-protected data must store primary copy in required jurisdiction |

Automation and Tooling primitives (examples)
- capture-evidence: collects time-bounded slices from all sources and generates signed manifests.
- evidence-signer: wraps and signs bundles with KMS and records signer principal.
- rbac-audit-linter: static analyzer that flags excessive role-permissions, missing residency tags, and suspicious role-attach patterns.

Post-incident: Review and Remediation
1) RCA playbook: SRE and Security to co-author timeline, root cause, and evidence index within 5 business days.
2) Remediation ticket must include: code fixes, SDLC gate improvements, additional monitoring, and a compliance evidence re-run to validate capture.
3) If failures are due to a process gap, update this playbook and re-run a tabletop in the next weekly game-day.

Checklist: Minimum Items for an Auditor Request (quick hit)
- Signed Evidence Bundle (S3 locked URL)
- Correlation ID and change-spec (YAML)
- Audit-event slice covering the incident window (ndjson)
- CI pipeline run + build artifact signatures
- RBAC pre/post diffs and access snapshots
- KMS access log slice and signer manifest
- Residency attestation or exception approval (if applicable)

Appendix A: Sample Change-spec (YAML)
---
id: rbac-change-2026-0420
author: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai
correlation_id: 9f2b1a...
reason: Enable model serving write role for predictor-v2
target_services: [predictor-v2, inference-router]
target_roles: [role/model-inference-writer]
residency_scopes: [eu]
rollout_strategy:
  canary_percent: 10
  canary_duration_h: 12
  auto_rollback_on_threshold: true
---

Appendix B: Common Playbook Commands (cheat sheet)
- Lock role changes: ./auth-api set-maintenance true --reason "incident {id}"
- Snapshot bindings: authctl list-bindings --format json --out /tmp/{id}-bindings.json
- Build evidence bundle: evidence-bundler --correlation-id {id} --out s3://evidence/locked/{id}.tar.gz
- Apply legal hold: aws s3api put-object-tagging --bucket evidence --key locked/{id}.tar.gz --tagging "LegalHold=true&Retention=10y"

Related reading and references
- RBAC pattern library: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth
- Audit schema contract: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/event-schema-contract-and-retention-runbook-2025
- SDLC gating standards: /confluence/security-and-compliance/secure-sdlc/sdlc-security-gates-rbac-audit-residency-checklist-2026

Revision history
- 2025-09-15: Created (initial draft)
- 2025-11-02: Added automation CLI examples and retention table
- 2026-02-10: Finalized evidence bundle signing flow and legal-hold integration

title: RBAC Evidence Accelerator and Failure-Mode Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook describes a structured, automation-first approach for: (1) ensuring RBAC and authorization changes are safe to deploy, (2) capturing immutable compliance evidence across failures and cross-border failovers, and (3) integrating audit logging, data-residency constraints, and SDLC gating into incident response and risk exception processes. It is intended for engineering SREs, security engineers, release managers, and compliance owners.

Scope
- Applies to: service accounts, role templates, entitlement matrices, role-binding changes, attribute-based access policy updates, and key release/build artifacts for model-serving and control-plane components.
- Environments: staging, canary, production, and cross-region failover (multi-region deployments).
- Exclusions: ad-hoc developer test accounts (see Access Sandbox policy).

Why this exists
- We observed gaps in evidence capture during several cross-region incidents: missing correlation between audit events and deployment artifacts; inconsistent retention applied by region; and lack of a single automatable artifact bundle for auditors. This playbook codifies the steps and tool primitives to close those gaps and minimize time-to-evidence during incidents and audits.

Ownership and SLAs
- Playbook owner: Security Engineering (Priya Natarajan)
- Oncall reviewer: SRE (Engineering)
- Evidence availability SLO: 95% of requests return a complete evidence bundle within 48 hours after incident declaration.
- Audit logging durability target: event replication to immutable index within 120 seconds for production-critical services.

Key Concepts and Definitions
- Evidence Bundle: Signed tarball containing: deployment manifest, CI pipeline run log, RBAC change diff, audit-event slice (time-bounded), KMS-access logs (key one-time actions), residency map.
- Correlation ID: 128-bit hex token attached to every RBAC change request and propagated in the pipeline, change API, and audit events.
- Residency Tag: Per-request geo tag (e.g., eu, na, ap) attached at ingress and enforced by policy.

Pre-deployment Controls (SDLC Gates)
1) Change Request and Ticketing
   - All RBAC/entitlement changes require a pre-authorized TFR (Technical Feature Request) with: justification, list of affected service-ids, residency impact matrix, and rollback plan.
2) CI/CD Policy Checks (automated)
   - Gate jobs: rbac-static-analysis, residency-impact, audit-propagation-checks. Failure of any job blocks promotion.
   - Example CLI: 
     - ./ci/gate-check --pr 1931 --jobs rbac-static-analysis,residency-impact
3) Signed Artifact Requirement
   - All release artifacts touching authz config must be GPG-signed and produce an artifact signature entry stored in the evidence catalog.

Operational Procedures: Standard Change (non-incident)
1) Author change in feature branch with change-spec (YAML):
   - change-spec fields: id, author, target-roles[], reason, residency-scopes[], correlation_id (auto-generated)
2) Run CI gates; attach correlation_id to pipeline metadata.
3) Canary rollout (10%) for 12 hours; collect audit slice: events where correlation_id = X.
4) If no anomalies (see detection thresholds below), proceed to full rollout and automatically generate Evidence Bundle.
5) Bundle publishing: evidence-bundler --correlation-id X --out s3://evidence-buckets/locked/{id}.tar.gz --kms-key arn:aws:kms:region:acct:key/aa11bb22

Detection thresholds and anomaly signals
- Unexpected 5xx in auth service after change: >0.5% increase over 1 hour window compared to baseline.
- Entitlement spike: more than 3x the historical attach rate for a role in 30 minutes.
- Cross-border residency violation: any event where request.residency_tag != resource.required_residency.

Incident response: RBAC or Residency Failure Mode
Purpose: This section covers both security incidents caused by authorization misconfiguration and operational failover events (region outage requiring residency override).

Declaration criteria
- Incident declared when: (A) a critical service experiences authorization failures impacting >20% of user requests for >5 minutes, or (B) an active cross-border residency breach is detected.

Immediate actions (0–15 minutes)
1) Pager escalation: security-oncall + oncall-sre + product-owner.
2) Run the fast-capture script: ./tools/incident/capture-evidence --correlation-id {id} --window now-15m --include-logs --include-deploy --out /tmp/{id}-fast-bundle.tar.gz
3) If service is degraded, enable fallback policy (policy id: fallback-std-2024) to route to read-only or lower-entitlement model variants. Document action in the incident ticket.

Containment (15–60 minutes)
1) Snapshot active role bindings: authctl list-bindings --format json --out /tmp/{id}-bindings.json
2) Lock further role changes by setting change-control mode to maintenance: ./auth-api set-maintenance true --reason "incident {id}"
3) If incident is a residency breach, trigger geo-fence: ./residency block-region --region <code> --apply-affected-resources=true
4) Start evidence orchestration task which will: collect audit slice, pipeline runs, KMS logs, and network egress metrics for the window [-30m, +30m].

Forensic collection and packaging (60–180 minutes)
- Evidence collector specification (table):
| Artifact | Retriever | Retention location | Responsible |
| -------- | --------- | ------------------ | ----------- |
| Audit events (auth-service) | audit-query --q "correlation_id:{id}" --start {t0} --end {t1} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/audit.ndjson | Oncall SRE |
| CI pipeline logs | ci-api fetch --build {build_id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/ci.log | Release Eng |
| RBAC diff | git diff --name-only {pre} {post} && git show {post}:auth/roles.yaml | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/rbac.yaml | Security Eng |
| KMS access audit | kms-audit --filter correlation_id={id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/kms.log | Security Eng |
| Network egress metrics | net-metrics --flow filter cid:{correlation_id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/egress.csv | NetOps |

- All artifacts must be SHA256-summed and the manifest signed by security-oncall. Example manifest line: "sha256:3b2f...  audit.ndjson"

Evidence bundling and legal hold
1) After collection, invoke: evidence-signer --bundle s3://evidence/immutable/{id} --signer /keys/evidence-signer-key --out s3://evidence/locked/{id}.tar.gz
2) Apply legal-hold tag if incident class >= P2: aws s3api put-object-tagging --bucket evidence --key locked/{id}.tar.gz --tagging "LegalHold=true&Retention=10y"
3) Notify Legal and Compliance channels with bundle link and summary.

Risk and Exception Handling (pre-authorized deviations)
- Risk acceptance template (must be attached to TFR): impact, compensating controls, monitoring plan, expiration date, approver (security+compliance).
- Temporary residency exception process: approval required from Regional Data Custodian and CISO; exception artifacts must be included in Evidence Bundle with a signed attestation.
- Automatic rollback triggers: if anomaly thresholds are exceeded during canary, system must automatically invoke rollback within 5 minutes and produce rollback evidence.

Audit Logging Schema (auth-service minimal required fields)
- JSON event example:
{ "timestamp": "2026-02-01T15:12:10Z", "service": "auth-service", "correlation_id": "a3f9c5e2-...", "actor": { "type": "service_account", "id": "sa-deployer-prod" }, "action": "role.bind", "target": { "type": "role", "id": "role/model-inference-writer" }, "residency": "eu", "outcome": "success|failure", "request_id": "req_...", "metadata": { "pipeline_build": 7213 } }

Retention and Residency Mapping (summary table)
| Artifact Class | Default Retention | Cross-border constraints |
| -------------- | ----------------- | ----------------------- |
| Audit events (auth) | 7 years (immutable store) | EU events stored in EU primary + replicated index in NA (read-only), replication only after redaction | 
| CI logs | 3 years | Build artifacts containing customer data do not leave region without exception |
| Evidence Bundles | 10 years (legal hold support) | Bundles that contain residency-protected data must store primary copy in required jurisdiction |

Automation and Tooling primitives (examples)
- capture-evidence: collects time-bounded slices from all sources and generates signed manifests.
- evidence-signer: wraps and signs bundles with KMS and records signer principal.
- rbac-audit-linter: static analyzer that flags excessive role-permissions, missing residency tags, and suspicious role-attach patterns.

Post-incident: Review and Remediation
1) RCA playbook: SRE and Security to co-author timeline, root cause, and evidence index within 5 business days.
2) Remediation ticket must include: code fixes, SDLC gate improvements, additional monitoring, and a compliance evidence re-run to validate capture.
3) If failures are due to a process gap, update this playbook and re-run a tabletop in the next weekly game-day.

Checklist: Minimum Items for an Auditor Request (quick hit)
- Signed Evidence Bundle (S3 locked URL)
- Correlation ID and change-spec (YAML)
- Audit-event slice covering the incident window (ndjson)
- CI pipeline run + build artifact signatures
- RBAC pre/post diffs and access snapshots
- KMS access log slice and signer manifest
- Residency attestation or exception approval (if applicable)

Appendix A: Sample Change-spec (YAML)
---
id: rbac-change-2026-0420
author: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai
correlation_id: 9f2b1a...
reason: Enable model serving write role for predictor-v2
target_services: [predictor-v2, inference-router]
target_roles: [role/model-inference-writer]
residency_scopes: [eu]
rollout_strategy:
  canary_percent: 10
  canary_duration_h: 12
  auto_rollback_on_threshold: true
---

Appendix B: Common Playbook Commands (cheat sheet)
- Lock role changes: ./auth-api set-maintenance true --reason "incident {id}"
- Snapshot bindings: authctl list-bindings --format json --out /tmp/{id}-bindings.json
- Build evidence bundle: evidence-bundler --correlation-id {id} --out s3://evidence/locked/{id}.tar.gz
- Apply legal hold: aws s3api put-object-tagging --bucket evidence --key locked/{id}.tar.gz --tagging "LegalHold=true&Retention=10y"

Related reading and references
- RBAC pattern library: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth
- Audit schema contract: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/event-schema-contract-and-retention-runbook-2025
- SDLC gating standards: /confluence/security-and-compliance/secure-sdlc/sdlc-security-gates-rbac-audit-residency-checklist-2026

- 2025-11-02: Added automation CLI examples and retention table
- 2026-02-10: Finalized evidence bundle signing flow and legal-hold integration
