source.title: Integrated authZ and evidence playbook for blue-green deployments
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

This playbook documents the integrated authorization (authZ), audit-evidence, data residency, secure-SDLC signoffs, and incident-response steps for executing blue/green deployments of model-serving components or inference pipelines. It is intended for platform engineers, release owners, security, and compliance reviewers. The goal is to ensure every blue/green switch is auditable end-to-end, minimizes risk to data locality and entitlement drift, and provides forensic-ready artifacts for regulators and customers.

Scope and applicability

- Applies to: model-serving clusters, inference feature flags, model artifacts, and associated routing components across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.
- Excludes: data scientist experiments in notebooks (handled by the sandbox governance doc).
- Trigger conditions: any production change that results in a live model change, routing change, or tenancy-affecting configuration change.

Key objectives

1. Ensure authorization san checks and entitlements are recorded and validated prior to traffic cutover.
2. Capture immutable evidence bundle that demonstrates compliance with residency and RBAC controls at cutover time.
3. Provide rollback and incident triage procedures aligned with SRE and security incident processes.

Ownership and escalation

- Release owner: platform-release-engineering@example (primary contact).
- Security reviewer: Priya Natarajan (security) — evidence signoff.
- Compliance custodian: Finance-Legal QA (ad-hoc audits).
- SRE on-call: referenced by PagerDuty policy "model-serving-oncall".
- Escalation path: Release owner -> Security reviewer -> Head of SRE -> Legal (if data residency or customer impact).

Prerequisites (pre-cutover checklist)

1. RBAC attestations:
   - Ensure the targeted green cluster has the same or stricter IAM bindings as blue.
   - Export entitlement snapshot for the green cluster: CLI example:
     - rbacctl snapshot --cluster green --out /tmp/entitlements-green.json
2. Audit logging readiness:
   - Confirm audit-stream is connected and latest schema version is used (schema v3.4).
   - Verify pipeline backlog < 5 minutes for affected region.
3. Data residency safeguards:
   - Verify storage and logs for green are assigned to the intended data residency region (region tag and bucket policy).
4. Secure-SDLC gates:
   - PRD/Change request approved in Ticketing system (link).
   - Model artifact signature present and validated via cosign: cosign verify --key v1.pub model.tar.gz.sig
5. Compliance evidence staging:
   - Create a new evidence bundle ID: EB-<YYYYMMDD>-<RELEASE-ID> and reserve storage with retention policy TTL=7 years (or customer-specific retention).

Playbook: Blue-to-Green deployment step-by-step

1) Pre-cutover validation (T-30 to T-5 minutes)
   - 1.1 Freeze config changes in the release window.
   - 1.2 Authorization snapshot: capture and sign entitlements for both blue/green. Store checksums in the evidence bundle.
   - 1.3 Audit-forward check: run audit-forward-smoke to ensure events from green reach the audit collector.
   - 1.4 Residency assertion: run residency-checker --cluster green to confirm all storage endpoints match allowed geo list.
   - 1.5 Canary traffic test: route 1% of traffic to green using the traffic-router API and validate token-level telemetry for 10 minutes.

2) Cutover decision and approval (T=0)
   - 2.1 Release owner performs a gated approval in the Console. Approval requires two sign-offs: Release owner + Security reviewer. Approvals are recorded as audit events with reason text.
   - 2.2 System executes entitlement reconciliation: compare pre-snapshot to current bindings; if delta > threshold (see Entitlement thresholds table) automatic rollback triggers.

3) Evidence capture and packaging (T+0 to T+15 minutes)
   - 3.1 Gather artifacts into evidence bundle EB-<id>:
       - entitlement-snapshot.json (signed), audit-stream-log-window (T-15..T+15), model-artifact-signature, residency-assertion-report, deployment-manifest.yaml, operator-approvals.log
   - 3.2 Persist the bundle to the immutable evidence store with metadata and cryptographic binding. Example metadata fields: release_id, cluster, region, signer, ttl, related_ticket.
   - 3.3 Notify compliance channel with bundle link and automated attestations.

4) Post-cutover monitoring and rollback criteria (T+15 to T+60 minutes)
   - 4.1 SLO monitor: latency increase > 30% or error rate > 2% triggers automatic step-back to blue.
   - 4.2 AuthZ drift monitor: new entitlements observed in audit > allowed delta triggers immediate rollback and incident creation.
   - 4.3 Data residency exception: if any log or payload is written to disallowed region, immediate cut traffic to blue and invoke legal.

5) Incident response integration
   - 5.1 If rollback occurs, create incident with tag "deployment-rollback" and attach the evidence bundle.
   - 5.2 Forensic preservation: mark the audit stream window (T-30..T+60) as high-priority retention and escalate to forensic pipeline.
   - 5.3 Post-incident review: cross-functional RCA within 5 business days, include an evidence ingestion report for compliance.

Entitlement thresholds table

| Domain | Allowed delta (add/remove) | Action if delta exceeded |
|--------|----------------------------:|-------------------------|
| Service-role bindings | 2 entries | Block cutover + auto rollback |
| Customer-scoped entitlements | 0 entries | Block cutover and escalate to Legal |
| Service-to-service roles | 5 entries | Warn and require manual override by Security reviewer |

Audit logging and schema requirements

- Required fields for an auditable cutover event: event_id, timestamp, user_id, approver_ids, release_id, artifact_checksum, cluster, region, action, signed_blob_url.
- Log retention: cutover audit events must be retained according to the evidence bundle TTL.
- Immutable markers: evidence store writes produce a signed manifest and mirror to the immutable store (WORM) for legal hold use cases.

Data residency and retention considerations

- All logs that are packaged into an evidence bundle must contain an explicit region tag and pseudonymized user ids where applicable.
- If a customer requests a legal hold, the evidence bundle must be copied to the customer-specific vault with residency constraints preserved.

Exception handling: when to use the Risk & Exceptions process

- If the green cluster cannot be configured to meet an entitlement parity threshold within the release window.
- If audit pipeline backlog cannot be brought under 5 minutes prior to cutover.
- If residency validation fails but a business-critical customer requires the change.

Exception process (numbered)

1. File an Exception request ticket (template: EXC-DEPLOY) with: reason, mitigation controls, compensating controls, TTL, and owner.
2. Security triage within 4 business hours; if accepted, Security populates the evidence requirements and logging augmentation steps.
3. Executive approval required for exceptions that affect customer-scoped entitlements or cross-border data writes.

Templates and example CLI snippets

- Entitlement snapshot command:
  rbacctl snapshot --cluster green --out s3://evidence-buckets/EB-20260112/entitlements-green.json --sign --key /secrets/keys/release-signing.key

- Audit-forward smoke check:
  auditctl forward-test --region eu-west-1 --window 900 --expected-schema v3.4

- Evidence packaging (example manifest):
  manifest:
    release_id: REL-2026-117
    bundle_id: EB-20260112-REL117
    artifacts: [entitlement-snapshot.json, deployment-manifest.yaml, audit-window.ndjson, model.sig]

Checklist for compliance evidence reviewers

- Verify identity of signers against corporate directory.
- Confirm residency tags match the declared release region.
- Validate that the audit log window includes the approver event and that the approver event contains a reason field.
- Confirm artifact signatures and checksums match recorded values.

KPIs and SLOs referenced

- Audit stream latency: target < 30s, warning threshold 3m, cutoff > 5m.
- Cutover authorization turnaround: manual approvals should not exceed 15 minutes for low-risk releases.
- Evidence bundle availability: packaged artifact available and mirrored to immutable store within 15 minutes of cutover.

Post-deployment evidence lifecycle and retention

- Retention default: 7 years for production deployments. Overrides allowed per customer contract.
- Evidence access policy: RBAC required; only Security, Legal, Release owner, and Compliance custodian role ids may request direct download. All access events are audited and retained for 10 years.

Audit and periodic review cadence

- Quarterly spot checks: sample 10% of blue/green cutovers and validate evidence completeness.
- Annual audit: full evidence and retention policy review executed by Compliance and external auditor if required.

Appendix: quick reference

- Emergency rollback command:
  traffic-router cutback --from green --to blue --reason "slo-exceeded" --ticket REL-2026-117
- Evidence bundle retrieval:
  evidencectl fetch --bundle EB-20260112-REL117 --dest ./local-evidence --auth $(gcloud auth print-access-token)
- Contact list:
  - Security: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai
  - SRE oncall: model-serving-oncall@pagerduty
  - Compliance: compliance-ops@redwood.ai

If you are executing a blue/green deployment with special constraints (multi-region, regulated customer, or cross-account access) contact Security for a pre-cutover review at least 72 hours prior to the scheduled window.

title: Integrated authZ and evidence playbook for blue-green deployments
preview:
Overview

This playbook documents the integrated authorization (authZ), audit-evidence, data residency, secure-SDLC signoffs, and incident-response steps for executing blue/green deployments of model-serving components or inference pipelines. It is intended for platform engineers, release owners, security, and compliance reviewers. The goal is to ensure every blue/green switch is auditable end-to-end, minimizes risk to data locality and entitlement drift, and provides forensic-ready artifacts for regulators and customers.

Scope and applicability

- Applies to: model-serving clusters, inference feature flags, model artifacts, and associated routing components across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.
- Excludes: data scientist experiments in notebooks (handled by the sandbox governance doc).
- Trigger conditions: any production change that results in a live model change, routing change, or tenancy-affecting configuration change.

Key objectives

1. Ensure authorization san checks and entitlements are recorded and validated prior to traffic cutover.
2. Capture immutable evidence bundle that demonstrates compliance with residency and RBAC controls at cutover time.
3. Provide rollback and incident triage procedures aligned with SRE and security incident processes.

Ownership and escalation

- Release owner: platform-release-engineering@example (primary contact).
- Security reviewer: Priya Natarajan (security) — evidence signoff.
- Compliance custodian: Finance-Legal QA (ad-hoc audits).
- SRE on-call: referenced by PagerDuty policy \"model-serving-oncall\".
- Escalation path: Release owner -> Security reviewer -> Head of SRE -> Legal (if data residency or customer impact).

Prerequisites (pre-cutover checklist)

1. RBAC attestations:
   - Ensure the targeted green cluster has the same or stricter IAM bindings as blue.
   - Export entitlement snapshot for the green cluster: CLI example:
     - rbacctl snapshot --cluster green --out /tmp/entitlements-green.json
2. Audit logging readiness:
   - Confirm audit-stream is connected and latest schema version is used (schema v3.4).
   - Verify pipeline backlog < 5 minutes for affected region.
3. Data residency safeguards:
   - Verify storage and logs for green are assigned to the intended data residency region (region tag and bucket policy).
4. Secure-SDLC gates:
   - PRD/Change request approved in Ticketing system (link).
   - Model artifact signature present and validated via cosign: cosign verify --key v1.pub model.tar.gz.sig
5. Compliance evidence staging:
   - Create a new evidence bundle ID: EB-<YYYYMMDD>-<RELEASE-ID> and reserve storage with retention policy TTL=7 years (or customer-specific retention).

Playbook: Blue-to-Green deployment step-by-step

1) Pre-cutover validation (T-30 to T-5 minutes)
   - 1.1 Freeze config changes in the release window.
   - 1.2 Authorization snapshot: capture and sign entitlements for both blue/green. Store checksums in the evidence bundle.
   - 1.3 Audit-forward check: run audit-forward-smoke to ensure events from green reach the audit collector.
   - 1.4 Residency assertion: run residency-checker --cluster green to confirm all storage endpoints match allowed geo list.
   - 1.5 Canary traffic test: route 1% of traffic to green using the traffic-router API and validate token-level telemetry for 10 minutes.

2) Cutover decision and approval (T=0)
   - 2.1 Release owner performs a gated approval in the Console. Approval requires two sign-offs: Release owner + Security reviewer. Approvals are recorded as audit events with reason text.
   - 2.2 System executes entitlement reconciliation: compare pre-snapshot to current bindings; if delta > threshold (see Entitlement thresholds table) automatic rollback triggers.

3) Evidence capture and packaging (T+0 to T+15 minutes)
   - 3.1 Gather artifacts into evidence bundle EB-<id>:
       - entitlement-snapshot.json (signed), audit-stream-log-window (T-15..T+15), model-artifact-signature, residency-assertion-report, deployment-manifest.yaml, operator-approvals.log
   - 3.2 Persist the bundle to the immutable evidence store with metadata and cryptographic binding. Example metadata fields: release_id, cluster, region, signer, ttl, related_ticket.
   - 3.3 Notify compliance channel with bundle link and automated attestations.

4) Post-cutover monitoring and rollback criteria (T+15 to T+60 minutes)
   - 4.1 SLO monitor: latency increase > 30% or error rate > 2% triggers automatic step-back to blue.
   - 4.2 AuthZ drift monitor: new entitlements observed in audit > allowed delta triggers immediate rollback and incident creation.
   - 4.3 Data residency exception: if any log or payload is written to disallowed region, immediate cut traffic to blue and invoke legal.

5) Incident response integration
   - 5.1 If rollback occurs, create incident with tag \"deployment-rollback\" and attach the evidence bundle.
   - 5.2 Forensic preservation: mark the audit stream window (T-30..T+60) as high-priority retention and escalate to forensic pipeline.
   - 5.3 Post-incident review: cross-functional RCA within 5 business days, include an evidence ingestion report for compliance.

Entitlement thresholds table

| Domain | Allowed delta (add/remove) | Action if delta exceeded |
|--------|----------------------------:|-------------------------|
| Service-role bindings | 2 entries | Block cutover + auto rollback |
| Customer-scoped entitlements | 0 entries | Block cutover and escalate to Legal |
| Service-to-service roles | 5 entries | Warn and require manual override by Security reviewer |

Audit logging and schema requirements

- Required fields for an auditable cutover event: event_id, timestamp, user_id, approver_ids, release_id, artifact_checksum, cluster, region, action, signed_blob_url.
- Log retention: cutover audit events must be retained according to the evidence bundle TTL.
- Immutable markers: evidence store writes produce a signed manifest and mirror to the immutable store (WORM) for legal hold use cases.

Data residency and retention considerations

- All logs that are packaged into an evidence bundle must contain an explicit region tag and pseudonymized user ids where applicable.
- If a customer requests a legal hold, the evidence bundle must be copied to the customer-specific vault with residency constraints preserved.

Exception handling: when to use the Risk & Exceptions process

- If the green cluster cannot be configured to meet an entitlement parity threshold within the release window.
- If audit pipeline backlog cannot be brought under 5 minutes prior to cutover.
- If residency validation fails but a business-critical customer requires the change.

Exception process (numbered)

1. File an Exception request ticket (template: EXC-DEPLOY) with: reason, mitigation controls, compensating controls, TTL, and owner.
2. Security triage within 4 business hours; if accepted, Security populates the evidence requirements and logging augmentation steps.
3. Executive approval required for exceptions that affect customer-scoped entitlements or cross-border data writes.

Templates and example CLI snippets

- Entitlement snapshot command:
  rbacctl snapshot --cluster green --out s3://evidence-buckets/EB-20260112/entitlements-green.json --sign --key /secrets/keys/release-signing.key

- Audit-forward smoke check:
  auditctl forward-test --region eu-west-1 --window 900 --expected-schema v3.4

- Evidence packaging (example manifest):
  manifest:
    release_id: REL-2026-117
    bundle_id: EB-20260112-REL117
    artifacts: [entitlement-snapshot.json, deployment-manifest.yaml, audit-window.ndjson, model.sig]

Checklist for compliance evidence reviewers

- Verify identity of signers against corporate directory.
- Confirm residency tags match the declared release region.
- Validate that the audit log window includes the approver event and that the approver event contains a reason field.
- Confirm artifact signatures and checksums match recorded values.

KPIs and SLOs referenced

- Audit stream latency: target < 30s, warning threshold 3m, cutoff > 5m.
- Cutover authorization turnaround: manual approvals should not exceed 15 minutes for low-risk releases.
- Evidence bundle availability: packaged artifact available and mirrored to immutable store within 15 minutes of cutover.

Post-deployment evidence lifecycle and retention

- Retention default: 7 years for production deployments. Overrides allowed per customer contract.
- Evidence access policy: RBAC required; only Security, Legal, Release owner, and Compliance custodian role ids may request direct download. All access events are audited and retained for 10 years.

Audit and periodic review cadence

- Quarterly spot checks: sample 10% of blue/green cutovers and validate evidence completeness.
- Annual audit: full evidence and retention policy review executed by Compliance and external auditor if required.

Appendix: quick reference

- Emergency rollback command:
  traffic-router cutback --from green --to blue --reason \"slo-exceeded\" --ticket REL-2026-117
- Evidence bundle retrieval:
  evidencectl fetch --bundle EB-20260112-REL117 --dest ./local-evidence --auth $(gcloud auth print-access-token)
- Contact list:
  - Security: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai
  - SRE oncall: model-serving-oncall@pagerduty
  - Compliance: compliance-ops@redwood.ai

If you are executing a blue/green deployment with special constraints (multi-region, regulated customer, or cross-account access) contact Security for a pre-cutover review at least 72 hours prior to the scheduled window.

source.title: Authorization Snapshotting and Retention Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

This playbook defines a cross-functional standard for creating, storing, and packaging point-in-time snapshots of authorization state and related telemetry across Redwood Inference services. It covers: RBAC and auth configuration capture, associated audit log evidence, retention schedules with data residency considerations, SDLC gates to ensure evidenceable changes, incident response usage, and exception handling. The goal is reproducible artifacts for audits and investigations while minimizing surface area for sensitive data exposure.

Scope and Intent

- Systems: identity provider (IdP), internal entitlement service (entd), gateway authz policies, tenant metadata store, CI/CD change history, and audit log pipelines (Splunk/BigQuery).
- Use cases: compliance audits, breach investigation, post-incident root cause, quarterly evidence packaging for regulated customers.
- Exclusions: application-level feature flags unrelated to access control, ephemeral debug traces older than 48 hours unless tagged for incident triage.

Definitions

- Authorization Snapshot: an immutable bundle containing RBAC configuration, role bindings, service-account keys metadata, and a time-anchored pointer to corresponding audit log segments.
- Evidence Bundle: the Snapshot plus audit log extracts, change-provenance records, and retention metadata required to demonstrate control operation and enforcement.

Principles

1) Minimal exposure: sanitize secrets and PII before storing snapshots in long-term evidence storage.
2) Verifiable lineage: every Snapshot must include commit hashes, CI run IDs, and a hash of the snapshot file for tamper detection.
3) Region-aware retention: follow data residency rules per tenant/region. Evidence for EU tenants must remain in EU-bound storage.
4) Operational usability: incident responders must be able to obtain a Snapshot within 1 hour (target) for active incidents.

Retention and Residency Matrix (summary)

| Artifact Type | Retention (default) | Retention (sensitive tenants) | Storage Location | Notes |
|--------------|--------------------:|------------------------------:|------------------|------|
| RBAC config snapshots | 3 years | 7 years (gov customers) | encrypted S3 / region-specific bucket | include commit hash and signer id |
| Audit log extracts (index pointers) | 1 year | 7 years | long-term log retention (BigQuery EU/US) | extract uses time-windowed export to preserve PII controls |
| Evidence bundles (packaged) | 7 years | 10 years | secure evidence vault (KMS-protected) | only accessible to security+legal roles |
| Ephemeral debug traces | 14 days | 14 days | ephemeral store | truncated / redacted by default |

Snapshot Creation: process and automation

Owner: Eng Security (sec-engineering) / entitlement-service SRE
Frequency: automated nightly snapshots + on-change snapshots triggered by PR merge to main affecting auth code or config

Steps (automated):
1. Pull canonical RBAC objects from entd API: roles, role-bindings, policy versions.
2. Export IdP group-to-claim mapping and service account metadata (rotate keys excluded; only metadata fingerprints).
3. Record commit hashes for any infra-as-code (Terraform) or config repos used to apply changes.
4. Trigger export of relevant audit log window: +/- 15 minutes around change events; for nightly snapshots export that day’s activity window.
5. Build snapshot.json and compute SHA256 (store SHA in snapshot manifest).
6. Sanitize: apply redaction rules (PII patterns, secrets).
7. Encrypt with evidence-vault KMS key and store in region-appropriate bucket.

CLI example (pseudo):

# create a one-off snapshot for tenant=acme-corp
redwood-cli authz snapshot create --tenant acme-corp --window 2026-02-01T00:00:00Z/2026-02-01T23:59:59Z --output /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.json

# compute verification hash
sha256sum /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.json > /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.sha256

Evidence Packaging: checklist for audit-ready bundles

Required contents for an Evidence Bundle:
- snapshot.json (RBAC + metadata)
- snapshot.sha256 (hash)
- audit-extract.ndjson (time-bounded)
- ci-provenance.txt (CI run ID, commit hashes, PR ids)
- retention-manifest.yaml (artifact type, retention expiration iso8601, storage region)
- signer-record.json (service identity that created snapshot)

Packaging procedure (numbered):
1. Assemble files into a timestamped directory naming convention: evidence/{tenant}/{YYYYMMDDThhmmss}Z/
2. Generate a signed manifest using the evidence service's signing key: evidence-sign sign --path evidence/...
3. Store package in evidence-vault and update the global evidence index with storage pointer and access controls.
4. Notify security-legal slack channel with read-only link for auditors.

Audit Log Integration and Query Patterns

- Logging sources: gateway-authz (envoy filter), entd change events, IdP SCIM events, CI/CD apply logs, infrastructure orchestration logs.
- Recommended index fields: timestamp, tenant_id, principal_type, principal_id, action, resource_id, decision, policy_version, request_id

Example Splunk/BigQuery query (pseudo):
SELECT timestamp, principal_id, action, resource_id, decision, policy_version
FROM `redwood-logs.authz_decisions`
WHERE tenant_id = "acme-corp" AND timestamp BETWEEN "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z" AND "2026-02-01T00:30:00Z"
ORDER BY timestamp ASC

Secure SDLC and Change Controls

Gates to ensure snapshotability and evidenceability:
- Any Terraform/Helm change touching entd or gateway-authz requires: PR checklist items (unit tests + canary policy tests + evidence generation script updated if schema changes).
- All changes that alter role definitions must be accompanied by a smallfootprint change-provenance file committed to the repo (provenance.json) that lists who approved, when, and link to design doc.
- CI step: after merge, CI job must call "redwood-cli authz snapshot create --auto" to create the post-merge snapshot and attach evidence to the merge commit.

Incident Response: using snapshots during investigations

- Priority: for a live incident impacting authorization decisions, incident commander (IC) can request a fast-track snapshot (target delivery < 60 minutes).
- Fast-track steps:
  a. Incident responder runs redwood-cli authz snapshot create --fast --window <incident-window>.
  b. Evidence automation exports an audit-log slice with increased fidelity (including debug tokens) to a short-lived secure bucket.
  c. Security engineer verifies SHA and attaches snapshot to incident ticket.
- Preservation: mark the fast-track evidence as immutable for the duration of the investigation.

Risk and Exceptions Workflow

- Risk scoring: each authorization change is scored for blast radius (tenant scope, service impact), sensitivity (PII scope), and rollback complexity. Changes scoring > threshold require security review and extended retention of snapshots.
- Exception handling:
  1. Submit exception request via the Risk Portal with fields: component, justification, requested retention deviation, compensating controls.
  2. Security reviews request within 5 business days.
  3. If approved, add explicit retention override and link to evidence bundle; log decision in exception register.

Operational Roles and Responsibilities

- Security (sec-engineering): owns snapshot tooling, evidence-vault, and retention enforcement.
- Entitlement SRE: ensures entd exports are available and validates snapshot integrity.
- Infra/Platform: configures regional buckets and KMS keys.
- Legal/Compliance: approves evidence retention schedules for customers under contract.
- Incident Response: consumes snapshots and coordinates preservation for investigations.

Access Control for Evidence

- Principle of least privilege: evidence-vault access is RBAC-gated (security:read, security:manage, legal:read).
- Access approvals are timeboxed (max 30 days) and recorded in the evidence index with a justification string.

Tamper Detection and Monitoring

- Each Snapshot manifest stores SHA256 and signer metadata. Changes to stored artifacts trigger a notarization job that re-verifies checksums daily. Any mismatch opens a P1 investigation workflow.
- Monitoring alerts: evidence-integrity-alert (pager), snapshot-creation-failure (slack to #sec-tooling), retention-expiry-warning (email to data-retention-steerco).

Sampling and Cost Controls

- For low-risk tenants, full snapshotting may be sampled (e.g., weekly) with metadata snapshots daily. Sampling configuration stored in retention-manifest.yaml and requires a documented risk acceptance.

Appendices

A. Redaction rules summary:
- Remove full email local-parts unless tenant consents.
- Replace service account key material with fingerprints and last-rotate timestamps.
- Strip request bodies unless flagged by incident responder.

B. Example retention-manifest.yaml (excerpt):
---
artifact: rbac-snapshot
tenant: acme-corp
created_at: 2026-02-01T02:05:00Z
retention_expires: 2029-02-01T02:05:00Z
storage_region: eu-west-1
redaction_level: standard
---

C. Troubleshooting quickwins
- Snapshot creation fails: check entd API tokens and evidence-vault KMS permissions.
- Hash mismatch on verification: do not delete artifact; open an evidence-integrity P1 and preserve both the artifact and system logs.

D. Change log
- 2025-11-15: Playbook created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2026-02-02: Added fast-track incident snapshot path and tamper-detection alerting (reviewers: Ethan Caldwell, Marta Ruiz).

Contact and escalation

- Primary: sec-engineering Slack: #sec-tooling, PagerDuty: sec-oncall
- Evidence vault owner: evidence-service@redwood.ai

title: Authorization Snapshotting and Retention Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook defines a cross-functional standard for creating, storing, and packaging point-in-time snapshots of authorization state and related telemetry across Redwood Inference services. It covers: RBAC and auth configuration capture, associated audit log evidence, retention schedules with data residency considerations, SDLC gates to ensure evidenceable changes, incident response usage, and exception handling. The goal is reproducible artifacts for audits and investigations while minimizing surface area for sensitive data exposure.

Scope and Intent

- Systems: identity provider (IdP), internal entitlement service (entd), gateway authz policies, tenant metadata store, CI/CD change history, and audit log pipelines (Splunk/BigQuery).
- Use cases: compliance audits, breach investigation, post-incident root cause, quarterly evidence packaging for regulated customers.
- Exclusions: application-level feature flags unrelated to access control, ephemeral debug traces older than 48 hours unless tagged for incident triage.

Definitions

- Authorization Snapshot: an immutable bundle containing RBAC configuration, role bindings, service-account keys metadata, and a time-anchored pointer to corresponding audit log segments.
- Evidence Bundle: the Snapshot plus audit log extracts, change-provenance records, and retention metadata required to demonstrate control operation and enforcement.

Principles

1) Minimal exposure: sanitize secrets and PII before storing snapshots in long-term evidence storage.
2) Verifiable lineage: every Snapshot must include commit hashes, CI run IDs, and a hash of the snapshot file for tamper detection.
3) Region-aware retention: follow data residency rules per tenant/region. Evidence for EU tenants must remain in EU-bound storage.
4) Operational usability: incident responders must be able to obtain a Snapshot within 1 hour (target) for active incidents.

Retention and Residency Matrix (summary)

| Artifact Type | Retention (default) | Retention (sensitive tenants) | Storage Location | Notes |
|--------------|--------------------:|------------------------------:|------------------|------|
| RBAC config snapshots | 3 years | 7 years (gov customers) | encrypted S3 / region-specific bucket | include commit hash and signer id |
| Audit log extracts (index pointers) | 1 year | 7 years | long-term log retention (BigQuery EU/US) | extract uses time-windowed export to preserve PII controls |
| Evidence bundles (packaged) | 7 years | 10 years | secure evidence vault (KMS-protected) | only accessible to security+legal roles |
| Ephemeral debug traces | 14 days | 14 days | ephemeral store | truncated / redacted by default |

Snapshot Creation: process and automation

Owner: Eng Security (sec-engineering) / entitlement-service SRE
Frequency: automated nightly snapshots + on-change snapshots triggered by PR merge to main affecting auth code or config

Steps (automated):
1. Pull canonical RBAC objects from entd API: roles, role-bindings, policy versions.
2. Export IdP group-to-claim mapping and service account metadata (rotate keys excluded; only metadata fingerprints).
3. Record commit hashes for any infra-as-code (Terraform) or config repos used to apply changes.
4. Trigger export of relevant audit log window: +/- 15 minutes around change events; for nightly snapshots export that day’s activity window.
5. Build snapshot.json and compute SHA256 (store SHA in snapshot manifest).
6. Sanitize: apply redaction rules (PII patterns, secrets).
7. Encrypt with evidence-vault KMS key and store in region-appropriate bucket.

CLI example (pseudo):

# create a one-off snapshot for tenant=acme-corp
redwood-cli authz snapshot create --tenant acme-corp --window 2026-02-01T00:00:00Z/2026-02-01T23:59:59Z --output /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.json

# compute verification hash
sha256sum /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.json > /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.sha256

Evidence Packaging: checklist for audit-ready bundles

Required contents for an Evidence Bundle:
- snapshot.json (RBAC + metadata)
- snapshot.sha256 (hash)
- audit-extract.ndjson (time-bounded)
- ci-provenance.txt (CI run ID, commit hashes, PR ids)
- retention-manifest.yaml (artifact type, retention expiration iso8601, storage region)
- signer-record.json (service identity that created snapshot)

Packaging procedure (numbered):
1. Assemble files into a timestamped directory naming convention: evidence/{tenant}/{YYYYMMDDThhmmss}Z/
2. Generate a signed manifest using the evidence service's signing key: evidence-sign sign --path evidence/...
3. Store package in evidence-vault and update the global evidence index with storage pointer and access controls.
4. Notify security-legal slack channel with read-only link for auditors.

Audit Log Integration and Query Patterns

- Logging sources: gateway-authz (envoy filter), entd change events, IdP SCIM events, CI/CD apply logs, infrastructure orchestration logs.
- Recommended index fields: timestamp, tenant_id, principal_type, principal_id, action, resource_id, decision, policy_version, request_id

Example Splunk/BigQuery query (pseudo):
SELECT timestamp, principal_id, action, resource_id, decision, policy_version
FROM `redwood-logs.authz_decisions`
WHERE tenant_id = "acme-corp" AND timestamp BETWEEN "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z" AND "2026-02-01T00:30:00Z"
ORDER BY timestamp ASC

Secure SDLC and Change Controls

Gates to ensure snapshotability and evidenceability:
- Any Terraform/Helm change touching entd or gateway-authz requires: PR checklist items (unit tests + canary policy tests + evidence generation script updated if schema changes).
- All changes that alter role definitions must be accompanied by a smallfootprint change-provenance file committed to the repo (provenance.json) that lists who approved, when, and link to design doc.
- CI step: after merge, CI job must call "redwood-cli authz snapshot create --auto" to create the post-merge snapshot and attach evidence to the merge commit.

Incident Response: using snapshots during investigations

- Priority: for a live incident impacting authorization decisions, incident commander (IC) can request a fast-track snapshot (target delivery < 60 minutes).
- Fast-track steps:
  a. Incident responder runs redwood-cli authz snapshot create --fast --window <incident-window>.
  b. Evidence automation exports an audit-log slice with increased fidelity (including debug tokens) to a short-lived secure bucket.
  c. Security engineer verifies SHA and attaches snapshot to incident ticket.
- Preservation: mark the fast-track evidence as immutable for the duration of the investigation.

Risk and Exceptions Workflow

- Risk scoring: each authorization change is scored for blast radius (tenant scope, service impact), sensitivity (PII scope), and rollback complexity. Changes scoring > threshold require security review and extended retention of snapshots.
- Exception handling:
  1. Submit exception request via the Risk Portal with fields: component, justification, requested retention deviation, compensating controls.
  2. Security reviews request within 5 business days.
  3. If approved, add explicit retention override and link to evidence bundle; log decision in exception register.

Operational Roles and Responsibilities

- Security (sec-engineering): owns snapshot tooling, evidence-vault, and retention enforcement.
- Entitlement SRE: ensures entd exports are available and validates snapshot integrity.
- Infra/Platform: configures regional buckets and KMS keys.
- Legal/Compliance: approves evidence retention schedules for customers under contract.
- Incident Response: consumes snapshots and coordinates preservation for investigations.

Access Control for Evidence

- Principle of least privilege: evidence-vault access is RBAC-gated (security:read, security:manage, legal:read).
- Access approvals are timeboxed (max 30 days) and recorded in the evidence index with a justification string.

Tamper Detection and Monitoring

- Each Snapshot manifest stores SHA256 and signer metadata. Changes to stored artifacts trigger a notarization job that re-verifies checksums daily. Any mismatch opens a P1 investigation workflow.
- Monitoring alerts: evidence-integrity-alert (pager), snapshot-creation-failure (slack to #sec-tooling), retention-expiry-warning (email to data-retention-steerco).

Sampling and Cost Controls

- For low-risk tenants, full snapshotting may be sampled (e.g., weekly) with metadata snapshots daily. Sampling configuration stored in retention-manifest.yaml and requires a documented risk acceptance.

Appendices

A. Redaction rules summary:
- Remove full email local-parts unless tenant consents.
- Replace service account key material with fingerprints and last-rotate timestamps.
- Strip request bodies unless flagged by incident responder.

B. Example retention-manifest.yaml (excerpt):
---
artifact: rbac-snapshot
tenant: acme-corp
created_at: 2026-02-01T02:05:00Z
retention_expires: 2029-02-01T02:05:00Z
storage_region: eu-west-1
redaction_level: standard
---

C. Troubleshooting quickwins
- Snapshot creation fails: check entd API tokens and evidence-vault KMS permissions.
- Hash mismatch on verification: do not delete artifact; open an evidence-integrity P1 and preserve both the artifact and system logs.

D. Change log
- 2025-11-15: Playbook created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2026-02-02: Added fast-track incident snapshot path and tamper-detection alerting (reviewers: Ethan Caldwell, Marta Ruiz).

Contact and escalation

- Primary: sec-engineering Slack: #sec-tooling, PagerDuty: sec-oncall
- Evidence vault owner: evidence-service@redwood.ai

Related policies and links

- RBAC standard (link internal): /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/least-privilege-evidence-and-access-risk-playbook-2026
- Audit log retention policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/immutable-audit-store-design-evidence-catalog-2026
- SDLC gating process: /confluence/security-and-compliance/secure-sdlc/sdlc-data-residency-audit-artifacts-manifest-2026

End of document

source.title: Exception process: emergency unsigned (hotfix) offline bundle for Redwood Private
source.author: Olga Petrov
source.content:
## Purpose
This page defines the **break-glass exception process** for shipping an **emergency unsigned offline bundle** ("unsigned hotfix bundle") for **Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)** when the standard bundle signing process is unavailable.

**Default policy:** All Redwood Private offline bundles MUST be signed and must fail closed in the installer when signature verification fails.

**Exception:** An unsigned bundle may be shipped only under the strict conditions and approvals below, with mandatory compensating controls, customer communication, and follow-up remediation.

This process exists to:
- Enable urgent customer impact fixes when signing is temporarily blocked.
- Control and document supply-chain risk.
- Produce auditable evidence for customers and internal compliance.

---

## Scope
Applies to:
- **Offline bundles** distributed to customers for air-gapped or restricted-network environments.
- **Hotfix** releases required for security vulnerabilities, data loss risk, severe availability incidents, or legal/compliance blockers.

Does not apply to:
- Standard signed releases.
- Customer-controlled internal artifact repackaging (customer actions outside Redwood distribution).
- Break-glass install flags on the customer side (covered in installer policy and runbooks).

---

## Definitions
- **Offline bundle:** The packaged set of artifacts used to install/upgrade Redwood Private (installer, charts/manifests, image references, runtime artifacts, model/runtime dependencies, SBOM pointer, etc.).
- **Unsigned hotfix bundle:** An offline bundle that is distributed **without Redwood’s cryptographic signature** due to an exception.
- **Compensating controls:** Additional verification steps (hash validation, controlled distribution, access restrictions, enhanced audit logging) required when signature-based integrity verification is not available.

---

## Policy statement
1) **Unsigned offline bundles are prohibited by default.**
2) **Unsigned offline bundles may be shipped only under an approved exception** and must:
   - Be limited in scope, time, and recipients.
   - Use compensating controls described in this document.
   - Include a committed plan and deadlines for a **signed replacement**.
3) Any use of this exception is treated as a **Security/GRC event** and must be recorded for audit purposes.

---

## Acceptable reasons (eligibility criteria)
An exception request is eligible only if ALL of the following are true:
- **Customer impact is high/urgent**, e.g.:
  - Critical security vulnerability (actively exploited or high confidence risk).
  - Severity-1 incident impacting production installs/upgrades.
  - Data corruption/loss risk.
  - Regulatory/legal deadline where delay would materially harm customer.
- **Standard signing is blocked** due to one of:
  - Signing key infrastructure outage/unavailability (HSM/KMS access failure).
  - Release pipeline break preventing signing with no timely workaround.
  - Emergency incident response requiring immediate patch distribution before signing path can be restored.
- **No safe alternative exists**, such as:
  - Shipping a signed bundle via an alternate signing environment.
  - Reverting to last known good signed bundle.
  - Using a signed minimal mitigation release.

Not eligible:
- Convenience, schedule pressure, non-urgent feature delivery.
- Avoiding a normal security review.
- Shipping unsigned because the signing process is “not finished”.

---

## Approval matrix (who must approve)
The approvers below provide explicit, recorded approval (ticket + written acknowledgment). Approvals may not be delegated without Security leadership consent.

| Decision / Step | Required approvers | Optional / FYI |
|---|---|---|
| Open exception request + initial triage | Release Eng Lead (Connor O'Brien), Security Eng Lead (Marcus Lin) | Product Owner Private (Vivek Kulkarni), SRE Lead (Sean Gallagher) |
| Risk acceptance to distribute unsigned bundle | Security Exec Sponsor (Dr. Aisha Rahman), GRC Lead (Olga Petrov), Release Eng Lead (Connor O'Brien) | Infra/SRE Exec Sponsor (Neha Kapoor) |
| Customer communication approval (template + recipients) | Product Owner Private (Vivek Kulkarni), Support Readiness Lead (Dev Patel), GRC Lead (Olga Petrov) | Customer Success Lead (Rachel Kim), Solutions Lead (Fatima Noor) |
| Final “go/no-go” on distribution | Security Exec Sponsor (Dr. Aisha Rahman) AND Release Eng Lead (Connor O'Brien) | CEO/Legal only if contractually required |

**Quorum rule:** If any required approver is unavailable, the exception is blocked unless Dr. Aisha Rahman and Connor O’Brien both approve an alternate approver in writing.

---

## Required artifacts and evidence (must be produced)
For each unsigned bundle exception, the release owner (Release Eng) must produce and store the following:

1) **Exception ticket** (single source of truth)
- Title format: `EXC-UNSIGNED-BUNDLE: <Customer/Segment> <Bundle version> <Reason>`
- Must include: impact, timeline, affected customers, mitigation plan, and risk acknowledgment.

2) **Bundle identity + immutable metadata**
- Bundle version (e.g., `private-2026.01.0-hotfix.1`)
- Build ID / CI run ID
- Git commit SHAs for installer/charts/runtime as applicable
- Artifact list (what is included)
- Intended recipients (named accounts)

3) **Compensating integrity verification package (hash manifest)**
Because signature verification is not available, Redwood must provide a **separate integrity verification package**:
- `SHA256SUMS` (or equivalent) covering:
  - Offline bundle archive
  - Internal bundle manifest
  - Installer binary (if shipped separately)
  - Any chart tarballs or dependency packages
  - SBOM file/pointer artifact if included
- Hashes must be generated on a controlled build runner and verified by a second operator.

4) **Two-person verification record**
- Operator A generates hashes and attaches them to the ticket.
- Operator B independently recomputes hashes from the staged artifacts and confirms match.
- Both sign off with timestamp and workstation/build environment identifier.

5) **Distribution controls record**
- Where the bundle is hosted (internal restricted bucket/release portal)
- Access control list (ACL) snapshot: who can download
- Expiration date for access

6) **Customer communication record**
- Approved message sent, time sent, recipients, and acknowledgement if required.

7) **Remediation plan with deadlines**
- Target date for restoring signing pipeline
- Target date for issuing a signed replacement bundle
- Plan for revocation/withdrawal of unsigned bundle

---

## Compensating controls (required)
When shipping an unsigned bundle, Redwood must apply ALL of the controls below.

### 1) Strict distribution limitations
- **Limit recipients** to explicitly approved accounts/environments.
- **Limit time window**: bundle access should expire within **7 calendar days** unless re-approved.
- Do not post unsigned bundles to broad download portals or public locations.

### 2) Out-of-band integrity verification instructions
Provide customers with:
- A hash file (e.g., `SHA256SUMS`) and verification steps.
- A clear statement: “Integrity must be verified before install; do not proceed if hashes do not match.”

Recommended customer commands (Linux example):
- `sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS`
- Or `sha256sum <bundle>.tar.zst` and compare with provided value.

### 3) Controlled transport guidance
Customers must be instructed to:
- Avoid tools known to mutate archives.
- Prefer binary-safe transport (e.g., secure copy to removable media, verify hashes after transfer).

### 4) Explicit risk labeling in artifacts
- Bundle filename must include `UNSIGNED` (example):
  - `redwood-private-2026.01.0-hotfix.1-UNSIGNED-offline-bundle.tar.zst`
- Include `UNSIGNED_README.txt` at bundle root stating:
  - Why unsigned
  - Required verification steps
  - Expiration / replacement plan

### 5) Installer behavior
- The installer must continue to **fail closed** on signature verification by default.
- If the customer must proceed without signature validation, this must be handled via the installer’s documented break-glass path (separately governed), and must be logged locally as a break-glass event.

---

## Risk acknowledgment (required language)
Approvers must acknowledge the following in the exception ticket:

- “We understand that shipping an unsigned offline bundle increases the risk of undetected tampering in transit or at rest.”
- “We confirm that the signed-bundle pipeline is currently blocked and that no safe signed alternative is feasible within the required timeline.”
- “We commit to issuing a signed replacement bundle by <DATE> and to withdrawing the unsigned bundle from distribution by <DATE>.”
- “We confirm compensating controls are in place: restricted distribution, hash-based verification, two-person verification, and documented customer communication.”

---

## Procedure
### Step 0 — Initiate exception
Owner: Connor O'Brien (Release Engineering) or delegate on the release train.

Create the exception ticket and include:
- Severity/impact summary
- Root cause of signing blockage (what failed)
- Why delay is unacceptable
- Proposed unsigned bundle version + recipients

### Step 1 — Security triage
Owner: Marcus Lin (Security Engineering)

Validate:
- Eligibility criteria
- Threat considerations (tampering, replay/downgrade risk)
- Whether additional controls are required for the specific customer environment

### Step 2 — Prepare unsigned bundle + hash manifest
Owner: Release Engineering

- Produce the bundle using deterministic build steps as usual.
- Generate `SHA256SUMS` for all required artifacts.
- Complete two-person verification.

### Step 3 — Obtain approvals
Owner: Olga Petrov (GRC) to coordinate approvals

- Collect approvals per matrix.
- Ensure risk acknowledgment language is included verbatim.

### Step 4 — Customer communication
Owner: Vivek Kulkarni (Product Private) + Dev Patel (Support)

Send approved message before sharing download links.

Minimum content:
- Reason for unsigned distribution
- Required hash verification steps
- Support contact path
- Replacement plan and expected timeline

### Step 5 — Distribute with access controls
Owner: Release Engineering

- Host in restricted location.
- Provide customer with:
  - Bundle
  - `SHA256SUMS`
  - Verification instructions
  - `UNSIGNED_README.txt`

### Step 6 — Track completion + remediation
Owner: Release Engineering + Security

- Confirm customer verified hashes and completed install/upgrade.
- Restore signing pipeline.
- Ship signed replacement bundle.
- Withdraw access to unsigned bundle.

---

## Customer communication templates
### Template A — Initial notice (email)
Subject: `[Action required] Redwood Private hotfix bundle integrity verification (unsigned emergency release)`

Body:
- We are providing an emergency hotfix bundle to address <ISSUE>.
- Due to <SIGNING BLOCKER>, this bundle is **unsigned**. This is an exception to Redwood’s standard signed-bundle process.
- Before installation, you must verify integrity using the provided `SHA256SUMS` file:
  1) Transfer both the bundle and `SHA256SUMS` to your offline environment.
  2) Run: `sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS`
  3) Do not proceed if any verification fails.
- We will provide a **signed replacement** by <DATE> and will retire this unsigned bundle by <DATE>.
- If you have any questions or verification failures, contact <Support alias/SE contact> and include the verification output.

### Template B — Follow-up (signed replacement available)
Subject: `Signed replacement available: Redwood Private <version> offline bundle`

Body:
- A signed replacement bundle is now available: <LINK/DELIVERY METHOD>.
- Please prefer the signed bundle for any new installs/upgrades.
- The previous unsigned bundle will be removed from distribution on <DATE>.

---

## Remediation requirements (mandatory)
Within **5 business days** after distribution of any unsigned bundle, Release Engineering must schedule and complete a remediation review with Security + GRC.

### Remediation checklist
- [ ] Root cause analysis: why signing was blocked (technical + process)
- [ ] Confirm the signed pipeline is restored OR a permanent workaround exists
- [ ] Issue a signed replacement bundle and validate verification end-to-end
- [ ] Withdraw unsigned bundle distribution and document withdrawal date/time
- [ ] Confirm customer has migrated (or documented why not)
- [ ] Update runbooks/tests to prevent recurrence
- [ ] File follow-up tickets for any process gaps (approvals, evidence, access controls)

### Evidence to attach after remediation
- Link to signed replacement release artifact
- Confirmation of unsigned bundle removal
- Summary of customer outcome (install success + any issues)

---

## Audit and retention
- Exception tickets and evidence must be retained for **7 years** (or longer if customer contract requires).
- All approvals must be recorded in writing (ticket comments are acceptable if identities are validated).
- Access logs for downloads must be retained for at least **1 year**.

---

## Escalation and incident handling
If an unsigned bundle is suspected to be tampered with, leaked, or redistributed beyond approved recipients:
1) Notify Security on-call and Release Engineering immediately.
2) Stop distribution (revoke links/credentials).
3) Initiate an incident: “Potential supply-chain compromise (unsigned bundle exception)”.
4) Provide customers with new verified artifacts and guidance.

---

## FAQ
**Q: Can we ship an unsigned bundle to keep a release train on schedule?**
No. Schedule pressure is not an eligible reason.

**Q: Can we provide only a single hash of the bundle archive?**
No. At minimum, provide a hash for the archive and the internal manifest; additionally provide hashes for critical install components (installer, charts/deps) to support targeted verification and troubleshooting.

**Q: Can Support approve this exception?**
No. Support participates in readiness and communication, but Security leadership, GRC, and Release Engineering approvals are mandatory.

**Q: Does this change the installer verification policy?**
No. The installer must continue to treat missing/invalid signatures as a failure by default; any customer-side override remains separately governed and must be audited.

title: Exception process: emergency unsigned (hotfix) offline bundle for Redwood Private
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **break-glass exception process** for shipping an **emergency unsigned offline bundle** ("unsigned hotfix bundle") for **Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)** when the standard bundle signing process is unavailable.

**Default policy:** All Redwood Private offline bundles MUST be signed and must fail closed in the installer when signature verification fails.

**Exception:** An unsigned bundle may be shipped only under the strict conditions and approvals below, with mandatory compensating controls, customer communication, and follow-up remediation.

This process exists to:
- Enable urgent customer impact fixes when signing is temporarily blocked.
- Control and document supply-chain risk.
- Produce auditable evidence for customers and internal compliance.

---

## Scope
Applies to:
- **Offline bundles** distributed to customers for air-gapped or restricted-network environments.
- **Hotfix** releases required for security vulnerabilities, data loss risk, severe availability incidents, or legal/compliance blockers.

Does not apply to:
- Standard signed releases.
- Customer-controlled internal artifact repackaging (customer actions outside Redwood distribution).
- Break-glass install flags on the customer side (covered in installer policy and runbooks).

---

## Definitions
- **Offline bundle:** The packaged set of artifacts used to install/upgrade Redwood Private (installer, charts/manifests, image references, runtime artifacts, model/runtime dependencies, SBOM pointer, etc.).
- **Unsigned hotfix bundle:** An offline bundle that is distributed **without Redwood’s cryptographic signature** due to an exception.
- **Compensating controls:** Additional verification steps (hash validation, controlled distribution, access restrictions, enhanced audit logging) required when signature-based integrity verification is not available.

---

## Policy statement
1) **Unsigned offline bundles are prohibited by default.**
2) **Unsigned offline bundles may be shipped only under an approved exception** and must:
   - Be limited in scope, time, and recipients.
   - Use compensating controls described in this document.
   - Include a committed plan and deadlines for a **signed replacement**.
3) Any use of this exception is treated as a **Security/GRC event** and must be recorded for audit purposes.

---

## Acceptable reasons (eligibility criteria)
An exception request is eligible only if ALL of the following are true:
- **Customer impact is high/urgent**, e.g.:
  - Critical security vulnerability (actively exploited or high confidence risk).
  - Severity-1 incident impacting production installs/upgrades.
  - Data corruption/loss risk.
  - Regulatory/legal deadline where delay would materially harm customer.
- **Standard signing is blocked** due to one of:
  - Signing key infrastructure outage/unavailability (HSM/KMS access failure).
  - Release pipeline break preventing signing with no timely workaround.
  - Emergency incident response requiring immediate patch distribution before signing path can be restored.
- **No safe alternative exists**, such as:
  - Shipping a signed bundle via an alternate signing environment.
  - Reverting to last known good signed bundle.
  - Using a signed minimal mitigation release.

Not eligible:
- Convenience, schedule pressure, non-urgent feature delivery.
- Avoiding a normal security review.
- Shipping unsigned because the signing process is “not finished”.

---

## Approval matrix (who must approve)
The approvers below provide explicit, recorded approval (ticket + written acknowledgment). Approvals may not be delegated without Security leadership consent.

| Decision / Step | Required approvers | Optional / FYI |
|---|---|---|
| Open exception request + initial triage | Release Eng Lead (Connor O'Brien), Security Eng Lead (Marcus Lin) | Product Owner Private (Vivek Kulkarni), SRE Lead (Sean Gallagher) |
| Risk acceptance to distribute unsigned bundle | Security Exec Sponsor (Dr. Aisha Rahman), GRC Lead (Olga Petrov), Release Eng Lead (Connor O'Brien) | Infra/SRE Exec Sponsor (Neha Kapoor) |
| Customer communication approval (template + recipients) | Product Owner Private (Vivek Kulkarni), Support Readiness Lead (Dev Patel), GRC Lead (Olga Petrov) | Customer Success Lead (Rachel Kim), Solutions Lead (Fatima Noor) |
| Final “go/no-go” on distribution | Security Exec Sponsor (Dr. Aisha Rahman) AND Release Eng Lead (Connor O'Brien) | CEO/Legal only if contractually required |

**Quorum rule:** If any required approver is unavailable, the exception is blocked unless Dr. Aisha Rahman and Connor O’Brien both approve an alternate approver in writing.

---

## Required artifacts and evidence (must be produced)
For each unsigned bundle exception, the release owner (Release Eng) must produce and store the following:

1) **Exception ticket** (single source of truth)
- Title format: `EXC-UNSIGNED-BUNDLE: <Customer/Segment> <Bundle version> <Reason>`
- Must include: impact, timeline, affected customers, mitigation plan, and risk acknowledgment.

2) **Bundle identity + immutable metadata**
- Bundle version (e.g., `private-2026.01.0-hotfix.1`)
- Build ID / CI run ID
- Git commit SHAs for installer/charts/runtime as applicable
- Artifact list (what is included)
- Intended recipients (named accounts)

3) **Compensating integrity verification package (hash manifest)**
Because signature verification is not available, Redwood must provide a **separate integrity verification package**:
- `SHA256SUMS` (or equivalent) covering:
  - Offline bundle archive
  - Internal bundle manifest
  - Installer binary (if shipped separately)
  - Any chart tarballs or dependency packages
  - SBOM file/pointer artifact if included
- Hashes must be generated on a controlled build runner and verified by a second operator.

4) **Two-person verification record**
- Operator A generates hashes and attaches them to the ticket.
- Operator B independently recomputes hashes from the staged artifacts and confirms match.
- Both sign off with timestamp and workstation/build environment identifier.

5) **Distribution controls record**
- Where the bundle is hosted (internal restricted bucket/release portal)
- Access control list (ACL) snapshot: who can download
- Expiration date for access

6) **Customer communication record**
- Approved message sent, time sent, recipients, and acknowledgement if required.

7) **Remediation plan with deadlines**
- Target date for restoring signing pipeline
- Target date for issuing a signed replacement bundle
- Plan for revocation/withdrawal of unsigned bundle

---

## Compensating controls (required)
When shipping an unsigned bundle, Redwood must apply ALL of the controls below.

### 1) Strict distribution limitations
- **Limit recipients** to explicitly approved accounts/environments.
- **Limit time window**: bundle access should expire within **7 calendar days** unless re-approved.
- Do not post unsigned bundles to broad download portals or public locations.

### 2) Out-of-band integrity verification instructions
Provide customers with:
- A hash file (e.g., `SHA256SUMS`) and verification steps.
- A clear statement: “Integrity must be verified before install; do not proceed if hashes do not match.”

Recommended customer commands (Linux example):
- `sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS`
- Or `sha256sum <bundle>.tar.zst` and compare with provided value.

### 3) Controlled transport guidance
Customers must be instructed to:
- Avoid tools known to mutate archives.
- Prefer binary-safe transport (e.g., secure copy to removable media, verify hashes after transfer).

### 4) Explicit risk labeling in artifacts
- Bundle filename must include `UNSIGNED` (example):
  - `redwood-private-2026.01.0-hotfix.1-UNSIGNED-offline-bundle.tar.zst`
- Include `UNSIGNED_README.txt` at bundle root stating:
  - Why unsigned
  - Required verification steps
  - Expiration / replacement plan

### 5) Installer behavior
- The installer must continue to **fail closed** on signature verification by default.
- If the customer must proceed without signature validation, this must be handled via the installer’s documented break-glass path (separately governed), and must be logged locally as a break-glass event.

---

## Risk acknowledgment (required language)
Approvers must acknowledge the following in the exception ticket:

- “We understand that shipping an unsigned offline bundle increases the risk of undetected tampering in transit or at rest.”
- “We confirm that the signed-bundle pipeline is currently blocked and that no safe signed alternative is feasible within the required timeline.”
- “We commit to issuing a signed replacement bundle by <DATE> and to withdrawing the unsigned bundle from distribution by <DATE>.”
- “We confirm compensating controls are in place: restricted distribution, hash-based verification, two-person verification, and documented customer communication.”

---

## Procedure
### Step 0 — Initiate exception
Owner: Connor O'Brien (Release Engineering) or delegate on the release train.

Create the exception ticket and include:
- Severity/impact summary
- Root cause of signing blockage (what failed)
- Why delay is unacceptable
- Proposed unsigned bundle version + recipients

### Step 1 — Security triage
Owner: Marcus Lin (Security Engineering)

Validate:
- Eligibility criteria
- Threat considerations (tampering, replay/downgrade risk)
- Whether additional controls are required for the specific customer environment

### Step 2 — Prepare unsigned bundle + hash manifest
Owner: Release Engineering

- Produce the bundle using deterministic build steps as usual.
- Generate `SHA256SUMS` for all required artifacts.
- Complete two-person verification.

### Step 3 — Obtain approvals
Owner: Olga Petrov (GRC) to coordinate approvals

- Collect approvals per matrix.
- Ensure risk acknowledgment language is included verbatim.

### Step 4 — Customer communication
Owner: Vivek Kulkarni (Product Private) + Dev Patel (Support)

Send approved message before sharing download links.

Minimum content:
- Reason for unsigned distribution
- Required hash verification steps
- Support contact path
- Replacement plan and expected timeline

### Step 5 — Distribute with access controls
Owner: Release Engineering

- Host in restricted location.
- Provide customer with:
  - Bundle
  - `SHA256SUMS`
  - Verification instructions
  - `UNSIGNED_README.txt`

### Step 6 — Track completion + remediation
Owner: Release Engineering + Security

- Confirm customer verified hashes and completed install/upgrade.
- Restore signing pipeline.
- Ship signed replacement bundle.
- Withdraw access to unsigned bundle.

---

## Customer communication templates
### Template A — Initial notice (email)
Subject: `[Action required] Redwood Private hotfix bundle integrity verification (unsigned emergency release)`

Body:
- We are providing an emergency hotfix bundle to address <ISSUE>.
- Due to <SIGNING BLOCKER>, this bundle is **unsigned**. This is an exception to Redwood’s standard signed-bundle process.
- Before installation, you must verify integrity using the provided `SHA256SUMS` file:
  1) Transfer both the bundle and `SHA256SUMS` to your offline environment.
  2) Run: `sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS`
  3) Do not proceed if any verification fails.
- We will provide a **signed replacement** by <DATE> and will retire this unsigned bundle by <DATE>.
- If you have any questions or verification failures, contact <Support alias/SE contact> and include the verification output.

### Template B — Follow-up (signed replacement available)
Subject: `Signed replacement available: Redwood Private <version> offline bundle`

Body:
- A signed replacement bundle is now available: <LINK/DELIVERY METHOD>.
- Please prefer the signed bundle for any new installs/upgrades.
- The previous unsigned bundle will be removed from distribution on <DATE>.

---

## Remediation requirements (mandatory)
Within **5 business days** after distribution of any unsigned bundle, Release Engineering must schedule and complete a remediation review with Security + GRC.

### Remediation checklist
- [ ] Root cause analysis: why signing was blocked (technical + process)
- [ ] Confirm the signed pipeline is restored OR a permanent workaround exists
- [ ] Issue a signed replacement bundle and validate verification end-to-end
- [ ] Withdraw unsigned bundle distribution and document withdrawal date/time
- [ ] Confirm customer has migrated (or documented why not)
- [ ] Update runbooks/tests to prevent recurrence
- [ ] File follow-up tickets for any process gaps (approvals, evidence, access controls)

### Evidence to attach after remediation
- Link to signed replacement release artifact
- Confirmation of unsigned bundle removal
- Summary of customer outcome (install success + any issues)

---

## Audit and retention
- Exception tickets and evidence must be retained for **7 years** (or longer if customer contract requires).
- All approvals must be recorded in writing (ticket comments are acceptable if identities are validated).
- Access logs for downloads must be retained for at least **1 year**.

---

## Escalation and incident handling
If an unsigned bundle is suspected to be tampered with, leaked, or redistributed beyond approved recipients:
1) Notify Security on-call and Release Engineering immediately.
2) Stop distribution (revoke links/credentials).
3) Initiate an incident: “Potential supply-chain compromise (unsigned bundle exception)”.
4) Provide customers with new verified artifacts and guidance.

---

## FAQ
**Q: Can we ship an unsigned bundle to keep a release train on schedule?**
No. Schedule pressure is not an eligible reason.

**Q: Can we provide only a single hash of the bundle archive?**
No. At minimum, provide a hash for the archive and the internal manifest; additionally provide hashes for critical install components (installer, charts/deps) to support targeted verification and troubleshooting.

**Q: Can Support approve this exception?**
No. Support participates in readiness and communication, but Security leadership, GRC, and Release Engineering approvals are mandatory.

**Q: Does this change the installer verification policy?**
No. The installer must continue to treat missing/invalid signatures as a failure by default; any customer-side override remains separately governed and must be audited.
