- Incident Commander (IC): Security oncall by default; coordinates cross-team evidence work. Owner: security-oncall@redwood.example.com.
- Forensic Lead: Senior SRE or Security Engineer who performs log extraction and sealing.
- Tenant Liaison: Customer Success engineer assigned for tenant-facing comms if required.
- Legal/Escalation: Security & Legal reviewers for high-sensitivity or cross-border incidents.

Immediate triage checklist (first 30 minutes)

1. IC confirms scope and classifies incident severity.
2. Enable immutable logging window on audit-collector for the affected timeframe (see commands).
3. Capture live snapshots: model-router config, routing-table revision, and active model-versions list.
4. Notify Forensic Lead and open a sealed evidence channel (S3 bucket with restricted policy or on-prem vault).

Collection windows and time bounding

- Primary window: T0-1m to T0+30m (T0 = first observed anomalous request/alert).
- Extended window: T0-24h for related telemetry (rate changes, autoscale events, gating changes).

Service-specific collection checklist

- inference-gateway:
  - Gather: request headers, per-request route decisions, tenant-id, request-id, response-id, and partial streaming deltas where applicable.
  - Command example: curl -X POST "https://infra-admin.internal/v1/diagnostics/search-requests" -d '{"start":"2025-11-15T14:23:00Z","end":"2025-11-15T14:55:00Z","service":"inference-gateway","filters":{"tenant_id":["tenant-A","tenant-B"]}}'

- model-router:
  - Gather: routing-table@revision, rollout flags, fallback-model-mapping, kernel-selection decisions.
  - Snapshot config: /etc/model-router/revisions/<rev>.yaml and /var/log/model-router/decision.log

- kv-prefix-cache:
  - Export: cache-hit/miss counters and exact keys evicted or promoted for the window.
  - Note: do NOT export raw cached payloads if they contain regulated user content; instead export key-level metadata and checksums.

- audit-collector and observability pipeline:
  - Export: raw audit events, ingestion receipts, and downstream archival confirmations. Ensure export includes ingestion-offsets to allow replay.
  - Retain: the original signed ingestion manifest produced by audit-collector.

RBAC and authorization evidence

- Capture RBAC decision logs for the window from the authz-service. Required fields: principal_id, role_bindings_snapshot, resource, action, decision, policy_version.
- If JIT (just-in-time) privilege elevation occurred, include activation token IDs and duration.
- For delegated tokens (e.g., model-to-model calls), include token lineage: parent token id -> child token id -> TTL at issuance.

Data residency / retention mapping (summary table)

- Artifact type | Typical location | Allowed export regions | Retention TTL
- ------------ | ---------------- | ---------------------- | ----------
- Request headers (redacted) | s3://audit-collector-regional/ | same-region only | 90 days
- Traces (service-level) | efs://obs-traces/ | cross-region read allowed via ephemeral access | 30 days
- Raw model outputs (customer content) | encrypted regional blob store | region-only, legal approval required for export | 7 days default
- RBAC decision logs | s3://authz-logs/ | same-region; meta-only copy allowed cross-region | 365 days

Evidence minimization rules

- Default: prefer metadata, checksums, and redacted payloads. Only include raw customer content when strictly required for root cause and only after Legal approval.
- Approved redaction patterns: replace customer PII in JSON strings with <REDACTED-PII> and retain SHA256 checksum of original content (stored separately in sealed manifest).

Chain-of-custody and sealing procedure (numbered steps)

1. Forensic Lead performs targeted extraction using read-only diagnostics APIs. Record exact CLI commands used and operator identity.
2. Create sealing manifest (manifest.json) listing files, SHA256 checksums, collector identity, and UTC timestamps.
3. Compute a signed manifest token using our internal HSM key (hsm-sign manifest.json) and attach signature as manifest.sig.
4. Move artifacts into the incident bucket: s3://incidents/<incident-id>/sealed/ and set bucket-policy to block public access and restrict to evidence-role.
5. Log the S3 object versions and retention locks in the audit-collector.

Tamper-evidence and verification

- All artifacts must include a SHA256 checksum listed in the manifest. Verifiers should re-compute and compare checksums.
- Manifest signature verification example: hsm-verify --key-id hsm-evidence-key --signature manifest.sig manifest.json

Packaging & handoff to Compliance/Legal

- When incident is classified as requiring external report or audit:
  1. IC opens Compliance request ticket and attaches sealed manifest.
  2. Forensic Lead prepares a redaction report describing redaction algorithm and checksums for any removed content.
  3. Legal approves cross-border export if needed and authorizes a one-time ephemeral share link (7-day expiry) with IP allowlist.

Forensic query snippets (for on-call use)

- Kibana-like query for cross-tenant anomalies:
  - service:"inference-gateway" AND response.status:[400 TO 599] AND (tenant_id:"tenant-A" OR tenant_id:"tenant-B") AND @timestamp:[2025-11-15T14:00:00Z TO 2025-11-15T15:00:00Z]

- Authz decision extraction (CLI):
  - authz-cli export-decisions --start "2025-11-15T14:00:00Z" --end "2025-11-15T15:00:00Z" --service model-router --out decisions.csv

Escalation criteria (when to involve legal / executive comms)

- Any evidence that tenant data was delivered to another tenant or external endpoint.
- Any cross-border export request where raw customer content would leave its residency region.
- Material customer outage impacting >5% of instances for >1 hour.

Post-incident evidence review (within 7 days)

- Forensic Lead produces a short evidence report that includes:
  - Timeline of key artifacts and hashes.
  - List of artifacts redacted and reason.
  - RBAC policy changes and rollout history tied to the incident.
  - SLO impact summary (latency error budget, token loss, request failure rate).

Retention and disposal

- Evidence bundles are retained for the minimum compliance TTL required by the most restrictive affected tenant. Default retention: 365 days unless a legal hold is placed.
- Disposal steps: remove S3 versions, expire KMS keys where allowed, and record disposal event in audit-collector with proof-of-deletion checksums.

Automation & tooling notes

- Use the incident-orchestrator service to run standardized extraction playbooks; it will populate manifest.json and optionally sign using the HSM.
- Integrate with the legal-approval service to gate cross-region transfers.

Appendix A - sample sealing manifest (example shown redacted)

{
  "incident_id": "INC-20251115-0234",
  "collected_by": "jared.liu@redwood.example.com",
  "collected_at": "2025-11-15T15:05:12Z",
  "files": ["inference-gateway-requests.log.sha256", "model-router-revision.yaml.sha256"],
  "signature": "<attached-manifest-signature>"
}

Appendix B - contact matrix

- Security oncall (IC): security-oncall@redwood.example.com
- Forensics rotation: forensics-rotation@redwood.example.com
- Legal escalation: legal-escalations@redwood.example.com
- Customer success (tenant liaison): cs-liaison@redwood.example.com

Related pages and references

- See: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/just-in-time-privilege-activation-and-audit-evidence-2025 for RBAC logging schema.
- See: /confluence/security-and-compliance/compliance-evidence/evidence-orchestration-for-model-serving-governance-2026 for policy-level evidence packaging guidance.

Change history

- 2025-11-15: Draft created by Jared Liu.
- 2025-12-03: Forensic extraction commands standardized by Maya Chen.
- 2026-01-20: Legal-approved cross-border gating and ephemeral share process added.

Summary

This playbook defines the evidence orchestration, collection, and packaging process for incidents that originate from model-serving failures in multi-tenant environments. It is focused on preserving chain-of-custody, meeting cross-border data residency constraints, and producing artifacts suitable for internal investigations and external audits.

Scope

- Applies to incidents where a model-serving event affects more than one tenant or where tenant isolation may be implicated (data leakage, cross-tenant inference responses, or authorization bypass).
- Covers services: inference-gateway, model-router, kv-prefix-cache, tokenizer-service, audit-collector, and inference-observability.
- Excludes: single-tenant application errors unrelated to model outputs (those follow standard app incident process).

Key goals

1. Collect a minimal, precise evidence set that establishes timeline, root cause signals, and affected tenants.
2. Maintain strong chain-of-custody and tamper-evident packaging suitable for compliance requests.
3. Respect data residency constraints: evidence extraction should avoid copying regulated customer content outside permitted regions.

Definitions

- Evidence bundle: A sealed archive containing logs, traces, config snapshots, RBAC audits, and migration/rollout metadata for the incident window.
- Sealing manifest: JSON metadata listing file checksums (SHA256), collection timestamps, and collecting agent identity.

Roles and responsibilities

- Incident Commander (IC): Security oncall by default; coordinates cross-team evidence work. Owner: security-oncall@redwood.example.com.

- Legal/Escalation: Security & Legal reviewers for high-sensitivity or cross-border incidents.

source.title: Bundle verification audit events (Redwood Private offline bundles)
source.author: Kevin Osei
source.body:
## Overview
This page defines the audit events emitted by Redwood Private tooling during *offline bundle verification* (preflight and install/upgrade). These events are designed for regulated and/or air-gapped environments where customers need local, tamper-evident evidence that:
- The offline bundle’s manifest and referenced artifacts were verified successfully, or
- Verification failed (and why), or
- A break-glass override was used.

This spec is consumed by:
- **redwood-private-installer** (event emission during `preflight`, `install`, `upgrade`)
- **audit-log-exporter** (schema mapping for SIEM forwarding when enabled)
- Support tooling (support bundle capture and triage)

## Goals
- Provide a consistent event envelope and stable event names across releases.
- Enable compliance evidence (who/what/when) for verification outcomes.
- Avoid leaking customer secrets or model/prompt content into audit logs.
- Work in fully offline deployments (local storage first; optional export).

## Non-goals
- Full end-to-end attestation of the customer’s environment state (that is handled by separate cluster/audit tooling).
- Customer-owned signing roots / customer-signed bundles (future work; this spec remains compatible).

---

## Event transport and storage (Private)
### Default local sink
- **Format:** JSON Lines (one JSON object per line)
- **Path (default):** `/var/log/redwood-private/audit/bundle-verification.log`
- **Permissions:** `root:root`, `0640` (installer runs with elevated privileges; read access restricted to `redwood-support` group where configured)
- **Rotation:** handled by `logrotate` when present; otherwise installer enforces size-based rotation (see Retention)

### Optional export
When the **audit-log-exporter** component is deployed and configured, relevant events are forwarded to a customer-configured destination.
- Supported export modes depend on deployment (e.g., syslog, file drop, Splunk HEC, HTTPS collector).
- In *air-gapped* environments with no network path, export commonly occurs by copying rotated log files to removable media under customer processes.

---

## Standard event envelope
All bundle verification audit events MUST conform to the following envelope.

### Required envelope fields
| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `event_name` | string | Stable name, e.g., `redwood.private.bundle.verify.success` |
| `event_version` | string | SemVer-like event schema version for the event payload (e.g., `1.0`) |
| `event_id` | string | UUIDv4 generated at emission time |
| `occurred_at` | string | RFC3339 timestamp, UTC recommended (e.g., `2026-01-20T18:22:11Z`) |
| `severity` | string | `INFO` | `WARN` | `ERROR` | `CRITICAL` |
| `environment` | string | `private` (reserved for Redwood Private) |
| `component` | string | Emitting component (e.g., `redwood-private-installer`) |
| `component_version` | string | Version of the emitting component |
| `host_id` | string | Stable host identifier when available (customer-defined or derived); otherwise `unknown` |
| `cluster_id` | string | Cluster identifier when available; otherwise `unknown` |

### Common optional envelope fields
| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `request_id` | string | Correlates a single installer run or command invocation |
| `session_id` | string | Correlates a multi-step flow (preflight → install → post-checks) |
| `actor_type` | string | `human` | `service` | `unknown` |
| `actor_id` | string | Username/service identity when available (avoid emails by default) |
| `actor_ip` | string | Source IP if known (often `127.0.0.1` in on-prem) |
| `command` | string | CLI command that triggered the event (redacted) |
| `dry_run` | string | `true`/`false` |

### Redaction rules (applies to *all* events)
Do NOT include the following in audit events:
- Prompt text, model inputs/outputs, customer proprietary data
- Private keys, tokens, passwords, kubeconfigs
- Full filesystem listings

Allowed with caution:
- File paths to the bundle location are allowed but should be minimized; prefer `bundle_path_hash` (sha256 of path string) when `--redact-paths` is enabled.

---

## Bundle verification context fields
These fields describe the bundle and verification context and SHOULD be included when applicable.

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `bundle_id` | string | Logical identifier from manifest (e.g., `private-2026.01.0`) |
| `bundle_build_id` | string | Build identifier used to correlate releases and support cases |
| `bundle_format_version` | string | Offline bundle format version |
| `bundle_manifest_digest_sha256` | string | Digest of the canonicalized manifest bytes |
| `bundle_signature_digest_sha256` | string | Digest of detached signature file bytes, if present |
| `signature_type` | string | e.g., `ed25519` or `x509-ecdsa-p256` (per ADR) |
| `signing_key_id` | string | Key identifier (stable; typically fingerprint-derived) |
| `trust_store_id` | string | Identifier/version of the trust store used |
| `verification_mode` | string | `preflight` | `install` | `upgrade` | `inspect` |
| `offline_mode` | string | `true`/`false` (should be `true` in air-gapped installs) |
| `time_source` | string | `system` | `override` | `unknown` |
| `time_skew_seconds` | string | Signed integer in seconds when detected, else omitted |

---

## Event taxonomy
### Event names (stable)
All names are prefixed with `redwood.private.bundle`.

| Event name | Severity | When emitted |
|---|---|---|
| `redwood.private.bundle.verify.started` | INFO | Verification process begins (preflight/install/upgrade/inspect) |
| `redwood.private.bundle.verify.success` | INFO | Bundle verified successfully (signature valid + manifest matches + required artifacts hashed) |
| `redwood.private.bundle.verify.failure` | ERROR | Verification failed and installer will fail closed (unless break-glass used) |
| `redwood.private.bundle.verify.downgrade_blocked` | WARN | Signed metadata indicates a downgrade attempt; install/upgrade blocked |
| `redwood.private.bundle.trust_store.unknown_key` | ERROR | Signature uses a key not present in trust store |
| `redwood.private.bundle.trust_store.updated` | INFO | Trust store updated (e.g., new public key added for rotation) |
| `redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.used` | CRITICAL | Break-glass override used to proceed despite verification issues |
| `redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.denied` | WARN | Break-glass requested but policy/guardrail denied |

### Failure reason codes
When emitting `...verify.failure` (and some related events), include `failure_reason` and (when possible) `failure_detail`.

| failure_reason | Typical root cause |
|---|---|
| `SIGNATURE_INVALID` | Signature bytes don’t validate against manifest digest |
| `SIGNATURE_EXPIRED` | Signing cert validity window ended (x509-based schemes) |
| `SIGNATURE_NOT_YET_VALID` | System time behind signing validity start; common in air-gapped time skew |
| `UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY` | Trust store missing the key used to sign this bundle |
| `MANIFEST_HASH_MISMATCH` | Manifest digest differs from expected/canonicalization mismatch or tampering |
| `ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH` | One or more artifacts referenced in manifest do not match recorded hashes |
| `MISSING_REQUIRED_ARTIFACT` | Bundle missing a required file referenced by manifest |
| `BUNDLE_FORMAT_UNSUPPORTED` | Installer too old to understand bundle format version |
| `DOWNGRADE_ATTEMPT` | Signed version metadata indicates downgrade vs installed state |
| `POLICY_BLOCKED` | Verification failed due to policy (e.g., disallowed algorithm) |
| `IO_ERROR` | Read error while hashing/verifying bundle contents |

---

## Event payload requirements by event
### `redwood.private.bundle.verify.started`
Required additional fields:
- `verification_mode`
- `bundle_id` (if manifest readable)
- `bundle_build_id` (if manifest readable)
- `bundle_format_version` (if manifest readable)

### `redwood.private.bundle.verify.success`
Required additional fields:
- `bundle_id`, `bundle_build_id`, `bundle_format_version`
- `bundle_manifest_digest_sha256`
- `signature_type`, `signing_key_id`, `trust_store_id`
- `verified_artifact_count` (string integer)

Optional:
- `sbom_digest_sha256` (if SBOM pointer present and hashed)
- `verification_duration_ms` (string integer)

### `redwood.private.bundle.verify.failure`
Required additional fields:
- `failure_reason`
- `failure_detail` (short, actionable; no secrets)
- `bundle_manifest_digest_sha256` (if computed)
- `signature_type` and `signing_key_id` (if identifiable)

Optional:
- `failed_artifact_path` (only when `--redact-paths=false`)
- `failed_artifact_digest_expected_sha256` / `failed_artifact_digest_actual_sha256` (allowed)
- `verification_duration_ms`

### `redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.used`
Required additional fields:
- `break_glass_ticket` (customer/internal ticket reference if provided)
- `break_glass_reason` (operator-provided; enforced non-empty)
- `break_glass_policy_version`
- `verification_mode`
- `failure_reason` (the reason being overridden, if known)

NOTE: Emission of this event MUST be accompanied by a visible warning to the operator and MUST also be included in the generated support bundle when enabled.

---

## Examples
### Success
{"event_name":"redwood.private.bundle.verify.success","event_version":"1.0","event_id":"0c7d2b3a-9f9e-4c1e-9a45-623f0d4bfa2a","occurred_at":"2026-01-20T18:22:11Z","severity":"INFO","environment":"private","component":"redwood-private-installer","component_version":"2.18.0","host_id":"node-03","cluster_id":"cluster-a1b2","request_id":"req-7e9f","session_id":"sess-91aa","actor_type":"human","actor_id":"installer-operator","command":"preflight --offline --bundle /mnt/media/redwood-private-2026.01.0.tgz","dry_run":"false","verification_mode":"preflight","offline_mode":"true","time_source":"system","bundle_id":"private-2026.01.0","bundle_build_id":"rbwpriv_2026.01.0_20260119.3","bundle_format_version":"0.4","bundle_manifest_digest_sha256":"9f3c...e10a","bundle_signature_digest_sha256":"b2d1...9a21","signature_type":"ed25519","signing_key_id":"ed25519:9c8f...","trust_store_id":"truststore-2026-01","verified_artifact_count":"143","verification_duration_ms":"18427"}

### Failure (unknown key)
{"event_name":"redwood.private.bundle.trust_store.unknown_key","event_version":"1.0","event_id":"8c0f2b13-2d41-4c8f-9cd2-5d0e2d5a9b4e","occurred_at":"2026-01-20T19:03:02Z","severity":"ERROR","environment":"private","component":"redwood-private-installer","component_version":"2.18.0","host_id":"node-01","cluster_id":"unknown","request_id":"req-0e2c","actor_type":"human","actor_id":"installer-operator","verification_mode":"preflight","offline_mode":"true","bundle_id":"private-2026.01.0","bundle_build_id":"rbwpriv_2026.01.0_20260119.3","signature_type":"ed25519","signing_key_id":"ed25519:1a2b...","trust_store_id":"truststore-2025-10","failure_reason":"UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY","failure_detail":"Signing key not found in trust store. Update trust store per key rotation guide before retrying."}

### Break-glass used
{"event_name":"redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.used","event_version":"1.0","event_id":"7c9e7b2f-3e1a-4c8c-8a9d-1c4e3b5d2f11","occurred_at":"2026-01-20T19:06:44Z","severity":"CRITICAL","environment":"private","component":"redwood-private-installer","component_version":"2.18.0","host_id":"node-01","cluster_id":"unknown","request_id":"req-0e2c","actor_type":"human","actor_id":"installer-operator","verification_mode":"install","offline_mode":"true","bundle_id":"private-2026.01.0","bundle_build_id":"rbwpriv_2026.01.0_20260119.3","break_glass_ticket":"SUP-31977","break_glass_reason":"Customer environment missing updated trust store; urgent restore window approved by security lead","break_glass_policy_version":"2026-01","failure_reason":"UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY"}

---

## Local retention and rotation guidance
### Defaults (recommended)
- **Retention period:** 30 days
- **Max file size:** 100 MB per file
- **Max rotated files:** 10

### Minimum for compliance-oriented deployments
- **Retention period:** 90 days (or customer policy)
- Ensure rotated logs are backed up to customer-controlled archival storage.

### Tamper considerations
Redwood Private does not assume OS-level immutability in all customer environments. Customers requiring stronger guarantees should:
- Forward logs to an append-only target (WORM, SIEM, or immutable object storage) when feasible, OR
- Apply OS controls (e.g., auditd rules, file immutability, restricted admin access) per their policy.

---

## SIEM integration notes (audit-log-exporter)
### Mapping
- Event names map 1:1 to exporter event types.
- Exporter MUST preserve `event_name`, `event_version`, `occurred_at`, `severity`, and `event_id` unchanged.
- Exporter MAY add transport metadata (e.g., `export_destination`, `exported_at`) but must not alter the original payload.

### Recommended SIEM fields
Most SIEMs benefit from parsing:
- `event_name` (as category)
- `severity`
- `actor_id`
- `bundle_id`, `bundle_build_id`
- `failure_reason`
- `signing_key_id`, `trust_store_id`

### Air-gapped export pattern
If the deployment cannot reach a SIEM endpoint:
1. Rotate and seal logs on the Private host(s) (customer policy).
2. Copy rotated files to removable media.
3. Import into an internal SIEM in a controlled enclave.

---

## Operational notes
### Alerting recommendations
Customers (and Redwood internal runbooks for managed Private) should consider alerting on:
- Any `redwood.private.bundle.verify.failure`
- Any `redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.used`
- Spike in `UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY` after key rotation windows

### Support bundle inclusion
When support bundle generation is enabled, include:
- Last 200 lines of `bundle-verification.log`
- Trust store identifier and key list (public info only)
- Last verification result and `request_id`/`session_id` correlation IDs

---

## Change control
- Owner: Security Engineering (Audit logging/SIEM requirements) — primary: Kevin Osei.
- Any breaking changes to event names or required fields require:
  - Security review (Marcus Lin)
  - Observability/logging standards review (Allison Grant)
  - Exporter schema update (audit-log-exporter) and installer update in the same release train

## Changelog
- 2026-01-20: Published v1.0 taxonomy; added break-glass event requirements and air-gapped export guidance.
- 2025-12-05: Initial draft envelope and failure reason codes.

title: Bundle verification audit events (Redwood Private offline bundles)
preview:
## Overview
This page defines the audit events emitted by Redwood Private tooling during *offline bundle verification* (preflight and install/upgrade). These events are designed for regulated and/or air-gapped environments where customers need local, tamper-evident evidence that:
- The offline bundle’s manifest and referenced artifacts were verified successfully, or
- Verification failed (and why), or
- A break-glass override was used.

This spec is consumed by:
- **redwood-private-installer** (event emission during `preflight`, `install`, `upgrade`)
- **audit-log-exporter** (schema mapping for SIEM forwarding when enabled)
- Support tooling (support bundle capture and triage)

## Goals
- Provide a consistent event envelope and stable event names across releases.
- Enable compliance evidence (who/what/when) for verification outcomes.
- Avoid leaking customer secrets or model/prompt content into audit logs.
- Work in fully offline deployments (local storage first; optional export).

## Non-goals
- Full end-to-end attestation of the customer’s environment state (that is handled by separate cluster/audit tooling).
- Customer-owned signing roots / customer-signed bundles (future work; this spec remains compatible).

---

## Event transport and storage (Private)
### Default local sink
- **Format:** JSON Lines (one JSON object per line)
- **Path (default):** `/var/log/redwood-private/audit/bundle-verification.log`
- **Permissions:** `root:root`, `0640` (installer runs with elevated privileges; read access restricted to `redwood-support` group where configured)
- **Rotation:** handled by `logrotate` when present; otherwise installer enforces size-based rotation (see Retention)

### Optional export
When the **audit-log-exporter** component is deployed and configured, relevant events are forwarded to a customer-configured destination.
- Supported export modes depend on deployment (e.g., syslog, file drop, Splunk HEC, HTTPS collector).
- In *air-gapped* environments with no network path, export commonly occurs by copying rotated log files to removable media under customer processes.

---

## Standard event envelope
All bundle verification audit events MUST conform to the following envelope.

### Required envelope fields
| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `event_name` | string | Stable name, e.g., `redwood.private.bundle.verify.success` |
| `event_version` | string | SemVer-like event schema version for the event payload (e.g., `1.0`) |
| `event_id` | string | UUIDv4 generated at emission time |
| `occurred_at` | string | RFC3339 timestamp, UTC recommended (e.g., `2026-01-20T18:22:11Z`) |
| `severity` | string | `INFO` | `WARN` | `ERROR` | `CRITICAL` |
| `environment` | string | `private` (reserved for Redwood Private) |
| `component` | string | Emitting component (e.g., `redwood-private-installer`) |
| `component_version` | string | Version of the emitting component |
| `host_id` | string | Stable host identifier when available (customer-defined or derived); otherwise `unknown` |
| `cluster_id` | string | Cluster identifier when available; otherwise `unknown` |

### Common optional envelope fields
| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `request_id` | string | Correlates a single installer run or command invocation |
| `session_id` | string | Correlates a multi-step flow (preflight → install → post-checks) |
| `actor_type` | string | `human` | `service` | `unknown` |
| `actor_id` | string | Username/service identity when available (avoid emails by default) |
| `actor_ip` | string | Source IP if known (often `127.0.0.1` in on-prem) |
| `command` | string | CLI command that triggered the event (redacted) |
| `dry_run` | string | `true`/`false` |

### Redaction rules (applies to *all* events)
Do NOT include the following in audit events:
- Prompt text, model inputs/outputs, customer proprietary data
- Private keys, tokens, passwords, kubeconfigs
- Full filesystem listings

Allowed with caution:
- File paths to the bundle location are allowed but should be minimized; prefer `bundle_path_hash` (sha256 of path string) when `--redact-paths` is enabled.

---

## Bundle verification context fields
These fields describe the bundle and verification context and SHOULD be included when applicable.

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `bundle_id` | string | Logical identifier from manifest (e.g., `private-2026.01.0`) |
| `bundle_build_id` | string | Build identifier used to correlate releases and support cases |
| `bundle_format_version` | string | Offline bundle format version |
| `bundle_manifest_digest_sha256` | string | Digest of the canonicalized manifest bytes |
| `bundle_signature_digest_sha256` | string | Digest of detached signature file bytes, if present |
| `signature_type` | string | e.g., `ed25519` or `x509-ecdsa-p256` (per ADR) |
| `signing_key_id` | string | Key identifier (stable; typically fingerprint-derived) |
| `trust_store_id` | string | Identifier/version of the trust store used |
| `verification_mode` | string | `preflight` | `install` | `upgrade` | `inspect` |
| `offline_mode` | string | `true`/`false` (should be `true` in air-gapped installs) |
| `time_source` | string | `system` | `override` | `unknown` |
| `time_skew_seconds` | string | Signed integer in seconds when detected, else omitted |

---

## Event taxonomy
### Event names (stable)
All names are prefixed with `redwood.private.bundle`.

| Event name | Severity | When emitted |
|---|---|---|
| `redwood.private.bundle.verify.started` | INFO | Verification process begins (preflight/install/upgrade/inspect) |
| `redwood.private.bundle.verify.success` | INFO | Bundle verified successfully (signature valid + manifest matches + required artifacts hashed) |
| `redwood.private.bundle.verify.failure` | ERROR | Verification failed and installer will fail closed (unless break-glass used) |
| `redwood.private.bundle.verify.downgrade_blocked` | WARN | Signed metadata indicates a downgrade attempt; install/upgrade blocked |
| `redwood.private.bundle.trust_store.unknown_key` | ERROR | Signature uses a key not present in trust store |
| `redwood.private.bundle.trust_store.updated` | INFO | Trust store updated (e.g., new public key added for rotation) |
| `redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.used` | CRITICAL | Break-glass override used to proceed despite verification issues |
| `redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.denied` | WARN | Break-glass requested but policy/guardrail denied |

### Failure reason codes
When emitting `...verify.failure` (and some related events), include `failure_reason` and (when possible) `failure_detail`.

| failure_reason | Typical root cause |
|---|---|
| `SIGNATURE_INVALID` | Signature bytes don’t validate against manifest digest |
| `SIGNATURE_EXPIRED` | Signing cert validity window ended (x509-based schemes) |
| `SIGNATURE_NOT_YET_VALID` | System time behind signing validity start; common in air-gapped time skew |
| `UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY` | Trust store missing the key used to sign this bundle |
| `MANIFEST_HASH_MISMATCH` | Manifest digest differs from expected/canonicalization mismatch or tampering |
| `ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH` | One or more artifacts referenced in manifest do not match recorded hashes |
| `MISSING_REQUIRED_ARTIFACT` | Bundle missing a required file referenced by manifest |
| `BUNDLE_FORMAT_UNSUPPORTED` | Installer too old to understand bundle format version |
| `DOWNGRADE_ATTEMPT` | Signed version metadata indicates downgrade vs installed state |
| `POLICY_BLOCKED` | Verification failed due to policy (e.g., disallowed algorithm) |
| `IO_ERROR` | Read error while hashing/verifying bundle contents |

---

## Event payload requirements by event
### `redwood.private.bundle.verify.started`
Required additional fields:
- `verification_mode`
- `bundle_id` (if manifest readable)
- `bundle_build_id` (if manifest readable)
- `bundle_format_version` (if manifest readable)

### `redwood.private.bundle.verify.success`
Required additional fields:
- `bundle_id`, `bundle_build_id`, `bundle_format_version`
- `bundle_manifest_digest_sha256`
- `signature_type`, `signing_key_id`, `trust_store_id`
- `verified_artifact_count` (string integer)

Optional:
- `sbom_digest_sha256` (if SBOM pointer present and hashed)
- `verification_duration_ms` (string integer)

### `redwood.private.bundle.verify.failure`
Required additional fields:
- `failure_reason`
- `failure_detail` (short, actionable; no secrets)
- `bundle_manifest_digest_sha256` (if computed)
- `signature_type` and `signing_key_id` (if identifiable)

Optional:
- `failed_artifact_path` (only when `--redact-paths=false`)
- `failed_artifact_digest_expected_sha256` / `failed_artifact_digest_actual_sha256` (allowed)
- `verification_duration_ms`

### `redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.used`
Required additional fields:
- `break_glass_ticket` (customer/internal ticket reference if provided)
- `break_glass_reason` (operator-provided; enforced non-empty)
- `break_glass_policy_version`
- `verification_mode`
- `failure_reason` (the reason being overridden, if known)

NOTE: Emission of this event MUST be accompanied by a visible warning to the operator and MUST also be included in the generated support bundle when enabled.

---

## Examples
### Success

### Success

### Failure (unknown key)
{"event_name":"redwood.private.bundle.trust_store.unknown_key","event_version":"1.0","event_id":"8c0f2b13-2d41-4c8f-9cd2-5d0e2d5a9b4e","occurred_at":"2026-01-20T19:03:02Z","severity":"ERROR","environment":"private","component":"redwood-private-installer","component_version":"2.18.0","host_id":"node-01","cluster_id":"unknown","request_id":"req-0e2c","actor_type":"human","actor_id":"installer-operator","verification_mode":"preflight","offline_mode":"true","bundle_id":"private-2026.01.0","bundle_build_id":"rbwpriv_2026.01.0_20260119.3","signature_type":"ed25519","signing_key_id":"ed25519:1a2b...","trust_store_id":"truststore-2025-10","failure_reason":"UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY","failure_detail":"Signing key not found in trust store. Update trust store per key rotation guide before retrying."}

### Break-glass used
{"event_name":"redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.used","event_version":"1.0","event_id":"7c9e7b2f-3e1a-4c8c-8a9d-1c4e3b5d2f11","occurred_at":"2026-01-20T19:06:44Z","severity":"CRITICAL","environment":"private","component":"redwood-private-installer","component_version":"2.18.0","host_id":"node-01","cluster_id":"unknown","request_id":"req-0e2c","actor_type":"human","actor_id":"installer-operator","verification_mode":"install","offline_mode":"true","bundle_id":"private-2026.01.0","bundle_build_id":"rbwpriv_2026.01.0_20260119.3","break_glass_ticket":"SUP-31977","break_glass_reason":"Customer environment missing updated trust store; urgent restore window approved by security lead","break_glass_policy_version":"2026-01","failure_reason":"UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY"}

---

## Local retention and rotation guidance
### Defaults (recommended)
- **Retention period:** 30 days
- **Max file size:** 100 MB per file
- **Max rotated files:** 10

### Minimum for compliance-oriented deployments
- **Retention period:** 90 days (or customer policy)
- Ensure rotated logs are backed up to customer-controlled archival storage.

### Tamper considerations
Redwood Private does not assume OS-level immutability in all customer environments. Customers requiring stronger guarantees should:
- Forward logs to an append-only target (WORM, SIEM, or immutable object storage) when feasible, OR
- Apply OS controls (e.g., auditd rules, file immutability, restricted admin access) per their policy.

---

## SIEM integration notes (audit-log-exporter)
### Mapping
- Event names map 1:1 to exporter event types.
- Exporter MUST preserve `event_name`, `event_version`, `occurred_at`, `severity`, and `event_id` unchanged.
- Exporter MAY add transport metadata (e.g., `export_destination`, `exported_at`) but must not alter the original payload.

### Recommended SIEM fields
Most SIEMs benefit from parsing:
- `event_name` (as category)
- `severity`
- `actor_id`
- `bundle_id`, `bundle_build_id`
- `failure_reason`
- `signing_key_id`, `trust_store_id`

### Air-gapped export pattern
If the deployment cannot reach a SIEM endpoint:
1. Rotate and seal logs on the Private host(s) (customer policy).
2. Copy rotated files to removable media.
3. Import into an internal SIEM in a controlled enclave.

---

## Operational notes
### Alerting recommendations
Customers (and Redwood internal runbooks for managed Private) should consider alerting on:
- Any `redwood.private.bundle.verify.failure`
- Any `redwood.private.bundle.break_glass.used`
- Spike in `UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY` after key rotation windows

### Support bundle inclusion
When support bundle generation is enabled, include:
- Last 200 lines of `bundle-verification.log`
- Trust store identifier and key list (public info only)
- Last verification result and `request_id`/`session_id` correlation IDs

---

## Change control
- Owner: Security Engineering (Audit logging/SIEM requirements) — primary: Kevin Osei.
- Any breaking changes to event names or required fields require:
  - Security review (Marcus Lin)
  - Observability/logging standards review (Allison Grant)
  - Exporter schema update (audit-log-exporter) and installer update in the same release train

## Changelog
- 2026-01-20: Published v1.0 taxonomy; added break-glass event requirements and air-gapped export guidance.
- 2025-12-05: Initial draft envelope and failure reason codes.

source.title: Offline Bundle Signing – Key Ceremony & Compliance Evidence Guide
source.author: Daniel Carter
source.body:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s **key ceremony and evidence collection procedure** for signing **offline bundles** used to install/upgrade **Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped)**.

It is written for:
- Security & Compliance (GRC) to collect **audit-ready evidence**
- Release Engineering / Build to execute a **repeatable** ceremony and ensure correct custody controls
- Security Engineering to validate threat-model mitigations and review artifacts

**Scope:** generation/rotation of offline bundle signing keys, approvals, required logs/artifacts, and standardized answers for customer security questionnaires.

**Confidentiality:** This document is **restricted**. Do not share externally. For customer-facing responses, use the approved response snippets in the “Customer questionnaire responses” section.

---

## System overview (what we are signing)
Offline installation bundles contain installer binaries, Helm charts/manifests, image references, runtime/model artifacts, and associated metadata. Integrity and provenance are enforced by:
1) A **bundle manifest** listing required artifacts and cryptographic hashes
2) A **detached signature** over the canonicalized manifest (e.g., `manifest.sig`)
3) A **trust store** shipped with the installer and updateable for key rotation

Installers verify signatures during **preflight** and fail closed unless a policy-gated break-glass path is used.

Authoritative references:
- Signing scheme: ADR-0147
- Pipeline controls: “Offline bundle signing CI pipeline”
- Rotation operations: “Release signing key rotation runbook”

---

## Roles and responsibilities (RACI)
**Key principle:** separation of duties and two-person control for key creation/activation.

### Required roles
- **Ceremony Lead (Release Engineering):** Connor O'Brien (or delegate)
  - Runs the checklist, coordinates access, ensures artifacts are captured.
- **Security Officer (Security leadership):** Dr. Aisha Rahman (or delegate)
  - Approves key creation/rotation and attests to compliance with policy.
- **Security Engineering Reviewer:** Marcus Lin (or delegate)
  - Confirms threat-model mitigations, validates configuration (non-exportable keys, audit logs enabled).
- **KMS/HSM Operator:** Hanae Suzuki (or delegate)
  - Executes hardware-backed key generation and policy configuration.
- **Evidence Recorder (GRC):** Olga Petrov or Daniel Carter
  - Captures evidence, stores it in the correct evidence repository, and updates the evidence index.

### Optional attendees
- **Build/CI owner:** Sahana Iyer
- **SRE on-call representative:** Sean Gallagher

### RACI summary
- Generate key material: **Responsible:** KMS/HSM Operator; **Accountable:** Security Officer
- Enable signing in CI: **Responsible:** Release Eng + Build/CI; **Accountable:** Release Eng Lead
- Evidence collection: **Responsible:** Evidence Recorder; **Accountable:** GRC Lead
- Final sign-off to use key for releases: **Responsible:** Security Eng + Release Eng; **Accountable:** Security Officer

---

## Preconditions (must be true before a ceremony)
1) **Approved change record** exists (Linear/Jira) with scope (new key vs rotation vs emergency) and requested effective date.
2) **Threat model actions** are not blocked (see threat model page for current required mitigations).
3) Signing environment is ready:
   - HSM/KMS reachable from the signing service in the prod release environment
   - Audit logging enabled for KMS/HSM and signing service
   - RBAC role `release-signing-operator` in place with least privilege (see RBAC policy bundle PRs)
4) **Key naming convention** and identifiers are defined:
   - Key purpose: `offline-bundle-signing`
   - Environment: `prod`
   - Key ID format: `rwi-offline-prod-<YYYYMM>-<seq>`
   - Public key fingerprint recorded in evidence
5) Installer trust store update plan is ready if this is a rotation:
   - Installer version(s) that will ship new trust roots
   - Backward compatibility window for older installers

---

## Key ceremony procedure (step-by-step)
### 0. Scheduling and access validation
- Ceremony is scheduled for a time when:
  - Ceremony Lead, Security Officer, Security Eng Reviewer, KMS/HSM Operator, Evidence Recorder can attend
  - Release pipeline is not actively cutting a Private release
- Validate attendees have:
  - Required SSO group membership
  - Just-in-time privileged access (time-bound)

**Evidence to capture:** calendar invite, attendee list, access approval/ticket IDs, time-bounded access logs.

### 1. Start-of-ceremony attestation
Ceremony Lead reads and records:
- Purpose (new key vs rotation)
- Change record ID
- Target effective date
- Expected outputs and storage locations

All required attendees confirm:
- Their identity
- Their role
- They understand confidentiality and handling requirements

**Evidence to capture:** ceremony script checklist (completed), attendance confirmation.

### 2. Generate hardware-backed signing key (non-exportable)
KMS/HSM Operator generates a new signing key under the approved key policy.
Minimum requirements:
- Private key is **non-exportable**
- Key usage limited to **sign/verify** as applicable
- Key is only callable by the **signing service** (not general CI runners)
- Administrative actions require elevated approval

Record the following metadata:
- Key ID / Resource name
- Algorithm and curve (per ADR-0147)
- Creation timestamp
- Key policy / access principals
- Attestation that private key material was never exported

**Evidence to capture:** screenshots/exports of key metadata, policy document hash, KMS/HSM audit log entries for `CreateKey` / `SetPolicy`.

### 3. Configure signing service + RBAC (separation of duties)
Release Eng configures the signing service to reference the new key and enforces:
- Only the release signing workflow can request signatures
- Requests require release identity (workload identity) and are constrained to the offline bundle manifest signing operation
- Human approval gates for production signing changes

**Evidence to capture:**
- PRs/changes to signing service configuration (commit hashes)
- RBAC policy change record and approval
- CI/CD pipeline run logs showing the configuration change applied

### 4. Produce and verify a test signature (staging bundle)
Run a controlled test:
1) Generate a test offline bundle manifest (non-production test artifacts)
2) Sign the manifest using the new key
3) Verify signature using installer preflight (or verification tool)
4) Validate failure modes by tampering (manifest modification should fail verification)

Acceptance criteria:
- Verification succeeds with untampered manifest
- Verification fails closed on any tamper
- Audit event(s) are emitted locally (as defined in audit event schema)

**Evidence to capture:**
- CI job/run ID for test signing
- Verification logs from installer/preflight
- Audit event samples (redacted)

### 5. Publish public key material / trust root update plan
KMS/HSM Operator or Release Eng publishes the **public key** and metadata to the trusted distribution mechanism used by Redwood Private installer.
Requirements:
- Public key is distributed with:
  - Key ID
  - Fingerprint
  - Validity/activation date
  - Rotation notes (if applicable)
- Older keys remain trusted during the defined transition period (unless emergency revocation)

**Evidence to capture:** public key artifact hash, installer trust store PR link, release notes draft reference.

### 6. Final approvals and sign-off
Security Officer provides final approval that:
- Key custody controls meet policy
- Audit logging is enabled and evidence captured
- Rotation communications plan is ready (if applicable)

Release Eng Lead confirms operational readiness:
- Pipeline gating is enabled (release cannot cut without signed bundles)
- Runbooks updated

**Evidence to capture:** signed-off approval record (ticket comment + approver identity), ceremony completion checklist.

---

## Evidence artifacts (what to store and where)
Store evidence in the **Security & Compliance evidence repository** under:
`Security & Compliance > Compliance Evidence > Offline Bundle Signing > <YYYY> > Key Ceremony - <Key ID>`

### Required evidence checklist
1) **Change record** (Linear/Jira) link and summary
2) Attendee list and roles
3) Access approvals for privileged actions (JIT access logs)
4) KMS/HSM key metadata:
   - Key ID, algorithm, created_at
   - Non-exportability attestation
   - Key policy (or policy hash) showing allowed principals
5) KMS/HSM audit logs:
   - Create key
   - Policy changes
   - Enable/disable operations
6) Signing service configuration changes:
   - PR links/commit SHAs
   - Deployment logs
7) Test signing job output:
   - Pipeline run ID
   - Generated signature file hash
   - Verification logs (success + expected failure)
8) Trust store update artifacts:
   - Public key fingerprint
   - Installer change PR link
   - Effective version(s) and rollback plan
9) Final approvals:
   - Security Officer approval record
   - Security Eng Review sign-off
10) Customer enablement references (as applicable):
   - Planned docs update link
   - Support readiness ticket link

### Evidence quality notes
- Redact secrets and customer identifiers.
- Prefer immutable evidence: exported logs, signed approvals, commit hashes.
- Ensure timestamps are present and consistent.

---

## Controls mapping (internal)
This ceremony supports the following control themes (map to your framework in the evidence index):
- Supply chain integrity for shipped software
- Cryptographic signing of release artifacts
- Key management (generation, storage, access control, rotation)
- Change management and approvals
- Audit logging and tamper-evident records

For customer audits (SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / internal procurement), reference:
- Artifact signing standard
- Threat model page for offline signing
- Key rotation runbook
- Exception process for unsigned/hotfix bundles

---

## Customer security questionnaire responses (approved snippets)
Use the following responses as a baseline. Do not share internal key policies, raw logs, or access group names.

### 1) “Do you sign offline install media? What is signed?”
Redwood provides offline installation bundles for Redwood Private that include a manifest of included artifacts and cryptographic hashes. The manifest is cryptographically signed by Redwood. The installer verifies the signature and the artifact hashes before proceeding.

### 2) “What algorithms do you use for signing and hashing?”
Redwood uses an industry-standard asymmetric signing algorithm and strong cryptographic hashing for bundle integrity. The signature covers a canonicalized manifest to ensure deterministic verification. (We can provide the exact algorithm identifiers under NDA if required.)

### 3) “How are signing keys protected?”
Signing keys are generated and stored in a hardware-backed key management system. Private key material is not exportable. Access is restricted by least privilege and separation of duties, and key operations are logged.

### 4) “Is signing automated? Who can trigger signing?”
Signing is performed by the release pipeline under a controlled release identity. Configuration changes and production key usage are governed by approvals. Only authorized release workflows can request signatures.

### 5) “Do you have audit logs for signing operations?”
Yes. Key operations and signing requests are logged. For Redwood Private, verification outcomes are also logged locally on the customer side, and can be exported to SIEM where supported.

### 6) “How do you handle key rotation and revocation?”
Redwood supports key rotation via a trust store mechanism that allows multiple trusted public keys during transition windows. Rotation and emergency response procedures are documented and tested. If revocation is required, Redwood will provide updated trust roots and guidance for customers operating in air-gapped environments.

### 7) “What happens if signature verification fails?”
The installer fails closed and provides actionable error messages. Customers can run an offline inspection/verification command to diagnose issues (e.g., tampering, transfer corruption, unknown key). A controlled break-glass path exists for emergency use with explicit logging and approvals.

### 8) “Can customers use their own signing keys?”
Customer-managed signing roots are not supported in the initial release. Redwood can share a roadmap direction for customer key delegation based on requirements.

---

## Break-glass and exceptions (summary)
Break-glass is intended for emergency recovery when normal verification cannot be performed (e.g., operational constraints in an incident).
- Requires explicit approval per the exception policy
- Must produce local audit markers and warnings
- Must be remediated: return to normal verification and ship a properly signed bundle as soon as possible

See: “Exception process – unsigned bundle hotfix.”

---

## FAQ (internal)
**Q: Why do we require two-person control?**
A: To reduce insider risk and align with common customer expectations for signing key custody.

**Q: What evidence do customers typically ask for?**
A: They usually want: confirmation of signing, key protection summary (HSM/KMS), rotation policy, and auditability. They rarely need raw ceremony artifacts; provide high-level policy statements and, when needed, an attestation letter coordinated by GRC.

**Q: Where do we record the currently active key ID/fingerprint?**
A: In the internal evidence index and the release engineering runbook. Do not publish fingerprints publicly unless the documentation plan explicitly calls for it.

---

## Document history
- 2025-11-12: Initial version for Phase 1 implementation (ceremony definition + evidence checklist).
- 2026-01-18: Updated for pilot learnings: clarified audit log artifacts, trust store transition evidence, and questionnaire response language.

---

## Break-glass and exceptions (summary)
Break-glass is intended for emergency recovery when normal verification cannot be performed (e.g., operational constraints in an incident).
- Requires explicit approval per the exception policy
- Must produce local audit markers and warnings
- Must be remediated: return to normal verification and ship a properly signed bundle as soon as possible

See: “Exception process – unsigned bundle hotfix.”

---

## FAQ (internal)
**Q: Why do we require two-person control?**
A: To reduce insider risk and align with common customer expectations for signing key custody.

**Q: What evidence do customers typically ask for?**
A: They usually want: confirmation of signing, key protection summary (HSM/KMS), rotation policy, and auditability. They rarely need raw ceremony artifacts; provide high-level policy statements and, when needed, an attestation letter coordinated by GRC.

**Q: Where do we record the currently active key ID/fingerprint?**
A: In the internal evidence index and the release engineering runbook. Do not publish fingerprints publicly unless the documentation plan explicitly calls for it.

---

## Document history
- 2025-11-12: Initial version for Phase 1 implementation (ceremony definition + evidence checklist).
- 2026-01-18: Updated for pilot learnings: clarified audit log artifacts, trust store transition evidence, and questionnaire response language.
text:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s **key ceremony and evidence collection procedure** for signing **offline bundles** used to install/upgrade **Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped)**.

It is written for:
- Security & Compliance (GRC) to collect **audit-ready evidence**
- Release Engineering / Build to execute a **repeatable** ceremony and ensure correct custody controls
- Security Engineering to validate threat-model mitigations and review artifacts

**Scope:** generation/rotation of offline bundle signing keys, approvals, required logs/artifacts, and standardized answers for customer security questionnaires.

**Confidentiality:** This document is **restricted**. Do not share externally. For customer-facing responses, use the approved response snippets in the “Customer questionnaire responses” section.

---

## System overview (what we are signing)
Offline installation bundles contain installer binaries, Helm charts/manifests, image references, runtime/model artifacts, and associated metadata. Integrity and provenance are enforced by:
1) A **bundle manifest** listing required artifacts and cryptographic hashes
2) A **detached signature** over the canonicalized manifest (e.g., `manifest.sig`)
3) A **trust store** shipped with the installer and updateable for key rotation

Installers verify signatures during **preflight** and fail closed unless a policy-gated break-glass path is used.

Authoritative references:
- Signing scheme: ADR-0147
- Pipeline controls: “Offline bundle signing CI pipeline”
- Rotation operations: “Release signing key rotation runbook”

---

## Roles and responsibilities (RACI)
**Key principle:** separation of duties and two-person control for key creation/activation.

### Required roles
- **Ceremony Lead (Release Engineering):** Connor O'Brien (or delegate)
  - Runs the checklist, coordinates access, ensures artifacts are captured.
- **Security Officer (Security leadership):** Dr. Aisha Rahman (or delegate)
  - Approves key creation/rotation and attests to compliance with policy.
- **Security Engineering Reviewer:** Marcus Lin (or delegate)
  - Confirms threat-model mitigations, validates configuration (non-exportable keys, audit logs enabled).
- **KMS/HSM Operator:** Hanae Suzuki (or delegate)
  - Executes hardware-backed key generation and policy configuration.
- **Evidence Recorder (GRC):** Olga Petrov or Daniel Carter
  - Captures evidence, stores it in the correct evidence repository, and updates the evidence index.

### Optional attendees
- **Build/CI owner:** Sahana Iyer
- **SRE on-call representative:** Sean Gallagher

### RACI summary
- Generate key material: **Responsible:** KMS/HSM Operator; **Accountable:** Security Officer
- Enable signing in CI: **Responsible:** Release Eng + Build/CI; **Accountable:** Release Eng Lead
- Evidence collection: **Responsible:** Evidence Recorder; **Accountable:** GRC Lead
- Final sign-off to use key for releases: **Responsible:** Security Eng + Release Eng; **Accountable:** Security Officer

---

## Preconditions (must be true before a ceremony)
1) **Approved change record** exists (Linear/Jira) with scope (new key vs rotation vs emergency) and requested effective date.
2) **Threat model actions** are not blocked (see threat model page for current required mitigations).
3) Signing environment is ready:
   - HSM/KMS reachable from the signing service in the prod release environment
   - Audit logging enabled for KMS/HSM and signing service
   - RBAC role `release-signing-operator` in place with least privilege (see RBAC policy bundle PRs)
4) **Key naming convention** and identifiers are defined:
   - Key purpose: `offline-bundle-signing`
   - Environment: `prod`
   - Key ID format: `rwi-offline-prod-<YYYYMM>-<seq>`
   - Public key fingerprint recorded in evidence
5) Installer trust store update plan is ready if this is a rotation:
   - Installer version(s) that will ship new trust roots
   - Backward compatibility window for older installers

---

## Key ceremony procedure (step-by-step)
### 0. Scheduling and access validation
- Ceremony is scheduled for a time when:
  - Ceremony Lead, Security Officer, Security Eng Reviewer, KMS/HSM Operator, Evidence Recorder can attend
  - Release pipeline is not actively cutting a Private release
- Validate attendees have:
  - Required SSO group membership
  - Just-in-time privileged access (time-bound)

**Evidence to capture:** calendar invite, attendee list, access approval/ticket IDs, time-bounded access logs.

### 1. Start-of-ceremony attestation
Ceremony Lead reads and records:
- Purpose (new key vs rotation)
- Change record ID
- Target effective date
- Expected outputs and storage locations

All required attendees confirm:
- Their identity
- Their role
- They understand confidentiality and handling requirements

**Evidence to capture:** ceremony script checklist (completed), attendance confirmation.

### 2. Generate hardware-backed signing key (non-exportable)
KMS/HSM Operator generates a new signing key under the approved key policy.
Minimum requirements:
- Private key is **non-exportable**
- Key usage limited to **sign/verify** as applicable
- Key is only callable by the **signing service** (not general CI runners)
- Administrative actions require elevated approval

Record the following metadata:
- Key ID / Resource name
- Algorithm and curve (per ADR-0147)
- Creation timestamp
- Key policy / access principals
- Attestation that private key material was never exported

**Evidence to capture:** screenshots/exports of key metadata, policy document hash, KMS/HSM audit log entries for `CreateKey` / `SetPolicy`.

### 3. Configure signing service + RBAC (separation of duties)
Release Eng configures the signing service to reference the new key and enforces:
- Only the release signing workflow can request signatures
- Requests require release identity (workload identity) and are constrained to the offline bundle manifest signing operation
- Human approval gates for production signing changes

**Evidence to capture:**
- PRs/changes to signing service configuration (commit hashes)
- RBAC policy change record and approval
- CI/CD pipeline run logs showing the configuration change applied

### 4. Produce and verify a test signature (staging bundle)
Run a controlled test:
1) Generate a test offline bundle manifest (non-production test artifacts)
2) Sign the manifest using the new key
3) Verify signature using installer preflight (or verification tool)
4) Validate failure modes by tampering (manifest modification should fail verification)

Acceptance criteria:
- Verification succeeds with untampered manifest
- Verification fails closed on any tamper
- Audit event(s) are emitted locally (as defined in audit event schema)

**Evidence to capture:**
- CI job/run ID for test signing
- Verification logs from installer/preflight
- Audit event samples (redacted)

### 5. Publish public key material / trust root update plan
KMS/HSM Operator or Release Eng publishes the **public key** and metadata to the trusted distribution mechanism used by Redwood Private installer.
Requirements:
- Public key is distributed with:
  - Key ID
  - Fingerprint
  - Validity/activation date
  - Rotation notes (if applicable)
- Older keys remain trusted during the defined transition period (unless emergency revocation)

**Evidence to capture:** public key artifact hash, installer trust store PR link, release notes draft reference.

### 6. Final approvals and sign-off
Security Officer provides final approval that:
- Key custody controls meet policy
- Audit logging is enabled and evidence captured
- Rotation communications plan is ready (if applicable)

Release Eng Lead confirms operational readiness:
- Pipeline gating is enabled (release cannot cut without signed bundles)
- Runbooks updated

**Evidence to capture:** signed-off approval record (ticket comment + approver identity), ceremony completion checklist.

---

## Evidence artifacts (what to store and where)
Store evidence in the **Security & Compliance evidence repository** under:
`Security & Compliance > Compliance Evidence > Offline Bundle Signing > <YYYY> > Key Ceremony - <Key ID>`

### Required evidence checklist
1) **Change record** (Linear/Jira) link and summary
2) Attendee list and roles
3) Access approvals for privileged actions (JIT access logs)
4) KMS/HSM key metadata:
   - Key ID, algorithm, created_at
   - Non-exportability attestation
   - Key policy (or policy hash) showing allowed principals
5) KMS/HSM audit logs:
   - Create key
   - Policy changes
   - Enable/disable operations
6) Signing service configuration changes:
   - PR links/commit SHAs
   - Deployment logs
7) Test signing job output:
   - Pipeline run ID
   - Generated signature file hash
   - Verification logs (success + expected failure)
8) Trust store update artifacts:
   - Public key fingerprint
   - Installer change PR link
   - Effective version(s) and rollback plan
9) Final approvals:
   - Security Officer approval record
   - Security Eng Review sign-off
10) Customer enablement references (as applicable):
   - Planned docs update link
   - Support readiness ticket link

### Evidence quality notes
- Redact secrets and customer identifiers.
- Prefer immutable evidence: exported logs, signed approvals, commit hashes.
- Ensure timestamps are present and consistent.

---

## Controls mapping (internal)
This ceremony supports the following control themes (map to your framework in the evidence index):
- Supply chain integrity for shipped software
- Cryptographic signing of release artifacts
- Key management (generation, storage, access control, rotation)
- Change management and approvals
- Audit logging and tamper-evident records

For customer audits (SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / internal procurement), reference:
- Artifact signing standard
- Threat model page for offline signing
- Key rotation runbook
- Exception process for unsigned/hotfix bundles

---

## Customer security questionnaire responses (approved snippets)
Use the following responses as a baseline. Do not share internal key policies, raw logs, or access group names.

### 1) “Do you sign offline install media? What is signed?”
Redwood provides offline installation bundles for Redwood Private that include a manifest of included artifacts and cryptographic hashes. The manifest is cryptographically signed by Redwood. The installer verifies the signature and the artifact hashes before proceeding.

### 2) “What algorithms do you use for signing and hashing?”
Redwood uses an industry-standard asymmetric signing algorithm and strong cryptographic hashing for bundle integrity. The signature covers a canonicalized manifest to ensure deterministic verification. (We can provide the exact algorithm identifiers under NDA if required.)

### 3) “How are signing keys protected?”
Signing keys are generated and stored in a hardware-backed key management system. Private key material is not exportable. Access is restricted by least privilege and separation of duties, and key operations are logged.

### 4) “Is signing automated? Who can trigger signing?”
Signing is performed by the release pipeline under a controlled release identity. Configuration changes and production key usage are governed by approvals. Only authorized release workflows can request signatures.

### 5) “Do you have audit logs for signing operations?”
Yes. Key operations and signing requests are logged. For Redwood Private, verification outcomes are also logged locally on the customer side, and can be exported to SIEM where supported.

### 6) “How do you handle key rotation and revocation?”
Redwood supports key rotation via a trust store mechanism that allows multiple trusted public keys during transition windows. Rotation and emergency response procedures are documented and tested. If revocation is required, Redwood will provide updated trust roots and guidance for customers operating in air-gapped environments.

### 7) “What happens if signature verification fails?”
The installer fails closed and provides actionable error messages. Customers can run an offline inspection/verification command to diagnose issues (e.g., tampering, transfer corruption, unknown key). A controlled break-glass path exists for emergency use with explicit logging and approvals.

### 8) “Can customers use their own signing keys?”
Customer-managed signing roots are not supported in the initial release. Redwood can share a roadmap direction for customer key delegation based on requirements.

---

## Break-glass and exceptions (summary)
Break-glass is intended for emergency recovery when normal verification cannot be performed (e.g., operational constraints in an incident).
- Requires explicit approval per the exception policy
- Must produce local audit markers and warnings
- Must be remediated: return to normal verification and ship a properly signed bundle as soon as possible

See: “Exception process – unsigned bundle hotfix.”

---

## FAQ (internal)
**Q: Why do we require two-person control?**
A: To reduce insider risk and align with common customer expectations for signing key custody.

**Q: What evidence do customers typically ask for?**
A: They usually want: confirmation of signing, key protection summary (HSM/KMS), rotation policy, and auditability. They rarely need raw ceremony artifacts; provide high-level policy statements and, when needed, an attestation letter coordinated by GRC.

**Q: Where do we record the currently active key ID/fingerprint?**
A: In the internal evidence index and the release engineering runbook. Do not publish fingerprints publicly unless the documentation plan explicitly calls for it.

---

## Document history
- 2025-11-12: Initial version for Phase 1 implementation (ceremony definition + evidence checklist).
- 2026-01-18: Updated for pilot learnings: clarified audit log artifacts, trust store transition evidence, and questionnaire response language.

source.title: Forensic Log Preservation and Legal Hold Playbook
source.author: Asha Patel
source.body:
Overview

This playbook defines Redwood Inference's standard operating procedure for preserving forensic logging artifacts and initiating a legal hold during a security incident that may have legal or regulatory implications. It covers: scope, responsibilities, technical collection steps, integrity verification, short- and long-term storage, RBAC controls during evidence handling, and coordination with Legal and Compliance.

Intended audience

- Incident responders (eng-sre, security ops)
- Platform engineers who own logging/export pipelines
- Legal and Compliance contacts handling preservation requests
- Engineering managers coordinating access and custody

Scope

Applies to all production and staging systems that generate audit logs, access logs, request traces, application-level telemetry, and associated artifacts that may be relevant to an incident. This includes:

- API gateway and request audit logs (redwood-gateway-audit)
- Serving runtime telemetry (runtime-metrics and kernel traces)
- Authentication and authorization logs (auth-service, IAM audit)
- Orchestration and scheduling logs (kube-audit, autoscaler events)
- Network flow captures and firewall logs (vpc-flow-logs)
- Customer-facing telemetry when subject to legal hold (consult Legal)

Definitions

- Forensic artifact: any file or record collected for the purpose of investigation or regulatory review.
- Legal hold: an instruction to preserve specific data from normal retention/deletion, initiated by Legal/Compliance.
- Collector: an authorized engineer or security operator who executes the collection steps.

Ownership and responsibilities

- Incident Commander (IC): declares evidence collection, coordinates with Legal, approves access escalations.
- Security Ops: executes collection playbook, ensures integrity, performs initial triage.
- Platform Storage Owner: prepares secured preservation buckets and ensures encryption/KMS.
- IAM/RBAC Owner: applies temporary access changes and documents approvals (break-glass).
- Legal/Compliance: issues the legal hold, defines custodians, and provides retention directives.

Pre-requisites (before collection)

1. Confirm scope and list of systems from IC + Legal.
2. Identify owners and responsible engineers for each system.
3. Ensure a preservation S3 (or cloud equivalent) bucket exists: redwood-preserve-{incident-id} with encryption enabled and restricted ACLs.
4. Ensure an evidence KMS key is provisioned and access is limited to security and legal service principals.
5. Verify audit logging pipelines are writable (no disk pressure) and snapshot capability exists for long-term storage.

High-level collection timeline (SLOs)

- Initial collection (critical artifacts): target within 4 hours of incident declaration.
- Full snapshot of relevant clusters/services: target within 24 hours.
- Evidence integrity manifest produced and stored: within 48 hours.

Step-by-step collection playbook

1) Triage and scope confirmation
   - IC confirms incident ID and legal hold scope.
   - Security Ops creates the preservation bucket and a ticket in the incident tracker with the tag: evidence-preserve/{incident-id}.

2) Lock down retention and prevent automated deletion
   - For each candidate bucket or index, apply an immediate retention override: set lifecycle to "retain" or disable deletion APIs.
   - Example (AWS CLI):
     aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration --bucket my-audit-bucket --lifecycle-configuration '{"Rules":[{"ID":"preserve-incident-123","Status":"Enabled","AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload":{"DaysAfterInitiation":36500}}]}'

3) Collect primary artifacts (critical first)
   - API gateway audit (last 30 days and live tail):
     aws s3 cp s3://redwood-gateway-audit/2025/02/ --recursive s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/ingest/gateway-audit/ --profile preserve-role
\   - Kubernetes audit logs: generate a cluster snapshot with kube-audit-collector and compress:
     kubectl --context=prod get events --all-namespaces -o json > prod-cluster-events.json
     tar -czf prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz prod-cluster-events.json /var/log/kube-apiserver/audit.log

4) Capture ephemeral state and KV caches
   - Export model-serving KV caches if relevant (coordination with runtime team). Use runtime export tool: runtimectl export-cache --model-id m-123 --since 2025-01-01 --out cache-export.pb

5) Network-level artifacts
   - If packet captures are required, coordinate with network team:
     tcpdump -i eth0 -s 0 -w /tmp/incident-123.pcap 'host 10.20.30.0/24'
     gcloud compute scp compute-instance:/tmp/incident-123.pcap . && aws s3 cp incident-123.pcap s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/network/

6) Database and object storage snapshots
   - Request DB snapshot via platform owner. For Postgres: create a read-replica snapshot and export logical dumps:
     pg_dump --host=db-read.prod.redwood.internal --username=readonly --format=custom --file=prod-incident-123.dump --schema=public

Integrity verification and manifest

- For every collected artifact produce a SHA-256 hash and add a line to a manifest file: manifest-INC-2025-02.txt
  Example manifest line: sha256sum prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz  prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz

- Sign the manifest with the collector's GPG key (if available) or store signed approval in the incident ticket.
  gpg --detach-sign --armor manifest-INC-2025-02.txt

- Example manifest header:
  Incident: INC-2025-02
  Collector: asha.patel@redwood.com
  CollectedAt: 2025-02-18T14:23:00Z

Packaging and encryption

- All artifacts MUST be uploaded to the preservation bucket within a directory named by incident ID and collection phase (e.g. s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/).
- Apply server-side encryption with evidence KMS key and enforce bucket policy to deny DeleteBucket and DeleteObject unless with break-glass approval.

- Example upload command:
  aws s3 cp prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/ --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:123456:key/abcd-ef01-2345

RBAC controls and emergency access (break-glass)

- Normal principle: least privilege. During preservation the following special rules apply:
  - Create a short-lived "evidence-access" role that grants: s3:GetObject, s3:ListBucket for redwood-preserve-{incident-id}, kms:Decrypt on evidence KMS key, and readonly access to index/search artifacts.
  - Access request must include: IC approval, Legal approval, and a Security Manager signature in the incident ticket.
  - All role assumptions are logged and a 24/7 oncall approver is designated.

- Break-glass checklist: time, approver name, justification, artifacts accessed, TTL of credential (max 8 hours), and post-access audit notes.

Legal hold coordination

1. Legal issues hold directive with scope and custodians. Security Ops tags the incident ticket and adds the hold label: legal-hold/INC-2025-02.
2. Custodians are notified with the required actions and stop-delete instructions.
3. Security Ops schedules weekly retention confirmation with Legal until release.

Evidence handoff to Legal or external counsel

- Handoff package should include: artifact tarball(s), manifest (sha256 and signed), collection timeline, collector identities, RBAC/approval log, and storage location.
- Preferred handoff: provide access to the preservation bucket with a time-limited read-only role rather than transferring copies. If copies are transferred, document chain of custody and destroy copies only with Legal approval.

Chain-of-custody form (ASCII table template)

| Item ID | Artifact path | Collected by | Collected at (UTC) | Hash (sha256) | Current Holder | Notes |
|---------|----------------|--------------|---------------------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz | asha.patel | 2025-02-18T14:23:00Z | abcd1234... | redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02 | initial collect |

Retention mapping and cross-region residency

- Legal/Compliance will specify retention periods (e.g., 7 years, 3 years, indefinite). Map retention requirements to storage classes and region restrictions:
  - Short-term (<=90 days): standard encrypted buckets in originating region.
  - Long-term (>90 days): move to dedicated preservation account with cross-region replication disabled if data residency prohibits replication.

- If the incident involves cross-border data, Legal will provide custody restrictions. Preservation buckets may be placed in-region only (e.g., eu-west-1) and access limited to EU-based engineers where required.

Secure SDLC tie-ins and post-incident actions

- Root cause findings that require code or infra changes MUST be triaged through our Secure SDLC process: open an RFC, perform a threat model update, and require change review with Security and SRE.
- Add verification suites to CI that assert: audit logging is enabled, log rotation retains at least N days, and alerting for missing streams is in place.

Evidence review and purge release

- Legal issues a release when the hold is lifted. Security Ops performs a release checklist: verify signed release, document final custody, and either remove retention locks or schedule normal lifecycle.

Audit logging configuration checklist (minimum)

- All services generate structured logs (JSON) with: timestamp, request_id, user_id, service, operation, source_ip, result_code.
- Centralized ingest to the audit pipeline with guaranteed delivery (SQS/Kafka backed) and monitoring on ingestion lag (>5m alerts).
- Retention policies defined per environment and override mechanism for legal hold.

Configuration snippet examples

- Minimal structured audit example (JSON line):
  {"ts":"2025-02-18T14:12:03Z","request_id":"r-7a2f","user":"service-account:ingest","svc":"gateway","op":"POST /v1/generate","src_ip":"198.51.100.23","result":200} 

- Simple lifecycle rule example (AWS bucket):
  {
    "Rules": [
      {
        "ID": "preserve-INC-2025-02",
        "Status": "Enabled",
        "NoncurrentVersionExpiration": {"NoncurrentDays": 36500}
      }
    ]
  }

Post-collection audits and logging of access

- All access to preserved artifacts must be logged and reviewed weekly by Security and Legal. Any unauthorized access triggers an immediate re-evaluation of custody and potential re-collection.

Related pages and references

- Incident response playbook (link: confluence://eng-sre/incident-response-playbook)
- Audit logging standards (link: confluence://security-and-compliance/audit-logging/standards)
- RBAC emergency escalation procedure (link: confluence://security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/emergency-escalation)

Appendix: common CLI commands and snippets

- Compute SHA-256: sha256sum artifact.tar.gz
- Upload with KMS: aws s3 cp artifact.tar.gz s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/ --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id <evidence-key-arn>
- Generate signed manifest: gpg --detach-sign --armor manifest-INC-2025-02.txt

Approval and change history

- 2025-02-18: Drafted by Asha Patel (security).
- 2025-03-02: Reviewed by Marco Hernandez (security ops) and Liang Zhou (platform).
- 2026-01-12: Updated retention mapping and cross-region guidance after legal review.

Contact

Security ops oncall: security-oncall@redwood.com
Legal contact: legal-preserve@redwood.com
