  CollectedAt: 2025-02-18T14:23:00Z

Packaging and encryption

- All artifacts MUST be uploaded to the preservation bucket within a directory named by incident ID and collection phase (e.g. s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/).
- Apply server-side encryption with evidence KMS key and enforce bucket policy to deny DeleteBucket and DeleteObject unless with break-glass approval.

- Example upload command:
  aws s3 cp prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/ --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:123456:key/abcd-ef01-2345

RBAC controls and emergency access (break-glass)

- Normal principle: least privilege. During preservation the following special rules apply:
  - Create a short-lived "evidence-access" role that grants: s3:GetObject, s3:ListBucket for redwood-preserve-{incident-id}, kms:Decrypt on evidence KMS key, and readonly access to index/search artifacts.
  - Access request must include: IC approval, Legal approval, and a Security Manager signature in the incident ticket.
  - All role assumptions are logged and a 24/7 oncall approver is designated.

- Break-glass checklist: time, approver name, justification, artifacts accessed, TTL of credential (max 8 hours), and post-access audit notes.

Legal hold coordination

1. Legal issues hold directive with scope and custodians. Security Ops tags the incident ticket and adds the hold label: legal-hold/INC-2025-02.
2. Custodians are notified with the required actions and stop-delete instructions.
3. Security Ops schedules weekly retention confirmation with Legal until release.

Evidence handoff to Legal or external counsel

- Handoff package should include: artifact tarball(s), manifest (sha256 and signed), collection timeline, collector identities, RBAC/approval log, and storage location.
- Preferred handoff: provide access to the preservation bucket with a time-limited read-only role rather than transferring copies. If copies are transferred, document chain of custody and destroy copies only with Legal approval.

Chain-of-custody form (ASCII table template)

| Item ID | Artifact path | Collected by | Collected at (UTC) | Hash (sha256) | Current Holder | Notes |
|---------|----------------|--------------|---------------------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz | asha.patel | 2025-02-18T14:23:00Z | abcd1234... | redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02 | initial collect |

Retention mapping and cross-region residency

- Legal/Compliance will specify retention periods (e.g., 7 years, 3 years, indefinite). Map retention requirements to storage classes and region restrictions:
  - Short-term (<=90 days): standard encrypted buckets in originating region.
  - Long-term (>90 days): move to dedicated preservation account with cross-region replication disabled if data residency prohibits replication.

- If the incident involves cross-border data, Legal will provide custody restrictions. Preservation buckets may be placed in-region only (e.g., eu-west-1) and access limited to EU-based engineers where required.

Secure SDLC tie-ins and post-incident actions

- Root cause findings that require code or infra changes MUST be triaged through our Secure SDLC process: open an RFC, perform a threat model update, and require change review with Security and SRE.
- Add verification suites to CI that assert: audit logging is enabled, log rotation retains at least N days, and alerting for missing streams is in place.

Evidence review and purge release

- Legal issues a release when the hold is lifted. Security Ops performs a release checklist: verify signed release, document final custody, and either remove retention locks or schedule normal lifecycle.

Audit logging configuration checklist (minimum)

- All services generate structured logs (JSON) with: timestamp, request_id, user_id, service, operation, source_ip, result_code.
- Centralized ingest to the audit pipeline with guaranteed delivery (SQS/Kafka backed) and monitoring on ingestion lag (>5m alerts).
- Retention policies defined per environment and override mechanism for legal hold.

Configuration snippet examples

- Minimal structured audit example (JSON line):
  {"ts":"2025-02-18T14:12:03Z","request_id":"r-7a2f","user":"service-account:ingest","svc":"gateway","op":"POST /v1/generate","src_ip":"198.51.100.23","result":200} 

- Simple lifecycle rule example (AWS bucket):
  {
    "Rules": [
      {
        "ID": "preserve-INC-2025-02",
        "Status": "Enabled",
        "NoncurrentVersionExpiration": {"NoncurrentDays": 36500}
      }
    ]
  }

Post-collection audits and logging of access

- All access to preserved artifacts must be logged and reviewed weekly by Security and Legal. Any unauthorized access triggers an immediate re-evaluation of custody and potential re-collection.

Related pages and references

- Incident response playbook (link: confluence://eng-sre/incident-response-playbook)
- Audit logging standards (link: confluence://security-and-compliance/audit-logging/standards)
- RBAC emergency escalation procedure (link: confluence://security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/emergency-escalation)

Appendix: common CLI commands and snippets

- Compute SHA-256: sha256sum artifact.tar.gz
- Upload with KMS: aws s3 cp artifact.tar.gz s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/ --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id <evidence-key-arn>
- Generate signed manifest: gpg --detach-sign --armor manifest-INC-2025-02.txt

Approval and change history

- 2025-02-18: Drafted by Asha Patel (security).
- 2025-03-02: Reviewed by Marco Hernandez (security ops) and Liang Zhou (platform).
- 2026-01-12: Updated retention mapping and cross-region guidance after legal review.

Contact

Security ops oncall: security-oncall@redwood.com
Legal contact: legal-preserve@redwood.com
text:
Overview

This playbook defines Redwood Inference's standard operating procedure for preserving forensic logging artifacts and initiating a legal hold during a security incident that may have legal or regulatory implications. It covers: scope, responsibilities, technical collection steps, integrity verification, short- and long-term storage, RBAC controls during evidence handling, and coordination with Legal and Compliance.

Intended audience

- Incident responders (eng-sre, security ops)
- Platform engineers who own logging/export pipelines
- Legal and Compliance contacts handling preservation requests
- Engineering managers coordinating access and custody

Scope

Applies to all production and staging systems that generate audit logs, access logs, request traces, application-level telemetry, and associated artifacts that may be relevant to an incident. This includes:

- API gateway and request audit logs (redwood-gateway-audit)
- Serving runtime telemetry (runtime-metrics and kernel traces)
- Authentication and authorization logs (auth-service, IAM audit)
- Orchestration and scheduling logs (kube-audit, autoscaler events)
- Network flow captures and firewall logs (vpc-flow-logs)
- Customer-facing telemetry when subject to legal hold (consult Legal)

Definitions

- Forensic artifact: any file or record collected for the purpose of investigation or regulatory review.
- Legal hold: an instruction to preserve specific data from normal retention/deletion, initiated by Legal/Compliance.
- Collector: an authorized engineer or security operator who executes the collection steps.

Ownership and responsibilities

- Incident Commander (IC): declares evidence collection, coordinates with Legal, approves access escalations.
- Security Ops: executes collection playbook, ensures integrity, performs initial triage.
- Platform Storage Owner: prepares secured preservation buckets and ensures encryption/KMS.
- IAM/RBAC Owner: applies temporary access changes and documents approvals (break-glass).
- Legal/Compliance: issues the legal hold, defines custodians, and provides retention directives.

Pre-requisites (before collection)

1. Confirm scope and list of systems from IC + Legal.
2. Identify owners and responsible engineers for each system.
3. Ensure a preservation S3 (or cloud equivalent) bucket exists: redwood-preserve-{incident-id} with encryption enabled and restricted ACLs.
4. Ensure an evidence KMS key is provisioned and access is limited to security and legal service principals.
5. Verify audit logging pipelines are writable (no disk pressure) and snapshot capability exists for long-term storage.

High-level collection timeline (SLOs)

- Initial collection (critical artifacts): target within 4 hours of incident declaration.
- Full snapshot of relevant clusters/services: target within 24 hours.
- Evidence integrity manifest produced and stored: within 48 hours.

Step-by-step collection playbook

1) Triage and scope confirmation
   - IC confirms incident ID and legal hold scope.
   - Security Ops creates the preservation bucket and a ticket in the incident tracker with the tag: evidence-preserve/{incident-id}.

2) Lock down retention and prevent automated deletion
   - For each candidate bucket or index, apply an immediate retention override: set lifecycle to "retain" or disable deletion APIs.
   - Example (AWS CLI):
     aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration --bucket my-audit-bucket --lifecycle-configuration '{"Rules":[{"ID":"preserve-incident-123","Status":"Enabled","AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload":{"DaysAfterInitiation":36500}}]}'

3) Collect primary artifacts (critical first)
   - API gateway audit (last 30 days and live tail):
     aws s3 cp s3://redwood-gateway-audit/2025/02/ --recursive s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/ingest/gateway-audit/ --profile preserve-role
\   - Kubernetes audit logs: generate a cluster snapshot with kube-audit-collector and compress:
     kubectl --context=prod get events --all-namespaces -o json > prod-cluster-events.json
     tar -czf prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz prod-cluster-events.json /var/log/kube-apiserver/audit.log

4) Capture ephemeral state and KV caches
   - Export model-serving KV caches if relevant (coordination with runtime team). Use runtime export tool: runtimectl export-cache --model-id m-123 --since 2025-01-01 --out cache-export.pb

5) Network-level artifacts
   - If packet captures are required, coordinate with network team:
     tcpdump -i eth0 -s 0 -w /tmp/incident-123.pcap 'host 10.20.30.0/24'
     gcloud compute scp compute-instance:/tmp/incident-123.pcap . && aws s3 cp incident-123.pcap s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/network/

6) Database and object storage snapshots
   - Request DB snapshot via platform owner. For Postgres: create a read-replica snapshot and export logical dumps:
     pg_dump --host=db-read.prod.redwood.internal --username=readonly --format=custom --file=prod-incident-123.dump --schema=public

Integrity verification and manifest

- For every collected artifact produce a SHA-256 hash and add a line to a manifest file: manifest-INC-2025-02.txt
  Example manifest line: sha256sum prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz  prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz

- Sign the manifest with the collector's GPG key (if available) or store signed approval in the incident ticket.
  gpg --detach-sign --armor manifest-INC-2025-02.txt

- Example manifest header:
  Incident: INC-2025-02
  Collector: asha.patel@redwood.com
  CollectedAt: 2025-02-18T14:23:00Z

Packaging and encryption

- All artifacts MUST be uploaded to the preservation bucket within a directory named by incident ID and collection phase (e.g. s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/).
- Apply server-side encryption with evidence KMS key and enforce bucket policy to deny DeleteBucket and DeleteObject unless with break-glass approval.

- Example upload command:
  aws s3 cp prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/ --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:123456:key/abcd-ef01-2345

RBAC controls and emergency access (break-glass)

- Normal principle: least privilege. During preservation the following special rules apply:
  - Create a short-lived "evidence-access" role that grants: s3:GetObject, s3:ListBucket for redwood-preserve-{incident-id}, kms:Decrypt on evidence KMS key, and readonly access to index/search artifacts.
  - Access request must include: IC approval, Legal approval, and a Security Manager signature in the incident ticket.
  - All role assumptions are logged and a 24/7 oncall approver is designated.

- Break-glass checklist: time, approver name, justification, artifacts accessed, TTL of credential (max 8 hours), and post-access audit notes.

Legal hold coordination

1. Legal issues hold directive with scope and custodians. Security Ops tags the incident ticket and adds the hold label: legal-hold/INC-2025-02.
2. Custodians are notified with the required actions and stop-delete instructions.
3. Security Ops schedules weekly retention confirmation with Legal until release.

Evidence handoff to Legal or external counsel

- Handoff package should include: artifact tarball(s), manifest (sha256 and signed), collection timeline, collector identities, RBAC/approval log, and storage location.
- Preferred handoff: provide access to the preservation bucket with a time-limited read-only role rather than transferring copies. If copies are transferred, document chain of custody and destroy copies only with Legal approval.

Chain-of-custody form (ASCII table template)

| Item ID | Artifact path | Collected by | Collected at (UTC) | Hash (sha256) | Current Holder | Notes |
|---------|----------------|--------------|---------------------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/phase-1/prod-cluster-audit.tar.gz | asha.patel | 2025-02-18T14:23:00Z | abcd1234... | redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02 | initial collect |

Retention mapping and cross-region residency

- Legal/Compliance will specify retention periods (e.g., 7 years, 3 years, indefinite). Map retention requirements to storage classes and region restrictions:
  - Short-term (<=90 days): standard encrypted buckets in originating region.
  - Long-term (>90 days): move to dedicated preservation account with cross-region replication disabled if data residency prohibits replication.

- If the incident involves cross-border data, Legal will provide custody restrictions. Preservation buckets may be placed in-region only (e.g., eu-west-1) and access limited to EU-based engineers where required.

Secure SDLC tie-ins and post-incident actions

- Root cause findings that require code or infra changes MUST be triaged through our Secure SDLC process: open an RFC, perform a threat model update, and require change review with Security and SRE.
- Add verification suites to CI that assert: audit logging is enabled, log rotation retains at least N days, and alerting for missing streams is in place.

Evidence review and purge release

- Legal issues a release when the hold is lifted. Security Ops performs a release checklist: verify signed release, document final custody, and either remove retention locks or schedule normal lifecycle.

Audit logging configuration checklist (minimum)

- All services generate structured logs (JSON) with: timestamp, request_id, user_id, service, operation, source_ip, result_code.
- Centralized ingest to the audit pipeline with guaranteed delivery (SQS/Kafka backed) and monitoring on ingestion lag (>5m alerts).
- Retention policies defined per environment and override mechanism for legal hold.

Configuration snippet examples

- Minimal structured audit example (JSON line):
  {"ts":"2025-02-18T14:12:03Z","request_id":"r-7a2f","user":"service-account:ingest","svc":"gateway","op":"POST /v1/generate","src_ip":"198.51.100.23","result":200} 

- Simple lifecycle rule example (AWS bucket):
  {
    "Rules": [
      {
        "ID": "preserve-INC-2025-02",
        "Status": "Enabled",
        "NoncurrentVersionExpiration": {"NoncurrentDays": 36500}
      }
    ]
  }

Post-collection audits and logging of access

- All access to preserved artifacts must be logged and reviewed weekly by Security and Legal. Any unauthorized access triggers an immediate re-evaluation of custody and potential re-collection.

Related pages and references

- Incident response playbook (link: confluence://eng-sre/incident-response-playbook)
- Audit logging standards (link: confluence://security-and-compliance/audit-logging/standards)
- RBAC emergency escalation procedure (link: confluence://security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/emergency-escalation)

Appendix: common CLI commands and snippets

- Compute SHA-256: sha256sum artifact.tar.gz
- Upload with KMS: aws s3 cp artifact.tar.gz s3://redwood-preserve-INC-2025-02/ --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id <evidence-key-arn>
- Generate signed manifest: gpg --detach-sign --armor manifest-INC-2025-02.txt

source.title: KMS Rotation Exceptions and Approval Process
source.author: Olga Petrov
source.body:
## Purpose
This page defines the standard process for requesting, reviewing, approving, documenting, and closing **exceptions to Redwood Inference’s KMS/key rotation requirements** for Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

This process exists to:
- Maintain consistent security posture while enabling business continuity when rotation cannot occur on schedule.
- Ensure exceptions are **time-bound**, **risk-assessed**, and have **compensating controls**.
- Produce **audit-ready evidence** (SOC 2 / ISO 27001) showing who approved the exception, why, and how risk was managed.

## Scope
### In scope
Exceptions to KMS/key management rotation requirements, including:
- **Delayed rotation** (missed quarterly/annual cadence).
- **Customer-imposed constraints** (e.g., regulated change windows, customer-controlled KMS policies, required CAB approvals).
- **Platform constraints** (e.g., dependency on a pending fix for dual-read/cutover, known KMS service degradation impacting safety).
- **Operational constraints** (e.g., on-call incident freeze, regional outage, or inability to validate cutover safely).

### Out of scope
- Requests to weaken cryptographic primitives or introduce new algorithms (handled via separate Security Architecture review).
- Broad IAM/auth changes unrelated to key management.
- Permanent exceptions with no end date (not permitted).

## Definitions
- **KMS**: Cloud provider Key Management Service (or HSM-backed equivalent).
- **KEK**: Key Encryption Key (wraps DEKs or encrypts key material).
- **DEK**: Data Encryption Key (encrypts data at rest; typically envelope-encrypted under KEK).
- **Rotation**: Creating a new key version (or new key) and moving encryption to it per the rotation runbook.
- **Dual-read / dual-decrypt window**: Temporary period where the system can decrypt using both the old and new key versions to enable safe cutover and rollback.

## Policy summary (what must normally happen)
1) Key rotation occurs on the scheduled cadence defined in the Key Management Standard.
2) Rotation changes are logged with audit events and change management artifacts.
3) Rotation is executed using approved tooling/runbooks and validated via dashboards/tests.

If (and only if) those conditions cannot be met, an exception may be requested.

## When an exception is allowed
Exceptions are only allowed when ALL of the following are true:
- There is a **clear, documented reason** the standard rotation procedure cannot be performed safely or on time.
- The exception is **time-bound** (explicit expiration date).
- **Compensating controls** reduce risk during the exception window.
- The exception is reviewed and approved by the required approvers (see below).

## Non-negotiables (exceptions will be rejected if…)
The request will be rejected if it proposes any of the following:
- No expiration date or “until further notice”.
- Disabling auditing/logging for KMS access or rotation events.
- Broadening IAM permissions beyond least privilege without an approved compensating control and explicit justification.
- Skipping required validation steps for cutover (where cutover is still planned) without a safer alternative.

## Roles and responsibilities
### Requestor
Typically one of:
- Private Deployments lead/engineer for a customer environment.
- Platform Control Plane / Data Plane engineer owning the key usage path.
- SRE on behalf of an incident response lead.

Requestor is responsible for:
- Submitting complete request information.
- Implementing compensating controls.
- Providing evidence and closing the exception on time.

### Security Engineering (Primary)
- **Owner:** Marcus Lin
- **Delegates:** Hanae Suzuki, Kevin Osei

Responsibilities:
- Validate technical correctness, blast radius, and failure modes.
- Confirm monitoring/alerting coverage and least-privilege posture.

### GRC / Compliance
- **Owner:** Olga Petrov
- **Support:** Daniel Carter

Responsibilities:
- Risk assessment, audit evidence requirements, exception register tracking.
- Ensure approvals and artifacts are complete and retained.

### Final approver (CISO)
- **Dr. Aisha Rahman**

Responsibilities:
- Approves or rejects high-risk exceptions and any exceptions exceeding standard time windows.

### SRE sign-off (as required)
- **Primary:** Sean Gallagher

Required when:
- The exception increases availability risk (e.g., changing retry budgets, circuit breaker behavior, or deferring a cutover due to reliability concerns).

## Required information for an exception request
Submit a ticket in the **Security Exceptions** queue (or the designated internal system) with the following fields. Requests missing these fields will be returned for completion.

### A) Identification
- Environment(s): Hosted / Dedicated / Private (include region(s) and tenant/customer name if applicable).
- System(s) impacted: control plane, data plane, installer, audit log exporter, etc.
- Key identifiers: key ARN/URI, key alias, key ring, or internal key ID (avoid pasting sensitive customer secrets; identifiers are OK).
- Rotation cadence requirement: quarterly / annual / customer-defined.

### B) Exception type
Choose one:
- Delayed rotation (planned)
- Emergency delay (incident / freeze)
- Customer constraint (change control / policy)
- Technical blocker (missing feature / bug)
- Other (must describe)

### C) Business justification
- Customer impact if rotation proceeds on schedule (e.g., outage risk, contractual change window).
- Business impact if exception is denied.

### D) Risk assessment inputs
- Data classification impacted (internal / customer-sensitive / regulated).
- Threat considerations (e.g., compromised key risk, insider access, external attacker with storage access).
- Expected duration of exception.
- Known open issues, incidents, or near-misses related to KMS for this environment.

### E) Compensating controls (required)
Include the set you will apply (see menu below), who will implement, and by when.

### F) Observability & validation
- Dashboards/alerts in place (links).
- Logs/audit events expected (what to look for).
- Validation plan: what checks prove the system is safe during the exception window.

### G) Expiration and closure plan
- Expiration date (hard deadline).
- Planned date of rotation execution.
- Owner for closure.
- Rollback considerations (if cutover will happen after the exception window).

## Compensating controls menu (choose what applies)
At least **two** compensating controls are required for delayed rotation, unless Security Engineering explicitly approves a smaller set due to low risk.

### Recommended controls (common)
1) **Tighten IAM scope**
   - Confirm runtime decrypt role is least privilege.
   - Separate rotation roles/principals from runtime decrypt where supported.
   - Disable unused principals and rotate credentials if applicable.

2) **Enhanced monitoring and alerting**
   - Enable/confirm alerts for KMS error rate, throttling, and latency.
   - Alert on AccessDenied/InvalidCiphertext spikes.
   - Add a temporary lower threshold during the exception window if risk is elevated.

3) **Increase audit evidence capture**
   - Verify audit logs include key version and cutover toggle changes.
   - Export rotation-related events to customer SIEM where applicable.

4) **Change freeze controls**
   - Restrict deploys or configuration changes to the key path unless approved by on-call + Security.

5) **Shorten exposure via staged remediation**
   - If rotation is blocked by a specific change (e.g., missing dual-read), deploy the unblocker first and attach proof (PR/rollout plan).

### Controls for customer-constrained environments (Private)
6) **Customer confirmation artifact**
   - Customer CAB ticket or email approval stating the earliest allowed rotation window.
   - Customer acknowledgment of temporary deviation (if required by their policy).

7) **Installer preflight + periodic validation**
   - Run KMS preflight validation and attach the validation report.
   - Schedule re-validation mid-exception window.

### Controls for elevated risk
8) **Key usage anomaly review**
   - Manual review of KMS usage logs (volume anomalies, unusual principals) at least weekly.

9) **Break-glass readiness**
   - Confirm break-glass access is available, approved, and time-bounded.
   - Confirm on-call runbook links and escalation paths.

## Approval workflow
### Standard exception (<= 30 days)
1) Requestor submits exception ticket with required fields.
2) **Security Engineering** reviews technical details and compensating controls.
3) **GRC** reviews evidence plan, ensures expiration date and tracking.
4) Approval recorded in the ticket, exception added to the exception register.

Required approvers:
- Security Engineering approver (Marcus Lin or delegate)
- GRC approver (Olga Petrov or delegate)

### Extended exception (31–90 days)
Follow Standard exception workflow plus:
- **CISO approval required** (Dr. Aisha Rahman)
- **SRE sign-off required** if reliability posture changes or if exception is due to operational instability

### High-risk exception (any duration)
Examples:
- Environment contains regulated workloads with strict encryption/rotation requirements.
- Prior decrypt mismatches, key material mismatch events, or recent KMS incident history.

Required approvers:
- Security Engineering
- GRC
- CISO
- SRE (if reliability impacted)

## SLA for review and escalation
- Standard requests: initial triage within **2 business days**.
- Emergency requests (incident/freeze): triage within **4 hours** during business hours, or via on-call escalation.

Escalation path:
- Security Engineering: page Security on-call (if applicable) or escalate to Marcus Lin.
- GRC: escalate to Olga Petrov.
- Executive escalation: Dr. Aisha Rahman.

## Evidence requirements (audit-ready)
Attach (or link) the following to the exception ticket:
- Exception request with all required fields (this page’s template).
- Approvals (ticket approvals count if identity is clear and timestamped).
- Compensating controls proof:
  - IAM policy diff or Terraform plan/apply summary (sanitized if needed).
  - Monitoring dashboard links + alert definitions.
  - Validation reports (installer preflight output, healthcheck results).
- Closure evidence:
  - Rotation execution evidence (runbook checklist, timestamps).
  - Post-rotation verification (error rates stable, decrypt success).
  - Final sign-off comment from requestor and Security Engineering.

Retention:
- GRC will retain exception artifacts per the evidence retention policy (minimum aligned to audit cycle). If customer-sensitive, store links in restricted locations and reference them from the ticket.

## Exception lifecycle management
### Tracking
GRC maintains an exception register with:
- Ticket ID
- Environment/customer
- Risk rating
- Expiration date
- Status (open/mitigated/closed)

### Reminders
- 14 days before expiration: automated reminder to requestor + Security Engineering.
- 7 days before expiration: escalation to Security Engineering lead.
- On expiration: escalates to CISO if not closed or renewed.

### Renewals
Renewals are not automatic.
- A renewal request must include an updated risk assessment and updated compensating controls status.
- Renewals beyond 90 days require CISO approval and a documented remediation plan with dates.

## Example scenarios
### Scenario 1: Customer change window delays rotation (Private)
- Customer CAB only allows changes on the first weekend of the next month.
- Compensating controls: enhanced monitoring + customer confirmation artifact + IAM re-validation.
- Approval: Security Engineering + GRC (CISO if >30 days).

### Scenario 2: Platform blocker for safe cutover (Hosted)
- Dual-read bug prevents safe rotation cutover without elevated rollback risk.
- Compensating controls: change freeze on key path + anomaly review + accelerated fix rollout plan.
- Approval: Security Engineering + GRC, plus SRE if reliability risks are elevated.

### Scenario 3: Incident freeze due to KMS instability
- Ongoing KMS timeouts make rotation unsafe.
- Compensating controls: throttle budgets/backpressure enabled, circuit breaker tuned, temporary alert threshold adjustment.
- Approval: Security Engineering + SRE + GRC; CISO if extended.

## Templates
### Exception request template (copy/paste)
**Environment(s):**

**System(s) impacted:**

**Key identifiers (ARN/URI/alias):**

**Rotation requirement and due date:**

**Exception type:**

**Reason rotation cannot occur on schedule:**

**Business justification:**

**Risk considerations (data classification, threat model notes):**

**Requested exception duration + expiration date:**

**Compensating controls (what, who, by when):**

**Monitoring/alerts links:**

**Validation plan:**

**Closure plan (planned rotation date + owner):**

## Contacts
- Security Engineering (KMS/rotation): Marcus Lin
- GRC / Exceptions register: Olga Petrov
- Audit evidence support: Daniel Carter
- SRE readiness / paging: Sean Gallagher
- CISO approval: Dr. Aisha Rahman

## Change log
- 2025-12-03: Updated approval tiers (<=30, 31–90, high-risk), clarified compensating control minimums, and added renewal requirements.
- 2025-11-14: Added customer-constrained Private scenario and required customer confirmation artifact.
- 2025-10-21: Initial publication.

Exceptions to KMS/key management rotation requirements, including:
- **Delayed rotation** (missed quarterly/annual cadence).
- **Customer-imposed constraints** (e.g., regulated change windows, customer-controlled KMS policies, required CAB approvals).
- **Platform constraints** (e.g., dependency on a pending fix for dual-read/cutover, known KMS service degradation impacting safety).
- **Operational constraints** (e.g., on-call incident freeze, regional outage, or inability to validate cutover safely).

### Out of scope
- Requests to weaken cryptographic primitives or introduce new algorithms (handled via separate Security Architecture review).
- Broad IAM/auth changes unrelated to key management.
- Permanent exceptions with no end date (not permitted).

## Definitions
- **KMS**: Cloud provider Key Management Service (or HSM-backed equivalent).
- **KEK**: Key Encryption Key (wraps DEKs or encrypts key material).
- **DEK**: Data Encryption Key (encrypts data at rest; typically envelope-encrypted under KEK).
- **Rotation**: Creating a new key version (or new key) and moving encryption to it per the rotation runbook.
- **Dual-read / dual-decrypt window**: Temporary period where the system can decrypt using both the old and new key versions to enable safe cutover and rollback.

## Policy summary (what must normally happen)
1) Key rotation occurs on the scheduled cadence defined in the Key Management Standard.
2) Rotation changes are logged with audit events and change management artifacts.
3) Rotation is executed using approved tooling/runbooks and validated via dashboards/tests.

If (and only if) those conditions cannot be met, an exception may be requested.

## When an exception is allowed
Exceptions are only allowed when ALL of the following are true:
- There is a **clear, documented reason** the standard rotation procedure cannot be performed safely or on time.
- The exception is **time-bound** (explicit expiration date).
- **Compensating controls** reduce risk during the exception window.
- The exception is reviewed and approved by the required approvers (see below).

## Non-negotiables (exceptions will be rejected if…)
The request will be rejected if it proposes any of the following:
- No expiration date or “until further notice”.
- Disabling auditing/logging for KMS access or rotation events.
- Broadening IAM permissions beyond least privilege without an approved compensating control and explicit justification.
- Skipping required validation steps for cutover (where cutover is still planned) without a safer alternative.

## Roles and responsibilities
### Requestor
Typically one of:
- Private Deployments lead/engineer for a customer environment.
- Platform Control Plane / Data Plane engineer owning the key usage path.
- SRE on behalf of an incident response lead.

Requestor is responsible for:
- Submitting complete request information.
- Implementing compensating controls.
- Providing evidence and closing the exception on time.

### Security Engineering (Primary)
- **Owner:** Marcus Lin
- **Delegates:** Hanae Suzuki, Kevin Osei

Responsibilities:
- Validate technical correctness, blast radius, and failure modes.
- Confirm monitoring/alerting coverage and least-privilege posture.

### GRC / Compliance
- **Owner:** Olga Petrov
- **Support:** Daniel Carter

Responsibilities:
- Risk assessment, audit evidence requirements, exception register tracking.
- Ensure approvals and artifacts are complete and retained.

### Final approver (CISO)
- **Dr. Aisha Rahman**

Responsibilities:
- Approves or rejects high-risk exceptions and any exceptions exceeding standard time windows.

### SRE sign-off (as required)
- **Primary:** Sean Gallagher

Required when:
- The exception increases availability risk (e.g., changing retry budgets, circuit breaker behavior, or deferring a cutover due to reliability concerns).

## Required information for an exception request
Submit a ticket in the **Security Exceptions** queue (or the designated internal system) with the following fields. Requests missing these fields will be returned for completion.

### A) Identification
- Environment(s): Hosted / Dedicated / Private (include region(s) and tenant/customer name if applicable).
- System(s) impacted: control plane, data plane, installer, audit log exporter, etc.
- Key identifiers: key ARN/URI, key alias, key ring, or internal key ID (avoid pasting sensitive customer secrets; identifiers are OK).
- Rotation cadence requirement: quarterly / annual / customer-defined.

### B) Exception type
Choose one:
- Delayed rotation (planned)
- Emergency delay (incident / freeze)
- Customer constraint (change control / policy)
- Technical blocker (missing feature / bug)
- Other (must describe)

### C) Business justification
- Customer impact if rotation proceeds on schedule (e.g., outage risk, contractual change window).
- Business impact if exception is denied.

### D) Risk assessment inputs
- Data classification impacted (internal / customer-sensitive / regulated).
- Threat considerations (e.g., compromised key risk, insider access, external attacker with storage access).
- Expected duration of exception.
- Known open issues, incidents, or near-misses related to KMS for this environment.

### E) Compensating controls (required)
Include the set you will apply (see menu below), who will implement, and by when.

### F) Observability & validation
- Dashboards/alerts in place (links).
- Logs/audit events expected (what to look for).
- Validation plan: what checks prove the system is safe during the exception window.

### G) Expiration and closure plan
- Expiration date (hard deadline).
- Planned date of rotation execution.
- Owner for closure.
- Rollback considerations (if cutover will happen after the exception window).

## Compensating controls menu (choose what applies)
At least **two** compensating controls are required for delayed rotation, unless Security Engineering explicitly approves a smaller set due to low risk.

### Recommended controls (common)
1) **Tighten IAM scope**
   - Confirm runtime decrypt role is least privilege.
   - Separate rotation roles/principals from runtime decrypt where supported.
   - Disable unused principals and rotate credentials if applicable.

2) **Enhanced monitoring and alerting**
   - Enable/confirm alerts for KMS error rate, throttling, and latency.
   - Alert on AccessDenied/InvalidCiphertext spikes.
   - Add a temporary lower threshold during the exception window if risk is elevated.

3) **Increase audit evidence capture**
   - Verify audit logs include key version and cutover toggle changes.
   - Export rotation-related events to customer SIEM where applicable.

4) **Change freeze controls**
   - Restrict deploys or configuration changes to the key path unless approved by on-call + Security.

5) **Shorten exposure via staged remediation**
   - If rotation is blocked by a specific change (e.g., missing dual-read), deploy the unblocker first and attach proof (PR/rollout plan).

### Controls for customer-constrained environments (Private)
6) **Customer confirmation artifact**
   - Customer CAB ticket or email approval stating the earliest allowed rotation window.
   - Customer acknowledgment of temporary deviation (if required by their policy).

7) **Installer preflight + periodic validation**
   - Run KMS preflight validation and attach the validation report.
   - Schedule re-validation mid-exception window.

### Controls for elevated risk
8) **Key usage anomaly review**
   - Manual review of KMS usage logs (volume anomalies, unusual principals) at least weekly.

9) **Break-glass readiness**
   - Confirm break-glass access is available, approved, and time-bounded.
   - Confirm on-call runbook links and escalation paths.

## Approval workflow
### Standard exception (<= 30 days)
1) Requestor submits exception ticket with required fields.
2) **Security Engineering** reviews technical details and compensating controls.
3) **GRC** reviews evidence plan, ensures expiration date and tracking.
4) Approval recorded in the ticket, exception added to the exception register.

Required approvers:
- Security Engineering approver (Marcus Lin or delegate)
- GRC approver (Olga Petrov or delegate)

### Extended exception (31–90 days)
Follow Standard exception workflow plus:
- **CISO approval required** (Dr. Aisha Rahman)
- **SRE sign-off required** if reliability posture changes or if exception is due to operational instability

### High-risk exception (any duration)
Examples:
- Environment contains regulated workloads with strict encryption/rotation requirements.
- Prior decrypt mismatches, key material mismatch events, or recent KMS incident history.

Required approvers:
- Security Engineering
- GRC
- CISO
- SRE (if reliability impacted)

## SLA for review and escalation
- Standard requests: initial triage within **2 business days**.
- Emergency requests (incident/freeze): triage within **4 hours** during business hours, or via on-call escalation.

Escalation path:
- Security Engineering: page Security on-call (if applicable) or escalate to Marcus Lin.
- GRC: escalate to Olga Petrov.
- Executive escalation: Dr. Aisha Rahman.

## Evidence requirements (audit-ready)
Attach (or link) the following to the exception ticket:
- Exception request with all required fields (this page’s template).
- Approvals (ticket approvals count if identity is clear and timestamped).
- Compensating controls proof:
  - IAM policy diff or Terraform plan/apply summary (sanitized if needed).
  - Monitoring dashboard links + alert definitions.
  - Validation reports (installer preflight output, healthcheck results).
- Closure evidence:
  - Rotation execution evidence (runbook checklist, timestamps).
  - Post-rotation verification (error rates stable, decrypt success).
  - Final sign-off comment from requestor and Security Engineering.

Retention:
- GRC will retain exception artifacts per the evidence retention policy (minimum aligned to audit cycle). If customer-sensitive, store links in restricted locations and reference them from the ticket.

## Exception lifecycle management
### Tracking
GRC maintains an exception register with:
- Ticket ID
- Environment/customer
- Risk rating
- Expiration date
- Status (open/mitigated/closed)

### Reminders
- 14 days before expiration: automated reminder to requestor + Security Engineering.
- 7 days before expiration: escalation to Security Engineering lead.
- On expiration: escalates to CISO if not closed or renewed.

### Renewals
Renewals are not automatic.
- A renewal request must include an updated risk assessment and updated compensating controls status.
- Renewals beyond 90 days require CISO approval and a documented remediation plan with dates.

## Example scenarios
### Scenario 1: Customer change window delays rotation (Private)
- Customer CAB only allows changes on the first weekend of the next month.
- Compensating controls: enhanced monitoring + customer confirmation artifact + IAM re-validation.
- Approval: Security Engineering + GRC (CISO if >30 days).

### Scenario 2: Platform blocker for safe cutover (Hosted)
- Dual-read bug prevents safe rotation cutover without elevated rollback risk.
- Compensating controls: change freeze on key path + anomaly review + accelerated fix rollout plan.
- Approval: Security Engineering + GRC, plus SRE if reliability risks are elevated.

### Scenario 3: Incident freeze due to KMS instability
- Ongoing KMS timeouts make rotation unsafe.
- Compensating controls: throttle budgets/backpressure enabled, circuit breaker tuned, temporary alert threshold adjustment.
- Approval: Security Engineering + SRE + GRC; CISO if extended.

## Templates
### Exception request template (copy/paste)
**Environment(s):**

**System(s) impacted:**

**Key identifiers (ARN/URI/alias):**

**Rotation requirement and due date:**

**Exception type:**

**Reason rotation cannot occur on schedule:**

**Business justification:**

**Risk considerations (data classification, threat model notes):**

**Requested exception duration + expiration date:**

**Compensating controls (what, who, by when):**

**Monitoring/alerts links:**

**Validation plan:**

**Closure plan (planned rotation date + owner):**

## Contacts
- Security Engineering (KMS/rotation): Marcus Lin
- GRC / Exceptions register: Olga Petrov
- Audit evidence support: Daniel Carter
- SRE readiness / paging: Sean Gallagher
- CISO approval: Dr. Aisha Rahman

## Change log
- 2025-12-03: Updated approval tiers (<=30, 31–90, high-risk), clarified compensating control minimums, and added renewal requirements.
- 2025-11-14: Added customer-constrained Private scenario and required customer confirmation artifact.
- 2025-10-21: Initial publication.

source.title: Key Management Standard: KMS Integrations
source.author: Hanae Suzuki
source.content:
## Purpose
This standard defines minimum security and reliability requirements for integrating Redwood Inference services with customer-managed or Redwood-managed Key Management Systems (KMS) used for envelope encryption (KEK/DEK patterns), including optional HSM-backed keys.

This document is intended to:
- Prevent insecure or brittle KMS usage (overbroad IAM, missing audit evidence, retry storms, unsafe caching).
- Make KMS-related failures diagnosable and operable (consistent logs/metrics/traces, runbooks, game-day readiness).
- Ensure KMS integrations pass enterprise security reviews for Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

## Scope
### In scope
- Any code path that calls a cloud KMS/HSM or on-prem KMS interface for: encrypt, decrypt, generate data key, re-encrypt, describe key, list key versions, or grant/permission operations.
- Control-plane and data-plane components.
- Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

### Out of scope
- Introducing new cryptographic primitives/algorithms (use existing approved primitives and libraries).
- Platform-wide authentication/authorization redesign.

## Definitions
- **KEK**: Key Encryption Key (often a KMS-managed key). Used to wrap/unwrap DEKs.
- **DEK**: Data Encryption Key (used for encrypting actual payloads; typically generated and wrapped by KMS).
- **Envelope encryption**: Data encrypted with DEK; DEK encrypted (wrapped) by KEK.
- **Rotation**: Periodic replacement of KEKs and/or DEKs. See ADR-008 for the approved strategy.
- **Call site**: A distinct service/component code path that invokes KMS APIs.

## Policy summary (MUST/SHOULD)
The following are mandatory unless an exception is approved (see “Exceptions”).

### Security controls
- MUST use envelope encryption for application data at rest when using KMS (DEKs for data; KEKs for wrapping).
- MUST scope IAM to least privilege for the specific key(s), operations, and environment.
- MUST separate runtime decrypt/encrypt permissions from rotation/admin permissions (separate roles/principals) for Dedicated/Private unless explicitly infeasible.
- MUST emit audit-relevant logs for key/rotation configuration changes (cutovers, key ID changes, policy toggles).
- MUST not log plaintext, DEKs, or wrapped key material.
- SHOULD use KMS/HSM-backed keys for regulated workloads (customer requirement-dependent).

### Reliability controls
- MUST use the standardized KMS client wrapper (ADR-007) for timeouts, retries, error taxonomy, and telemetry.
- MUST implement bounded retries with jitter and global retry budgets to avoid retry amplification.
- MUST include circuit breaker / degrade-mode behavior for KMS dependency failures where service availability requires it.
- MUST document expected behavior when KMS is degraded/unavailable (fail closed vs fail open) and obtain Security + SRE sign-off.

### Observability controls
- MUST emit standard metrics and trace spans for every KMS operation.
- MUST map provider-specific errors to the canonical Redwood KMS error taxonomy.
- SHOULD provide dashboards and alerts (or explicitly document why the integration is low-risk/no alerting needed).

## Approved integration patterns
### 1) Envelope encryption (recommended default)
1. Use KMS `GenerateDataKey` (or equivalent) to obtain plaintext DEK + encrypted/wrapped DEK.
2. Encrypt data locally with plaintext DEK.
3. Store ciphertext + wrapped DEK + key metadata (key ID/ARN, key version if provided, encryption context identifiers).
4. Discard plaintext DEK from memory as soon as possible.
5. On read, decrypt wrapped DEK via KMS and decrypt data locally.

### 2) Direct KMS encryption (restricted)
Direct KMS encrypt/decrypt for application payloads is discouraged due to size limits, latency, and cost. It MAY be used only for very small blobs (e.g., short secrets) with explicit Security approval and documented SLO impact.

### 3) Customer-managed keys (CMK / external key)
When customers provide keys (BYOK), integrations MUST:
- Support validating key accessibility at install/preflight time (Private) and at configuration time (control plane).
- Require explicit customer ownership of key lifecycle and access control, documented in customer-facing materials.

## IAM and permission scoping requirements
### Baseline principles
- Principle of least privilege: restrict **actions**, **resources**, **conditions**, and **principals**.
- Prefer explicit key ARNs/IDs over wildcards.
- Prefer conditions that bind requests to known contexts (where supported): encryption context, source VPC endpoints, principal tags, or workload identity.
- Separate roles:
  - **Runtime role**: encrypt/decrypt/generate data keys required by the service.
  - **Rotation role**: permissions to change key version references, update config, manage grants, and perform cutover steps.
  - **Break-glass role**: time-bound, audited access used only during incidents with approvals.

### Minimum permissions (illustrative)
Actual permissions differ by provider; implement via the Infra baseline modules where available.

**Runtime role MUST be limited to:**
- Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey/ReEncrypt (only if required)
- DescribeKey (if required for health validation; otherwise avoid)
- No key policy changes

**Rotation/admin role MAY include:**
- Grant management operations (where applicable)
- Ability to update relevant config in Redwood control plane
- Access to audit/event logs for evidence collection

### Provider-specific notes
- **AWS KMS**:
  - Use key policies to restrict admin operations; avoid granting broad `kms:*`.
  - Use grants where appropriate to reduce key policy sprawl.
  - Prefer condition keys like `kms:EncryptionContext:*` and `kms:ViaService` when feasible.
- **GCP Cloud KMS**:
  - Scope permissions at key ring / crypto key level; restrict `cloudkms.cryptoKeyVersions.useToDecrypt`/`useToEncrypt`.
- **Azure Key Vault / Managed HSM**:
  - Use RBAC where possible; scope to key object and operations; be explicit about `get`, `wrapKey`, `unwrapKey` equivalents.

### Credential management
- MUST avoid long-lived static credentials in code or baked images.
- MUST use workload identity (cloud-native) or customer-approved credential injection (Private) with clear rotation paths.

## Required logging, metrics, and tracing
### Logging (application logs)
MUST include structured fields on KMS calls and failures:
- `kms.provider` (aws|gcp|azure|other)
- `kms.operation` (encrypt|decrypt|generate_data_key|reencrypt|describe_key|...)
- `kms.key_id` (redacted/normalized identifier; do not log full customer key ARN if customer policy requires redaction)
- `kms.region` (if applicable)
- `kms.error_category` (canonical taxonomy)
- `kms.http_status` or provider status (if available)
- `kms.request_id` (provider request id when available)
- `deployment_mode` (hosted|dedicated|private)
- `env` and `service`

MUST NOT log:
- Plaintext data
- Plaintext DEKs
- Wrapped DEKs (unless explicitly approved; default is “do not log”)
- Customer secrets, tokens, or credential material

### Canonical KMS error taxonomy
All KMS provider errors MUST map to the following categories (used for alerting and runbooks):
- `AccessDenied`
- `NotFound` (key or version not found)
- `Throttling`
- `Timeout`
- `InvalidCiphertext` (includes key mismatch, wrong context, corrupted material)
- `KeyDisabled` / `KeyInvalidState`
- `DependencyUnavailable` (provider outage / transient 5xx)
- `Unknown`

### Metrics (MUST)
Every service making KMS calls MUST emit the following metrics (names are illustrative; use the platform standard naming):
- `kms_client_request_total{operation,provider,env,region,result,error_category}`
- `kms_client_latency_ms_bucket{operation,provider,env,region}` (histogram)
- `kms_client_throttled_total{operation,provider,env,region}`
- `kms_client_retry_attempts_total{operation,provider,env,region}`
- `kms_client_circuit_breaker_state{service,env}` (gauge) where circuit breaker is implemented

### Tracing (MUST)
- All KMS calls MUST create a trace span with:
  - operation name, provider, error category, and retry attempt count.
- Spans MUST not include sensitive payloads.

### Audit logs (configuration and rotation)
Changes that affect cryptographic behavior MUST be recorded in audit logs:
- Key ID / key version reference updates
- Dual-read window enable/disable
- Cutover toggles
- Policy overrides (retry budget overrides, fail-open toggles, etc.)
- Break-glass access activation (where integrated)

## Timeouts, retries, circuit breakers, and caching
### Standardized client wrapper
All KMS integrations MUST use the Redwood standard KMS client wrapper (ADR-007). Direct provider SDK calls in application code require an exception.

### Timeouts
- MUST enforce explicit per-call timeouts.
- MUST set a maximum end-to-end budget per request path (KMS calls included) and ensure KMS retries do not exceed that budget.

### Retries
- MUST use exponential backoff with jitter.
- MUST cap maximum attempts and total retry time.
- MUST distinguish retryable vs non-retryable error categories:
  - Retryable: `Timeout`, `DependencyUnavailable`, some `Throttling` (with backoff)
  - Non-retryable: `AccessDenied`, `NotFound`, `InvalidCiphertext`, `KeyDisabled/InvalidState`

### Circuit breaker / degrade mode
Where KMS failure can cause cascading outages:
- MUST implement circuit breaker behavior and document the degrade policy.
- MUST define what happens when the circuit is open (examples):
  - Fail closed with clear errors and backpressure (default for cryptographic strictness)
  - Limited fail open only if data confidentiality/integrity is not reduced (rare; requires Security approval)
- MUST have SRE-reviewed alerting for circuit open events.

### Caching
- MAY cache KMS key metadata for short durations if it does not create rotation hazards.
- MUST NOT cache plaintext DEKs.
- MUST ensure caching does not cause stale key version usage that breaks rotation. Any caching around key identifiers or key version metadata MUST include:
  - bounded TTLs
  - invalidation on cutover
  - explicit rollback behavior

## Rotation readiness requirements
All new KMS call sites MUST be compatible with the approved rotation approach (ADR-008):
- Support key versioning and cutover without requiring downtime.
- Support dual-read/dual-decrypt during the transition window when applicable.
- Emit signals to detect mismatch (`InvalidCiphertext` spikes, decrypt failures, version skew).

Minimum rotation readiness checklist:
- Key identifiers stored with ciphertext/wrapped material in a way that supports selecting the correct key.
- Ability to read/decrypt items encrypted under both old and new key versions during the cutover window (where applicable).
- Documented rollback plan that does not require data rewrite under incident pressure.

## Incident handling and operational expectations
### Required artifacts
- A runbook or runbook section describing:
  - Symptoms, dashboards, and log queries
  - Common root causes (AccessDenied, throttling, timeout spikes, wrong key ID)
  - Mitigation steps and safe stopgaps
  - Escalation path to Security + Platform
- Link the runbook to on-call rotations and ensure it is discoverable.

### Paging and severity guidance
- `AccessDenied` or `InvalidCiphertext` spikes are treated as correctness/security-impacting until proven otherwise.
- Sustained `Timeout`/`DependencyUnavailable` or elevated `Throttling` causing request failures should page SRE based on service SLO impact.

### Break-glass access
- Break-glass use MUST be:
  - time-bound (auto-expire)
  - approved per the access process
  - logged and included in the incident record

## Required reviews for new KMS call sites
### When this standard applies
If your change introduces any new KMS interaction (new service, new operation, new key, new provider, or new runtime path), you MUST complete this review.

### Reviewers (minimum)
- Security Engineering reviewer (required)
- Infra/Platform IAM reviewer (required)
- SRE reviewer (required if the call site is in a latency-sensitive or high-QPS path)

### Evidence to attach to the PR / design review
- Link to the call site inventory entry (service + operation + key ID class)
- IAM policy diff (or Terraform module usage) demonstrating least privilege
- Observability screenshots/links (dashboards, metric names) or validation logs from staging
- Failure-mode test plan or test results (timeouts, throttling, AccessDenied, key disabled)
- Rotation readiness statement (how dual-read and rollback are handled)

## Implementation requirements (engineering checklist)
Use this checklist for each new KMS call site.

### A) Design and data model
- [ ] Uses envelope encryption (or has approved exception).
- [ ] Stores sufficient metadata to select the correct key/version for decryption.
- [ ] Defines encryption context usage (if supported) and ensures it is stable across reads.

### B) Permissions
- [ ] Runtime role has only required KMS operations.
- [ ] Resource scoping is explicit (key(s), environment).
- [ ] Rotation/admin permissions are separated from runtime permissions.
- [ ] No wildcard admin permissions without exception.

### C) Reliability behavior
- [ ] Uses the standard KMS wrapper.
- [ ] Explicit timeout configured.
- [ ] Retries are bounded, jittered, and category-aware.
- [ ] Circuit breaker behavior implemented (if required) and documented.
- [ ] Backpressure/throttle budgets considered for high-QPS paths.

### D) Observability
- [ ] Standard metrics emitted with required tags.
- [ ] Trace spans added.
- [ ] Errors mapped to canonical taxonomy.
- [ ] Logs are structured and do not include sensitive material.
- [ ] Alerts/dashboards exist or a rationale is documented.

### E) Testing
- [ ] Integration tests cover: throttling, timeouts, AccessDenied, key disabled/invalid state.
- [ ] Tests assert retry budgets and latency caps (no retry storms).
- [ ] Rotation test (where applicable): decrypt under old+new key versions during dual-read window.

### F) Documentation
- [ ] Runbook updated (or new runbook added) with troubleshooting steps.
- [ ] Private/Dedicated operator docs updated if the integration is customer-configurable.

## Exceptions
If you cannot meet any MUST requirement, you MUST request an exception using the KMS rotation exceptions process:
- Provide: scope, duration, risk assessment, compensating controls, and rollback plan.
- Approvals: Security Engineering lead + GRC/Compliance as required.
- Exceptions are time-bound and must include a plan to remediate.

## References
- ADR-007: KMS client wrapper and failure policy
- ADR-008: Key rotation strategy (dual-read and cutover)
- KMS decrypt failures runbook
- IAM baseline and rotation roles standard
- Private deployment KMS operator guide

## Owner and change management
- Owner: Security Engineering
- Changes to this standard require Security + SRE review and are announced in #eng-security and #eng-sre.

---
Appendix: Quick guidance for common pitfalls
- **AccessDenied in Private deployments**: almost always mis-scoped principal/key policy/region mismatch. Ensure preflight validation checks are enabled and run.
- **Throttle spikes**: confirm retry jitter is enabled and that request concurrency has backpressure. Do not “solve” throttling by increasing retries.
- **InvalidCiphertext during rotation**: treat as potential key/version mismatch; verify dual-read settings and that ciphertext metadata includes the correct key reference.

title: Key Management Standard: KMS Integrations
preview:
## Purpose
This standard defines minimum security and reliability requirements for integrating Redwood Inference services with customer-managed or Redwood-managed Key Management Systems (KMS) used for envelope encryption (KEK/DEK patterns), including optional HSM-backed keys.

This document is intended to:
- Prevent insecure or brittle KMS usage (overbroad IAM, missing audit evidence, retry storms, unsafe caching).
- Make KMS-related failures diagnosable and operable (consistent logs/metrics/traces, runbooks, game-day readiness).
- Ensure KMS integrations pass enterprise security reviews for Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

## Scope
### In scope
- Any code path that calls a cloud KMS/HSM or on-prem KMS interface for: encrypt, decrypt, generate data key, re-encrypt, describe key, list key versions, or grant/permission operations.
- Control-plane and data-plane components.
- Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

### Out of scope
- Introducing new cryptographic primitives/algorithms (use existing approved primitives and libraries).
- Platform-wide authentication/authorization redesign.

## Definitions
- **KEK**: Key Encryption Key (often a KMS-managed key). Used to wrap/unwrap DEKs.
- **DEK**: Data Encryption Key (used for encrypting actual payloads; typically generated and wrapped by KMS).
- **Envelope encryption**: Data encrypted with DEK; DEK encrypted (wrapped) by KEK.
- **Rotation**: Periodic replacement of KEKs and/or DEKs. See ADR-008 for the approved strategy.
- **Call site**: A distinct service/component code path that invokes KMS APIs.

## Policy summary (MUST/SHOULD)
The following are mandatory unless an exception is approved (see “Exceptions”).

### Security controls
- MUST use envelope encryption for application data at rest when using KMS (DEKs for data; KEKs for wrapping).
- MUST scope IAM to least privilege for the specific key(s), operations, and environment.
- MUST separate runtime decrypt/encrypt permissions from rotation/admin permissions (separate roles/principals) for Dedicated/Private unless explicitly infeasible.
- MUST emit audit-relevant logs for key/rotation configuration changes (cutovers, key ID changes, policy toggles).
- MUST not log plaintext, DEKs, or wrapped key material.
- SHOULD use KMS/HSM-backed keys for regulated workloads (customer requirement-dependent).

### Reliability controls
- MUST use the standardized KMS client wrapper (ADR-007) for timeouts, retries, error taxonomy, and telemetry.
- MUST implement bounded retries with jitter and global retry budgets to avoid retry amplification.
- MUST include circuit breaker / degrade-mode behavior for KMS dependency failures where service availability requires it.
- MUST document expected behavior when KMS is degraded/unavailable (fail closed vs fail open) and obtain Security + SRE sign-off.

### Observability controls
- MUST emit standard metrics and trace spans for every KMS operation.
- MUST map provider-specific errors to the canonical Redwood KMS error taxonomy.
- SHOULD provide dashboards and alerts (or explicitly document why the integration is low-risk/no alerting needed).

## Approved integration patterns
### 1) Envelope encryption (recommended default)
1. Use KMS `GenerateDataKey` (or equivalent) to obtain plaintext DEK + encrypted/wrapped DEK.
2. Encrypt data locally with plaintext DEK.
3. Store ciphertext + wrapped DEK + key metadata (key ID/ARN, key version if provided, encryption context identifiers).
4. Discard plaintext DEK from memory as soon as possible.
5. On read, decrypt wrapped DEK via KMS and decrypt data locally.

### 2) Direct KMS encryption (restricted)
Direct KMS encrypt/decrypt for application payloads is discouraged due to size limits, latency, and cost. It MAY be used only for very small blobs (e.g., short secrets) with explicit Security approval and documented SLO impact.

### 3) Customer-managed keys (CMK / external key)
When customers provide keys (BYOK), integrations MUST:
- Support validating key accessibility at install/preflight time (Private) and at configuration time (control plane).
- Require explicit customer ownership of key lifecycle and access control, documented in customer-facing materials.

## IAM and permission scoping requirements
### Baseline principles
- Principle of least privilege: restrict **actions**, **resources**, **conditions**, and **principals**.
- Prefer explicit key ARNs/IDs over wildcards.
- Prefer conditions that bind requests to known contexts (where supported): encryption context, source VPC endpoints, principal tags, or workload identity.
- Separate roles:
  - **Runtime role**: encrypt/decrypt/generate data keys required by the service.
  - **Rotation role**: permissions to change key version references, update config, manage grants, and perform cutover steps.
  - **Break-glass role**: time-bound, audited access used only during incidents with approvals.

### Minimum permissions (illustrative)
Actual permissions differ by provider; implement via the Infra baseline modules where available.

**Runtime role MUST be limited to:**
- Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey/ReEncrypt (only if required)
- DescribeKey (if required for health validation; otherwise avoid)
- No key policy changes

**Rotation/admin role MAY include:**
- Grant management operations (where applicable)
- Ability to update relevant config in Redwood control plane
- Access to audit/event logs for evidence collection

### Provider-specific notes
- **AWS KMS**:
  - Use key policies to restrict admin operations; avoid granting broad `kms:*`.
  - Use grants where appropriate to reduce key policy sprawl.
  - Prefer condition keys like `kms:EncryptionContext:*` and `kms:ViaService` when feasible.
- **GCP Cloud KMS**:
  - Scope permissions at key ring / crypto key level; restrict `cloudkms.cryptoKeyVersions.useToDecrypt`/`useToEncrypt`.
- **Azure Key Vault / Managed HSM**:
  - Use RBAC where possible; scope to key object and operations; be explicit about `get`, `wrapKey`, `unwrapKey` equivalents.

### Credential management
- MUST avoid long-lived static credentials in code or baked images.
- MUST use workload identity (cloud-native) or customer-approved credential injection (Private) with clear rotation paths.

## Required logging, metrics, and tracing
### Logging (application logs)
MUST include structured fields on KMS calls and failures:
- `kms.provider` (aws|gcp|azure|other)
- `kms.operation` (encrypt|decrypt|generate_data_key|reencrypt|describe_key|...)
- `kms.key_id` (redacted/normalized identifier; do not log full customer key ARN if customer policy requires redaction)
- `kms.region` (if applicable)
- `kms.error_category` (canonical taxonomy)
- `kms.http_status` or provider status (if available)
- `kms.request_id` (provider request id when available)
- `deployment_mode` (hosted|dedicated|private)
- `env` and `service`

MUST NOT log:
- Plaintext data
- Plaintext DEKs
- Wrapped DEKs (unless explicitly approved; default is “do not log”)
- Customer secrets, tokens, or credential material

### Canonical KMS error taxonomy
All KMS provider errors MUST map to the following categories (used for alerting and runbooks):
- `AccessDenied`
- `NotFound` (key or version not found)
- `Throttling`
- `Timeout`
- `InvalidCiphertext` (includes key mismatch, wrong context, corrupted material)
- `KeyDisabled` / `KeyInvalidState`
- `DependencyUnavailable` (provider outage / transient 5xx)
- `Unknown`

### Metrics (MUST)
Every service making KMS calls MUST emit the following metrics (names are illustrative; use the platform standard naming):
- `kms_client_request_total{operation,provider,env,region,result,error_category}`
- `kms_client_latency_ms_bucket{operation,provider,env,region}` (histogram)
- `kms_client_throttled_total{operation,provider,env,region}`
- `kms_client_retry_attempts_total{operation,provider,env,region}`
- `kms_client_circuit_breaker_state{service,env}` (gauge) where circuit breaker is implemented

### Tracing (MUST)
- All KMS calls MUST create a trace span with:
  - operation name, provider, error category, and retry attempt count.
- Spans MUST not include sensitive payloads.

### Audit logs (configuration and rotation)
Changes that affect cryptographic behavior MUST be recorded in audit logs:
- Key ID / key version reference updates
- Dual-read window enable/disable
- Cutover toggles
- Policy overrides (retry budget overrides, fail-open toggles, etc.)
- Break-glass access activation (where integrated)

## Timeouts, retries, circuit breakers, and caching

## Rotation readiness requirements
All new KMS call sites MUST be compatible with the approved rotation approach (ADR-008):

Minimum rotation readiness checklist:

- Link the runbook to on-call rotations and ensure it is discoverable.

- `AccessDenied` or `InvalidCiphertext` spikes are treated as correctness/security-impacting until proven otherwise.
- Sustained `Timeout`/`DependencyUnavailable` or elevated `Throttling` causing request failures should page SRE based on service SLO impact.

### Break-glass access
- Break-glass use MUST be:
  - time-bound (auto-expire)
